reference: – Less than a month after its big launch, Google+ has hit its first minus: Usage declined 3% to 1.79 million visits last week, reports Bloomberg. What's more, time spent on the new social media site dropped 10%, falling to 5 minutes to 15 seconds. It's important to note Experian Hitwise's numbers include neither international nor mobile web traffic, and Google+ is technically still in "limited beta," writes MG Siegler of Techcrunch. But when your prime competition has 750 million users, any setback can be daunting. Since making its debut June 28, Google+ quickly rose to 20 million users in just three weeks. "There was a sense of excitement: Could this really be the next big social network?" writes Siegler. But "after initially checking it several times a day, I now load Google+ about twice a day, mainly to see if I’m missing anything. I rarely find that I am. I +1 a few things here and there, maybe leave a comment. But overall, the content feels fairly stale. The new car smell is wearing off. And it’s time for reality." And that reality? "User retention is a bitch," says Siegler.

final_summary:
Roger Watson was drafted on October 6, 1966, and met his lifelong friend Dave Brown during basic Army training at Fort Hood, Texas. The two men, both from northwest Detroit, spent a month at home before serving in Vietnam, where they were part of the 9th Infantry and served as combat engineer demolition specialists. However, after their initial deployment, they lost touch and never saw or heard from each other again.

Dave Brown had heard that Roger was wounded early in his tour but never knew if he had survived. Meanwhile, Roger returned home, got married, had children, and settled in Berkley, often wondering about his missing friend. In 2011, Roger befriended his neighbor, Peggy Brown, who shared his interest in birdwatching. Unbeknownst to Roger, Peggy was married to Dave Brown.

Peggy discovered Roger's connection to the military when she spotted the Purple Heart logo on his license plate and a 9th Infantry Vietnam Veterans cap in his car. This sparked a series of emails between Peggy and Roger, which eventually led to the revelation that they had been neighbors for 18 years without realizing it. As they exchanged messages, they pieced together their past, including their shared experience in the 9th Infantry and their time in Vietnam.

In 1968, Dave Brown had searched for Roger's name on the Vietnam Veterans Memorial in Washington, D.C., but was unable to find it. Later, he learned that Roger had indeed been wounded on March 8, 1968, suffering significant injuries, including the loss of a kidney and 3 feet of small intestine, as well as damage to his liver. Despite this, Roger survived and went on to lead a normal life, marrying, having children, and working as a supervisor for the Michigan Department of Corrections.

The reunion between Roger and Dave took place in October 2015, when they met at the chain-link fence separating their backyards in Berkley. The encounter was emotional, with both men expressing shock and amazement at their chance discovery. Since then, they have been spending quality time together, reminiscing about their past and making new memories as friends and neighbors.

reference: – A Detroit man who long pondered the fate of his old Army buddy found him in the oddest of places: living practically next door for the last 18 years. Dave Brown heard that his basic training buddy Roger Watson had been wounded in Vietnam, which was true—Watson lost a kidney and suffered other organ damage after being shot early in his tour in 1968, the Detroit Free Press reports. Brown even checked the Vietnam Veterans Memorial to see whether his fellow combat engineer demolition specialist, who trained with him at Ft. Hood in Texas, had died. Then Brown's wife, Peggy, befriended a kitty-corner neighbor over the back fence, chatting with him about their mutual interest in birds. She was also intrigued by the Purple Heart license plate on his car, and 9th Infantry Vietnam Veterans cap in his car's rear window. So she asked about them in an email. Soon, the exclamation points were flying: "Small world!!!!!!" and "Wow!!!!" are among the words in their email exchange as they figured out who was who. A backyard meeting was quickly arranged, and Brown "came running out of the house, over to the corner, had to beat back the brush, we just shook hands and couldn’t believe it," Watson tells WXYZ. "It was just an incredible feeling, just amazing." Reclaiming their friendship after nearly half a century, they plan to "share a beer every now and then" and see "a lot more of each other," says Watson. "Grow very old and watch our grandchildren grow up," adds Dave. "It's just great to see him alive. It makes me happy." (Read about a guy apparently outed as "fake" Army veteran.)

final_summary:
Florida Senator Marco Rubio has hinted at potentially running for re-election, citing the recent Orlando mass shooting as a factor in his consideration. However, according to Rubio's long-time friend and fellow Floridian, Lieutenant Governor Carlos Lopez-Cantera, Rubio's decision remains uncertain. The two friends engaged in a candid conversation about their respective futures following the tragic event, with Lopez-Cantera urging Rubio to reconsider his decision not to run.

Lopez-Cantera, who has been running for Rubio's Senate seat in a crowded GOP primary for over a year, expressed his commitment to continuing his campaign despite Rubio's initial reluctance to run. Rubio had previously encouraged Lopez-Cantera to pursue the seat, but Lopez-Cantera's conversation with Rubio removed one of the main obstacles to Rubio's potential candidacy - the risk of damaging their friendship.

The conversation took place in Rubio's pickup truck, about an hour before sunset on Sunday, as they witnessed the aftermath of the deadliest mass shooting in US history. Lopez-Cantera emphasized that his decision to discuss the matter was driven by a sense of national responsibility and the significance of the upcoming election, rather than personal ambition.

Despite Lopez-Cantera's reassurances that nothing has changed, and he remains committed to his own campaign, many speculate that Rubio may ultimately decide to run. This would likely impact the dynamics of the Republican primary, with some candidates already positioning themselves as alternatives to Rubio. 

Polling suggests that Rubio is the strongest Republican contender in a potential matchup against Democratic Representative Patrick Murphy, which could determine control of the Senate. However, Rubio's decision is complicated by his desire to spend more time with his family and pursue lucrative opportunities outside of politics.

Some Republicans and conservative groups have questioned the sincerity of Lopez-Cantera's intentions, suggesting that the conversation may have been orchestrated to influence Rubio's decision. Meanwhile, Rubio's allies argue that he should run for re-election to maintain his platform and influence on policy issues, particularly foreign policy, which has become a specialty during his tenure in the Senate.

Rubio's response to a question about his decision on a conservative radio show sparked speculation that he may be reconsidering his stance. While he initially expressed hesitation to link the Orlando shooting to his decision, he acknowledged being "deeply impacted" by the tragedy and open to re-evaluating his role in public service.

Ultimately, only Rubio knows what he will decide, and his announcement is expected to have significant implications for the Republican primary and the future of the Senate.

reference: – The main obstacle keeping Marco Rubio from seeking re-election to his Senate seat just disappeared. The prospect of Rubio doing so gained traction in the wake of the Orlando shooting, but he continued to insist that he wouldn't run against his friend already in the race, Carlos Lopez-Cantera. Now, however, Lopez-Cantera himself is publicly giving Rubio the all-clear, reports Politico. He says he and Rubio discussed it in recent days, with Lopez-Cantera encouraging him to consider changing his mind. “I don’t want you to feel like you have to say that because of outside pressure,” he quotes Rubio as responding. The incumbent told reporters on Wednesday that he'd go home this week to spend time with his family and consider his next steps. Rumors are swirling that the pair have some sort of plan—perhaps for Rubio to win the seat over a Democrat (Republicans believe he has the best shot), then resign and allow Lopez-Cantera to run before the 2020 presidential campaign. But Lopez-Cantera says that's not the case. "Until I mentioned this, he was really committed to spending time with his kids, being able to be home for more than 10 days without having to go somewhere," he says. As of now, "nothing has changed,” he adds. "I'm still running. Marco isn't." Rubio's decision isn’t expected until the weekend at the earliest, per the Wall Street Journal; he has until June 24 to file for re-election. If he does so, Lopez-Cantera won't run, says Politico.

final_summary:
Twitter recently announced plans to overhaul its process for verifying accounts, resulting in the removal of the blue checkmark from several prominent far-right users. Among those affected was Tim "Baked Alaska" Gionet, a former BuzzFeed staffer turned alt-right figure, who was permanently banned from the platform. In response, Gionet livestreamed his breakdown at an In-N-Out Burger while yelling at bystanders.

Laura Loomer, a former employee of Project Veritas and known for disrupting a New York City performance of "Julius Caesar," was particularly upset by the loss of her verification status. When a verified Twitter user suggested that losing one's verification was not comparable to the Holocaust, Loomer doubled down, drawing parallels between the situation and the genocide of six million Jews.

Notably, other far-right figures, including neo-Nazis Richard Spencer and Jason Kessler, who organized the Charlottesville rally, retained their verification despite being stripped of it. This led some to question whether Twitter's actions were merely a superficial attempt to address harassment and abuse without effectively removing problematic users from the platform.

Loomer has a history of controversy, having been banned from Uber and Lyft after posting anti-Muslim tweets following the New York terror attack. Her comments included calls for a ride-sharing service that did not employ Muslim drivers, sparking widespread criticism and accusations of stoking racism. Loomer has since threatened to take legal action against both companies for banning her. Celebrities, including Gigi Hadid and Alyssa Milano, condemned Loomer's behavior, labeling her a "f***ing moron" and reporting her to Twitter for hate speech. Despite the backlash, Loomer continued to tweet about the issue, further solidifying her reputation as a vocal member of the alt-right.

reference: – A few Twitter users lost the coveted blue tick on Wednesday after Twitter released new guidelines regarding verified accounts. The move came after the company was attacked for granting verified status to Jason Kessler, who put together the white nationalist rally that rocked Charlottesville in August (and issued this "vile" tweet about victim Heather Heyer). In a series of tweets, Twitter explained that "verification was meant to authenticate identity & voice ... [but] verification has long been perceived as an endorsement. We gave verified accounts visual prominence on the service which deepened this perception. We should have addressed this earlier but did not prioritize the work as we should have." General verification requests had been put on ice as of Nov. 9 pending a review of the program, and the Guardian reports that Twitter's new policy specifies that accounts that promote hate, support hate groups, or harass users will see verification revoked. Among those to immediately lose the check, per the Washington Post, were Kessler, white nationalist Richard Spencer, and far-right activist Laura Loomer (known for rushing the stage during Julius Caesar and being banned from Uber and Lyft after anti-Muslim tweets). Loomer's response to her notice of verification removal including tweeting "Translation: I'm a conservative" and, per Splinter, comparing her situation to that of Jews during the Holocaust.

final_summary:
Alice Walker, the celebrated author of The Color Purple, has been criticized for recommending a book by David Icke, a British author and public speaker known for spreading conspiracy theories, including anti-Semitic ones. In an interview with The New York Times Book Review, Walker listed "And the Truth Shall Set You Free" by Icke as one of the books on her nightstand, sparking outrage among critics who pointed out that the book is an "unhinged anti-Semitic conspiracy tract." 

Icke's book contains numerous anti-Semitic tropes, including claims that a secret Jewish cabal controls the world, that the Talmud is "among the most appallingly racist documents on the planet," and that Jews are behind anti-Semitic attacks. The book also quotes and seconds the assertion that Jews bankrolled their own extermination in the Holocaust. Icke's views are not new, as he has been promoting similar ideas for decades, including in his previous book, "Human Race Get Off Your Knees."

Walker has a history of praising Icke's work and expressing anti-Semitic views. In 2013, she wrote a glowing review of "Human Race Get Off Your Knees," calling it an "amazing book" that allowed her to understand the "root of the incredible evil that has engulfed our planet." She has also shared interviews featuring Icke and promoted his lectures on social media. Additionally, Walker has posted an anti-Semitic poem on her blog, "It Is Our (Frightful) Duty to Study the Talmud," which blames the Talmud for various global problems and accuses Jews of viewing non-Jews as "sub-human."

Despite her history of promoting anti-Semitic views, Walker has not been held accountable by elite cultural critics. Her defenders argue that her criticism of Israel is being unfairly conflated with anti-Semitism, but experts note that anti-Zionism and anti-Semitism are not interchangeable terms. The New York Times has also been criticized for publishing Walker's recommendation without qualification, allowing her anti-Semitic views to reach a wider audience.

Walker's views on Israel have been used to deflect criticism of her anti-Semitism, but experts argue that this is a flawed argument. While Walker is a vocal critic of Israel, her views on the Jewish state are not necessarily anti-Semitic, but her promotion of anti-Semitic ideologies is a separate issue. The lack of accountability for Walker's views highlights a broader problem in the cultural establishment, where anti-Semitism is often tolerated or ignored when it comes from certain quarters.

Critics argue that the New York Times and other cultural institutions have a responsibility to challenge and condemn anti-Semitism, regardless of the identity or politics of the person promoting it. By failing to do so, they enable the spread of anti-Semitic ideas and create a culture of impunity for those who promote them.

reference: – The New York Times is standing by its Q&A with Pulitzer-winning author Alice Walker despite complaints about her comments on a book by an accused anti-Semite. The 74-year-old author of The Color Purple told the newspaper's By the Book column that was she was currently reading David Icke's And the Truth Shall Set You Free and called it "a curious person's dream come true." At Tablet, Yair Rosenberg writes that the book referencing The Protocols of the Elders of Zion, a text accusing Jewish people of a plot for global domination, "is an unhinged anti-Semitic conspiracy tract written by one of Britain's most notorious anti-Semites." The Washington Post highlights this quote: "I strongly believe that a small Jewish clique which has contempt for the mass of Jewish people worked with non-Jews to create the First World War, the Russian Revolution, and the Second World War." A Times rep says the column is a "portrait of a public person through the lens of books; it is not a list of recommendations from our editors," who might "dislike, disdain or even abhor" the books mentioned, per the Guardian. But Amy Russo at HuffPo notes the paper failed to provide context in an editor's note, "thereby allowing [Icke's] name to be promoted unchecked." Rosenberg argues the column only ensures "racism is disseminated to more people." Walker also said this of the book: "In Icke’s books there is the whole of existence, on this planet and several others, to think about." Her comments are "especially corrosive because the anti-Jewish conspiracies she uplifted and adopted are part of the same white supremacist power structure she so deftly fought through her written work in the past," adds Jewish filmmaker Rebecca Pierce. (Walker once blocked an Israeli publisher from printing her book.)

final_summary:
At the Alipore zoo in Kolkata, India, a unique problem has arisen in the captive breeding program for white tigers. Ten-year-old Vishal, the only male in the breeding age group, has shown little interest in mating with eight-year-old tigress Rupa, despite repeated attempts by zoo officials to encourage him. According to Dayanarayan Banerjee, a former senior veterinary officer at the zoo, Vishal's reluctance to mate is likely due to his shyness resulting from being bred in captivity, rather than a lack of virility.

Zoo officials had high hopes for Vishal and Rupa, selecting them for their youth and vigor after receiving permission to resume captive breeding last year. However, since then, Vishal has consistently avoided Rupa, running away with his tail between his legs whenever she initiates contact. Despite administering vitamins A and E and a libido enhancer to boost his sexual appetite, Vishal remains uninterested in mating.

Veterinary experts attribute Vishal's behavior to his lack of interaction with the opposite sex due to being raised in captivity, causing him to be shy and unsure of how to respond to biological signals. This is a significant concern for the zoo, as the breeding of tigers is crucial for maintaining a healthy population and ensuring the continuation of the species.

The last time a tiger cub was born in the zoo was over a decade ago, and officials are eager to revive the breeding program. However, the age and temperament of the current tigers pose challenges, as they may become aggressive during mating, posing a risk to themselves and others. Despite these obstacles, zoo authorities are not giving up on Vishal and are exploring alternative options, such as introducing him to a younger tigress named Rani, who is described as "young and full of vigor."

reference: – Male tigers are a lot like human men when it comes to sex. They're shy and awkward about their bodies if they haven't spent a lot of time around female tigers. They take libido enhancers when they get older. And, if a partner their own age isn't doing it for them, they consider trading them in for a younger, prettier tiger. BBC News reports India's Alipore zoo is having a difficult time getting its 10-year-old white tiger Vishal to mate. The zoo has tried vitamins and even de-worming him; nothing seems to be working. Experts are blaming the fact that Vishal was born in captivity, meaning he doesn't have much experience with females and might not understand the biological signals his body is sending, according to the Times of India. Plus, Vishal is at the outer limits of breeding age for tigers. This is apparently frustrating to Rupa, a female white tiger the zoo has assigned to breed with Vishal. While in heat, Rupa will chase, touch, and scratch Vishal, but he "runs away with his tail between his legs," a veterinary consultant tells the Times. Zoo staff have been trying to mate the two tigers for six months, and it's important they succeed because the zoo's tiger population is getting older and hasn't had a birth in more than a decade. Now, staff are considering trying Vishal out with a much younger Bengal tiger named Rani, who is described as having "a very beautiful face," the BBC reports. Ouch, Rupa.

final_summary:
A new pill, known as nalmefene, has become available in the UK to help individuals reduce their alcohol consumption. The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) has issued formal guidance recommending the use of nalmefene, a medication that can be prescribed to those who drink excessively, defined as consuming at least half a bottle of wine or three pints a night. This recommendation follows draft guidance released in October, allowing patients to request the drug if they meet specific criteria.

Nalmefene, also marketed under the brand name Selincro, is intended for individuals who regularly consume high amounts of alcohol, exceeding the World Health Organization's recommended daily limits of 7.5 units for men and 5 units for women. The pill works by reducing the urge to drink and is designed to be used in conjunction with counseling support. It is not suitable for individuals with severe alcohol problems who require immediate cessation.

According to NICE, the medication can be taken up to once a day and costs approximately £3. The pill is meant to be used strategically, helping patients manage their cravings and gradually decrease their alcohol intake. Lyndsey Dudley, a spokesperson for NICE, likened the effect of nalmefene to wearing a nicotine patch to aid in quitting smoking, acknowledging that some days may be easier than others.

Nalmefene is expected to benefit around 35,000 individuals in the first year, although this number may be lower due to potential reluctance among patients to opt for medication. In Scotland, where nalmefene was approved for use in October 2013, only 53 prescriptions were issued in the subsequent year, despite predictions suggesting a higher uptake.

The medication is aimed at individuals who do not necessarily recognize themselves as having an alcohol problem, such as middle-class drinkers who consume more than three glasses of wine a night. According to Professor Carole Longson, director of NICE's Health Technology Evaluation Centre, many people have a complicated relationship with alcohol, maintaining stable lifestyles yet regularly exceeding recommended daily limits, which can lead to serious health consequences.

Professor Jonathan Chick, a medical advisor for Drinkaware, emphasized that nalmefene is not intended for individuals who simply drink moderately, but rather for those who have attempted to cut down on their drinking and continue to exceed low-risk levels. Patients prescribed nalmefene typically consume higher amounts than the recommended limits, often exhibiting changes in brain patterns that make it challenging to control their drinking habits.

Nalmefene is available starting today and can be purchased in packs of 14 tablets for £42.42, equivalent to approximately £3 per day. The medication is designed to be used alongside counseling support to help individuals reduce their alcohol intake by up to 1.8 units per day.

reference: – Those Brits who want to cut down on their alcohol intake—and can spare about $5 a day—now have access to a pill to reduce their craving. UK officials have approved the drug nalmefene for use along with counseling to help reduce alcohol dependency, the International Business Times reports. The official approval means people who fit the right profile can request the prescription drug. "Those who could be prescribed nalmefene have already taken the first big steps by visiting their doctor, engaging with support services, and taking part in therapy programs," says a researcher. Nalmefene, which can be taken once a day, helps people feel less of an urge to drink, experts say. It's considered appropriate for many people drinking the equivalent of at least half a bottle of wine daily, the Guardian reports. Plenty of potential candidates to take the drug "probably don’t even recognize themselves as an alcoholic," says a health official, who compares it to a nicotine patch. But since nalmefene is intended to be used to gradually reduce alcohol intake, it's not a good solution for people who need to quit right away, the Guardian notes.

final_summary:
Texas Senator Ted Cruz vehemently opposed President Barack Obama's nominee for Attorney General, Loretta Lynch, citing concerns over her stance on immigration and executive overreach. During her confirmation hearing, Lynch's responses failed to alleviate Cruz's concerns, leading him to argue that she would continue the "lawlessness" exhibited by outgoing Attorney General Eric Holder. Cruz delivered a 10-minute speech on the Senate floor, emphasizing his opposition to Lynch's nomination and accusing her of embracing Holder's disregard for the Constitution and laws.

Cruz participated in a procedural vote earlier in the day, voting against cloture, which ultimately passed 66-34, allowing the confirmation process to move forward. However, he missed the final confirmation vote, becoming the only senator to do so. His office initially cited a fundraiser in Dallas as the reason for his absence, but later clarified that his opposition to Lynch's nomination had been clear through various public statements and actions.

Cruz's decision to skip the final vote drew attention to his attendance record, which has been scrutinized in recent weeks. According to reports, he has missed a significant number of hearings and votes, including a crucial human trafficking bill that passed unanimously without his presence. Despite his strong opposition to Lynch, Cruz acknowledged that the outcome of the vote was predetermined, stating that the Republican majority had chosen to confirm her.

Lynch's confirmation ultimately passed with a 56-43 vote, with Senator John Cornyn being the only other Republican to vote against her. Cruz's criticism of Lynch centered around her defense of Obama's executive actions on immigration and her failure to distance herself from Holder's views, which he deemed unacceptable for the position of Attorney General.

reference: – Today's vote in the Senate to make Loretta Lynch the next attorney general was 56-43. So who was the lone missing senator out of 100? Ted Cruz, which is generating some head scratches considering that he spoke for 10 minutes on the floor of the Senate prior to the vote pleading with his colleagues to reject her. It turns out that Cruz had to catch a flight back to Texas for a fundraiser before the final vote, reports the Dallas Morning News. His spokesperson is downplaying the move, noting that Cruz voted against the procedure for ending a filibuster that cleared the way for a final vote. "He voted for cloture, that was the vote that mattered," says Rick Tyler. "He made the case against her, he voted against her in cloture, and he didn’t prevail." Still, the unusual combination of the presidential candidate blasting Lynch and then being the only senator to skip the vote is making headlines at Politico, BuzzFeed, the Washington Post, and elsewhere. As for Cruz's objection to Lynch, he says she "embraced the lawlessness" that marked the tenure of Eric Holder.

final_summary:
On Sunday evening, a 6-year-old boy with autism went missing in Mount Vernon, Westchester County, after slipping away from his grandmother while they were near a park on South 7th Avenue. The boy, who was accompanied by his mother, had been playing near the park when he managed to get onto a blue, battery-powered toy ATV, which he proceeded to drive towards the nearby Bronx River Parkway. Witnesses described the scene as chaotic, with children running back and forth, and the boy's frantic mother asking if anyone had seen him.

As the boy approached the parkway, he entered the far-right lane, where cars were traveling at speeds of at least 45mph. Several witnesses, including Ana Rubio and her husband Joel, who were among the first to spot the boy, sprang into action, slowing down their vehicles to block him from other traffic. Rubio recalled that the boy seemed determined to continue driving, attempting to merge into the middle lane of the highway.

Rubio and the other good Samaritans managed to safely corral the boy and his ATV, preventing potential harm from passing vehicles. When the boy saw a police officer approaching, he initially attempted to run back onto the highway, but Rubio intervened, hugging him and reassuring him that the officer would take him home. The boy was eventually taken to Montefiore Mount Vernon Hospital for observation and was found to be okay.

According to Westchester County police spokesperson Kieran O'Leary, the boy's family had reported him missing to Mount Vernon police, prompting a flurry of 911 calls about a child operating a toy ATV on the parkway. The incident remains under investigation, but thanks to the quick thinking and bravery of the good Samaritans, the boy was safely reunited with his family.

reference: – Drivers on the Bronx River Parkway were a little surprised to see a 6-year-old driving his miniature ATV beside them last night. The boy's mom was even more surprised to discover him missing. It started on a family trip to a park in Mount Vernon, New York, where the autistic boy rode off unnoticed at around 7pm, the Journal News reports. "They realized pretty quickly he was gone but couldn't lay eyes on him," a police spokesman said. Bystanders say the mother was frantically calling for her boy as kids ran back and forth looking for him, 7Online reports. Soon he had zoomed onto the northbound parkway and was even trying to merge into the middle lane, police and witnesses say. So drivers boxed him in and grabbed him just as police arrived. "He was like, 'I need to go home, mom, mom,' all he kept saying was 'mom,'" said Ana Rubio, one of the drivers. Another was said to be shaking with goosebumps, telling people, "I almost hit a kid." As for the boy, he seemingly "didn't know he was on a highway, he just wanted to go home," said Rubio. He was taken to a hospital first and "found to be OK," reports 7Online, but police are still investigating. (Read about an autistic boy allegedly kept in a cage.)

final_summary:
Sally Hootnick, a resident of Manlius, New York, reported her iPad missing after she left it on a seat on an American Airlines flight that landed at John F. Kennedy International Airport (JFK) in New York City on April 1. She initially contacted the airline's lost and found department, leaving messages on their answering machine, but received no response. 

Hootnick then utilized the tracking feature of her MobileMe account, which allowed her to locate her iPad through its GPS coordinates. To her surprise, the device was not at the airport, but rather at a residence on Long Island, New York. By accessing the location on Google Earth, Hootnick was able to view the exact house with a curving driveway.

Despite informing American Airlines and the Port Authority of New York & New Jersey about the iPad's whereabouts, no action was taken for several days. Frustrated, Hootnick decided to take matters into her own hands. She sent a message to the iPad, stating that she knew its location and had already informed the authorities. However, instead of returning the device to the lost and found, the iPad began to move, traveling to various locations including the JFK parking lot, Los Angeles International Airport, and San Francisco International Airport before returning to JFK.

Convinced that the iPad was being used by an American Airlines employee, Hootnick used another application to command the device to emit a loud "beep" every two minutes and send messages to the thief, reiterating that she knew their identity and had notified the police. Over the course of a month, Hootnick exchanged emails with the airline, informing them of her suspicions and threatening to contact a consumer protection agency if no action was taken.

After a month-long delay, American Airlines finally responded, with a representative from their corporate security department reaching out to Hootnick for further information. Two additional conversations were held between Hootnick and a police detective assigned to investigate the case by the airline. Despite these developments, the current status of the iPad remains unknown, as Hootnick replaced her device and disabled the tracking service, rendering it unable to be located.

According to Hootnick, the iPad is likely unusable to the individual who possesses it, as the serial number will flag the device as stolen if anyone attempts to activate new wireless services.

reference: – There's an app for pretty much everything—except cutting through bureaucracy. A woman who mistakenly left her iPad on a plane was able to track the whereabouts of the device, but couldn't convince authorities to follow up on it, reports the Syracuse Post-Standard, via the Consumerist. The woman was fairly certain she left the iPad on a plane after deboarding in New York City, and after leaving messages with American Airlines, she used the GPS function of MobileMe to find that the iPad was in a house on Long Island. She contacted airline and law-enforcement authorities with the exact location, but no action was taken. She suspects an airline employee pocketed the iPad because it then made several trips back and forth between JFK airport and California. She can't trace it anymore because she replaced the iPad and shut off wireless service to the old one. At one point, she used an app to make it beep every two minutes. “I just wanted to harass who ever took it,” she said. “I didn’t want him to enjoy it if he was using it.”

final_summary:
President Barack Obama expressed concern over the failure to act on available information that could have potentially prevented the attempted Christmas Day bombing of a Northwest Airlines flight. According to White House national security adviser James Jones, the president is "legitimately and correctly alarmed" that patterns of behavior and available information regarding the suspect, Umar Farouk Abdulmutallab, were not addressed. Abdulmutallab, a 23-year-old Nigerian man, was indicted on charges of attempting to murder nearly 300 people and using a weapon of mass destruction.

Abdulmutallab's father had warned US embassy officials in Nigeria about his son's potential involvement with extremist ideologies, but his visa was not revoked, and he was not added to the "no-fly" list. The suspect claimed to have received training and equipment from a group affiliated with al-Qaeda in Yemen, where he met radical cleric Anwar al-Awlaki. Al-Awlaki, a US citizen of Yemeni descent, has been linked to several terrorist plots, including the Fort Hood shooting in Texas, which resulted in the deaths of 13 people.

The White House plans to release an unclassified report detailing the missed opportunities to prevent the attempted bombing. Jones stated that the solutions to preventing future incidents involve "tweaks" rather than a complete overhaul of the system, citing the need to hire additional personnel to alleviate the workload of intelligence analysts. Senate Intelligence Committee Chairwoman Dianne Feinstein suggested expanding the criteria for inclusion on the "no-fly" list to individuals with "reasonable suspicion."

In related news, Yemeni Deputy Prime Minister Rashad al-Alimi confirmed that Abdulmutallab joined al-Qaeda in London, contradicting earlier claims that he was recruited in Yemen. Al-Awlaki was reportedly met by Abdulmutallab in his ancestral home province of Shabwa, Yemen. The US has been pressuring Yemen to take action against al-Qaeda, which has been accused of soft-peddling the terrorist organization.

final_summary:
In a groundbreaking move, National Louis University, a private institution based in Chicago, has partnered with Groupon to offer a unique deal on a graduate-level introduction to teaching course. This unprecedented partnership marks the first time an academic university has utilized the popular online marketplace to promote student interest in higher education. The exclusive offer allows prospective students to enroll in the three-credit course for nearly 60% off the regular tuition price of $2,232, bringing the total down to $950.

The course, designed specifically for individuals with little to no teaching experience, is open to anyone holding an undergraduate degree and aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the teaching profession. The three-hour evening classes will take place at the university's Chicago campus, located at 122 S. Michigan Ave, from late September to December. Although the course is part of the university's graduate program, it is only worth three of the required 36 credits needed to graduate.

According to Jocelyn Zivin, Vice President of Marketing and Communications at National Louis University, the initiative seeks to address the growing concerns surrounding teacher shortages and declining interest in teaching careers. By offering a discounted rate, the university hopes to attract potential educators who may be deterred by the high costs associated with pursuing a teaching degree. "We'd like (prospective students) to understand what the realities are, whether you are committed to this profession... and see if you have what it takes," Zivin explained.

Groupon's Communications Director, Julie Mossler, views this collaboration as an innovative step forward, regardless of whether the deal reaches its cap of 25 participants. "This is... an opportunity for our subscribers to take the first step toward what could be a new career," Mossler stated. The Groupon deal will go live on Tuesday and will expire either when it reaches the maximum number of participants or on Wednesday, whichever comes first.

The course is specifically designed for Groupon, and assumes students have no prior teaching experience. It’s open to anyone with an undergrad degree, but if students want to take more courses they’ll have to go through the normal admissions process. “There are all kinds of factors in the K-12 world that are really discouraging,” the school’s VP of marketing says. “We'd like (potential students) to understand what the realities are, whether you are committed to this profession ... and see if you have what it takes.” The Groupon deal goes live today: It offers the $2,232 course for $950 and will run until tomorrow, or until 25 people sign up, according to Mashable. Once students complete the class, they'll have three of the 36 credits they need for a degree.

final_summary:
On Sunday morning, two explosions rocked two churches in Las Cruces, New Mexico, within a span of approximately 20 minutes, causing no injuries but significant concern among the communities. The first explosion occurred at Calvary Baptist Church around 8:20 a.m., when a device detonated in a mailbox located near the administrative entrance, where about 30 people were gathered before services began. Church member Dennis Llewellyn, a former Marine special forces member, identified the device as a bomb, citing the presence of an Eveready battery among the debris.

Twenty minutes later, a second explosion occurred at Holy Cross Catholic Church, where a device detonated in a trash can outside the main entrance, shattering the glass front of the building and startling worshippers in the midst of their morning Mass. According to Monsignor John Anderson, the pastor, the timing of the explosion coincided with the consecration portion of the Mass, where everyone was kneeling and facing the altar, potentially averting a more severe outcome. Had the bomb gone off just 10 minutes later, it is likely that several people would have been injured.

The explosions were described as "dangerous, much more dangerous than just fireworks or firecrackers" by Las Cruces Police Department spokesman Dan Trujillo, who emphasized that the devices were intended to cause harm. However, investigators have yet to determine the exact nature of the explosives or the motivations behind the attacks. New Mexico State Police, the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, the Federal Bureau of Investigation, and the Department of Homeland Security are leading the investigation, assisted by local law enforcement agencies.

New Mexico Governor Susana Martinez condemned the attacks, stating that only a "coward" would target places of worship, and expressing confidence that the perpetrators would be brought to justice. State Police Chief Pete Kassetas acknowledged that the explosions undoubtedly caused terror, but stopped short of labeling them as an act of terrorism, citing the need for further evidence.

In response to the incidents, Holy Cross Catholic Church has announced plans to increase security measures, including adding patrols to the parking lot and installing cameras at the main entrance. The church's pastor, Monsignor John Anderson, expressed his gratitude that no one was harmed and vowed that the community would not be intimidated by the attacks. Meanwhile, faith leaders from across the region rallied together in support, calling for forgiveness and unity in the face of adversity.

reference: – Police are investigating a pair of blasts that rocked two New Mexico churches where parishioners had gathered yesterday morning, though there were no injuries and damage was "relatively minor," authorities tell NBC News. One explosion came from a mailbox outside an administrative entrance to Calvary Baptist Church in Las Cruces at about 8:20am local time, reports the Washington Post. About 30 people had gathered inside and witnesses say they felt the building shake. Twenty minutes later, the pastor at Holy Cross Catholic Church, three miles away, said he heard a "pow" while passing out communion. "I didn't know if it was a shotgun blast…but it was very loud," he tells the Las Cruces News. The bomb exploded in a trash can outside the church's glass front doors, which shattered. "I'm just thankful to God nobody was standing by the door, because there's usually always somebody standing there," the pastor says. "Ten minutes later we would have been leaving and standing around that space." A churchgoer at Calvary Baptist—which is on the same street as several other churches that weren't targeted—says he recognized bomb parts at the scene, including an Eveready battery. A police rep says only that the two devices "were not fireworks" and "were intended, I believe, to do harm and could have done harm to people." Police add the blasts are considered related and authorities have warned other nearby churches "to be on the lookout for anything suspicious." Both churches remained closed yesterday afternoon, while classes at Holy Cross' adjoining school were canceled today.

final_summary:
A massive global email virus, dubbed "Here You Have," swept across the globe on Thursday, crippling email services at numerous high-profile organizations, including NASA, Comcast, Disney, and the Florida Department of Transportation. The virus, which was first spotted in August but gained momentum on Thursday, was characterized by emails with the subject line "Here You Have" or "Just For You," often accompanied by a link claiming to contain a PDF document. However, upon closer inspection, the link was revealed to be a malicious piece of malware designed to spread virally once downloaded.

According to McAfee Labs, the virus was not a traditional worm, as it required human interaction to spread, specifically when a user clicked on the malicious link. Once activated, the malware could infect connected computers and USB drives, making it a significant concern for corporate networks. The virus was described as a Trojan, capable of stopping and deleting security services, and was identified as having multiple variants, making it challenging to track and eradicate.

SecureWorks linked the virus to a cyber-jihad organization called Brigades of Tariq ibn Ziyad, citing a 2008 posting from a known Libyan hacker who aimed to unite like-minded hackers in a cyber-jihad against US agencies, particularly those belonging to the US Army. The hacker's goal, as stated in the posting, was to penetrate these agencies.

McAfee Vice President Dmitri Alperovitch confirmed that the company was investigating the attack, describing it as an email-based worm that propagated through links allegedly leading to PDF documents. The company's report noted that the risk level for both home and corporate email users was low, but warned that the malware could spread virally once installed on a machine.

Symantec compared the threat to the "Anna Kournikova" virus from 2001, which also used the subject line "Here You Have" and tricked users into opening an email message supposedly containing a picture of the tennis player. The company speculated that the threat originated from a botnet and appeared to be targeting numerous companies indiscriminately.

As the virus spread, Department of Homeland Security officials launched an investigation, with the US-CERT and DHS National Cyber Security Division conducting forensic analysis to determine the origin of the worm. Several federal departments and agencies, including NASA, experienced the virus, while the Florida Department of Transportation reported that six other agencies in the state were also affected.

Adobe Systems had previously alerted computer security experts to vulnerabilities in the Adobe Reader software, which could potentially be exploited by hackers. The use of the PDF format in the malicious emails may have been a deliberate attempt to take advantage of these vulnerabilities.

The virus caused disruptions to email services, forcing some organizations to shut down their email systems temporarily. Employees took to social media to express frustration and amusement at the situation, with NASA's Lunar Science Institute tweeting, "Houston, we have a problem... it's called spam."

reference: – An email spam virus swept through corporate America's inboxes yesterday, causing servers to be shut down at major operations including Comcast, AIG, Disney, and NASA. The virus spread through emails with the subject line "Here you have," which contained a link that downloads a virus that sends the same email to everybody in a user's address book. The Department of Homeland Security is investigating, reports ABC News. Security experts say anybody receiving the "here you have" email should delete it and contact their IT departments. They stress that people should never click links in emails from unknown people, or even in suspect emails from people they know. "The fact that it managed to spread widely through various multinational businesses doesn't say a lot for the security savvy of the workers," notes Neil Rubenking at PC World.

final_summary:
A new study warns that rising global temperatures due to climate change will significantly impact the aviation industry, making it harder for planes to take off in the coming decades. According to the study, published in the journal Climatic Change, during the hottest parts of the day, 10 to 30 percent of fully loaded planes may have to remove some fuel, cargo, or passengers, or wait for cooler hours to fly. The study, conducted by researchers Ethan Coffel and Radley Horton from Columbia University, analyzed data from 19 major airports worldwide, including those in the US, Europe, the Middle East, China, and South Asia.

The researchers found that as air warms, it expands and becomes less dense, reducing the lift generated by an airplane's wings, making it difficult for planes to take off safely. This issue is particularly pronounced in areas with short runways, high temperatures, and high elevations. The study estimates that if global warming continues unabated, fuel capacities and payload weights will have to be reduced by as much as 4 percent on the hottest days for some aircraft, resulting in significant costs for the airline industry.

Airports with short runways, such as Washington's Reagan National Airport and New York's LaGuardia Airport, will be severely impacted, with planes needing to offload weight half the time during the hottest days. Dubai International Airport, with its extremely high temperatures, will also face significant challenges. On the other hand, airports with longer runways and milder climates, such as New York's JFK, London Heathrow, and Paris's Charles de Gaulle, will be less affected.

The study's findings highlight the urgent need for the aviation industry to adapt to the changing climate. Researchers suggest that airlines may need to adjust their schedules to depart earlier in the day when it's cooler, design planes with lighter materials, or develop new wing designs that generate additional lift. However, these solutions will come at a cost, and expanding runways in densely populated cities is unlikely.

The study's projections are based on a 1 to 3 degree Celsius warming over the next half-century, which is expected to result in more frequent heatwaves and rising temperatures. By 2080, annual maximum daily temperatures at airports could rise by 7 to 14 degrees Fahrenheit, leading to costly delays and cancellations. The study's lead author, Ethan Coffel, notes that the weight restrictions imposed by rising temperatures will impose a "non-trivial cost" on the aviation industry, potentially disrupting other sectors of the economy.

Previous research has also highlighted the impact of climate change on aviation, including increased turbulence on transatlantic flights. Atmospheric scientist Paul D. Williams from the University of Reading emphasizes the need for the industry to address climate change more aggressively, developing engines that produce fewer greenhouse gases and adapting planes to the future climate.

reference: – Remember in June when it got too hot for planes to take off in Phoenix? A new study suggests stories like that will become quite common in the not-too-distant future. Via Reuters, the study published in Climatic Change estimates that rising temperatures could leave up to one-third of planes grounded during heat waves in the coming decades. The problem centers around the wings’ capacity for lift, which is lessened in warmer conditions. By 2100, worldwide average temperatures are expected to rise by 5.4 degrees Fahrenheit. To safely take off, airlines would need to make adjustments during the hottest part of the day, with 10% to 30% of planes affected. Full flights will either stay grounded until conditions cool down or lighten their loads by removing fuel, cargo, or passengers, per a release at EurekAlert. Think 12 to 13 people asked to leave a 160-person passenger plane. "Our results suggest that weight restriction may impose a non-trivial cost," one of the study’s lead authors says. Wired reports that among the airports studied, New York’s LaGuardia and Washington’s Reagan-National fared poorly because of short runways, as did Dubai International Airport, where extreme heat could force reduced loads up to 55% of the time during peak temperatures. Better news: New York's JFK wouldn't be much affected. Delayed departures won't be the only hassle. Scientists say severe turbulence would become more common. “I’ve yet to see a benefit of climate change to aviation,” says atmospheric scientist Paul Williams, who believes it’s high time for the airline industry to prepare and adapt. (An essay at New York provided a decidedly bleak outlook on climate change.)

final_summary:
Lance Armstrong, the former professional cyclist, is set to give his first television interview since being stripped of his seven Tour de France titles in a 202-page report by the United States Anti-Doping Agency (USADA), which described his involvement in a sophisticated doping program as "the most professionalized and successful doping program that sport has ever seen." The interview, a 90-minute "no-holds-barred" conversation with Oprah Winfrey, is scheduled to air on January 17 on the Oprah Winfrey Network. 

According to USADA Chief Executive Travis Tygart, Armstrong offered to donate over $150,000 to the organization in 2004, which was rejected due to a potential conflict of interest. This revelation comes amidst ongoing controversy surrounding Armstrong's involvement in doping, with many questioning why he would make such an offer given the accusations against him. 

Armstrong has consistently denied allegations of doping throughout his career, claiming he passed over 500 drug tests without failing. However, the USADA report cited testimony from former teammates stating they cheated or avoided tests altogether. Armstrong's lawyer, Tim Herman, declined to comment on reports that Armstrong is considering publicly admitting to using performance-enhancing drugs, which could potentially lead to his reinstatement under World Anti-Doping Agency rules.

The interview with Oprah Winfrey marks a significant moment in the ongoing saga surrounding Armstrong's career, particularly given the network's ties to Discovery Communications, which sponsored Armstrong's team in 2005. Critics have questioned whether Winfrey will hold Armstrong accountable for his actions, pointing to her previous interview with Marion Jones, a track and field athlete who claimed to have unintentionally used performance-enhancing drugs.

Armstrong's reputation as a cancer survivor and philanthropist through his Livestrong Foundation has been tarnished by the doping allegations, which have led to widespread criticism and calls for accountability. His decision to step down from the foundation's board in 2012 marked a turning point in his public image. The outcome of the interview remains uncertain, with many speculating about the potential consequences for Armstrong, including possible perjury charges related to his sworn testimony denying the use of banned substances.

reference: – Lance Armstrong is rumored to be ready to confess to drug use—and it could happen on Oprah's show. The disgraced cyclist will give a "no-holds-barred" interview to Oprah Winfrey that will air on her network next week, CNN reports. In his first formal interview since he was banned from the sport and stripped of his titles for doping, Armstrong will "address the alleged doping scandal, years of accusations of cheating, and charges of lying about the use of performance-enhancing drugs throughout his storied cycling career," the network says in a statement. In a 60 Minutes Sports interview to be aired tonight, the chief of the US Anti-Doping Agency says an Armstrong representative offered to donate around $250,000 to the agency, reports the New York Times. Travis Tygart, who accuses Armstrong of trying to intimidate former teammates, says the 2004 offer represented a clear conflict of interest. "We had no hesitation in rejecting that offer," he says. Tygart says he received death threats during his organization's investigation of Armstrong.

final_summary:
In the aftermath of a brutal double stabbing in a New York City Housing Authority (NYCHA) building in East New York, authorities launched a massive manhunt for the suspect, Daniel St. Hubert, a 27-year-old black male with a history of violence. On June 1, 2014, six-year-old Prince Joshua "P.J." Avitto and seven-year-old Mikayla Capers were stabbed multiple times in the elevator of the Boulevard Houses on Schenk Avenue. P.J. succumbed to his injuries, while Mikayla remained in critical condition.

The NYPD, led by Police Commissioner Bill Bratton, Mayor Bill de Blasio, and Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams, held a press conference to announce the suspect's identity and appeal to the public for assistance in locating him. St. Hubert was described as 5'9" and 210 pounds, with nine prior arrests and a record of domestic violence. He was on parole since May 23 for assaulting a police officer and a correction officer.

Chief of Detectives Robert Boyce emphasized that St. Hubert was considered "armed and dangerous" and urged the public not to approach him. The police released his name, photos, and a description of his tattoos, including a satanic goat's head with horns under the word "Aries" on his right forearm.

As the investigation unfolded, authorities revealed that forensic evidence, specifically DNA from the steak knife used in the attack, linked St. Hubert to the crime scene. A cell phone trace and DNA match ultimately led police to his location in Queens, where he was apprehended shortly after 8 p.m. on June 4, 2014, in the vicinity of 145th Street and 133rd Avenue.

St. Hubert's arrest brought relief to the community, particularly the families of the victims. P.J.'s mother, Aricka McClinton, expressed gratitude to the NYPD, saying, "It feels like my baby can rest in peace." Mikayla's great-grandmother, Regenia Trevathan, also expressed confidence in the justice system and the NYPD.

The case drew attention to concerns about safety in NYCHA buildings, with many lacking security cameras, despite a $27 million allocation for security equipment. Local politicians, including Mayor de Blasio, vowed to increase police presence in these areas and work closely with residents to ensure their safety.

The investigation also raised questions about the potential connection between St. Hubert and the fatal stabbing of 18-year-old Tanaya Grant Copeland on Friday, four blocks away from the Boulevard Houses. Although St. Hubert has not been charged in Copeland's murder, police suspect he may be linked to the crime due to similarities between the knives used in both incidents.

As the community breathed a sigh of relief following St. Hubert's arrest, community leaders and activists praised the NYPD for their efforts in bringing the suspect to justice. The case serves as a reminder of the importance of community vigilance and cooperation in preventing such tragedies.

history.draft:
In the aftermath of a brutal double stabbing in a New York City Housing Authority (NYCHA) building in East New York, authorities launched a massive manhunt for the suspect, Daniel St. Hubert, a 27-year-old black male with a history of violence. On June 1, 2014, six-year-old Prince Joshua "P.J." Avitto and seven-year-old Mikayla Capers were stabbed multiple times in the elevator of the Boulevard Houses on Schenk Avenue. P.J. succumbed to his injuries, while Mikayla remained in critical condition.

The NYPD, led by Police Commissioner Bill Bratton, Mayor Bill de Blasio, and Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams, held a press conference to announce the suspect's identity and appeal to the public for assistance in locating him. St. Hubert was described as 5'9" and 210 pounds, with nine prior arrests and a record of domestic violence. He was on parole since May 23 for assaulting a police officer and a correction officer.

Chief of Detectives Robert Boyce emphasized that St. Hubert was considered "armed and dangerous" and urged the public not to approach him. The police released his name, photos, and a description of his tattoos, including a satanic goat's head with horns under the word "Aries" on his right forearm.

As the investigation unfolded, authorities revealed that forensic evidence, specifically DNA from the steak knife used in the attack, linked St. Hubert to the crime scene. A cell phone trace and DNA match ultimately led police to his location in Queens, where he was apprehended shortly after 8 p.m. on June 4, 2014, in the vicinity of 145th Street and 133rd Avenue.

St. Hubert's arrest brought relief to the community, particularly the families of the victims. P.J.'s mother, Aricka McClinton, expressed gratitude to the NYPD, saying, "It feels like my baby can rest in peace." Mikayla's great-grandmother, Regenia Trevathan, also expressed confidence in the justice system and the NYPD.

The case drew attention to concerns about safety in NYCHA buildings, with many lacking security cameras, despite a $27 million allocation for security equipment. Local politicians, including Mayor de Blasio, vowed to increase police presence in these areas and work closely with residents to ensure their safety.

The investigation also raised questions about the potential connection between St. Hubert and the fatal stabbing of 18-year-old Tanaya Grant Copeland on Friday, four blocks away from the Boulevard Houses. Although St. Hubert has not been charged in Copeland's murder, police suspect he may be linked to the crime due to similarities between the knives used in both incidents.

As the community breathed a sigh of relief following St. Hubert's arrest, community leaders and activists praised the NYPD for their efforts in bringing the suspect to justice. The case serves as a reminder of the importance of community vigilance and cooperation in preventing such tragedies.

reference: – Police in New York City arrested a 27-year-old man tonight accused of fatally stabbing a 6-year-old boy and injuring his 7-year-old playmate over the weekend, reports Gothamist. Daniel St. Hubert is in custody, the arrest made soon after police publicly identified him as a suspect. As the Daily News explains, the break in the case came when investigators matched DNA found on a knife left at the scene with the state's criminal database. Hubert has a long criminal record for offenses including assault and had been paroled on May 23, reports the New York Times. Police haven't called him a suspect in another fatal stabbing that occurred just two nights earlier, but the investigation is continuing. The same brand of knife—a model discontinued 10 years ago—was left behind in the slaying of 18-year-old Tanaya Copeland, and the man seen fleeing that scene had the same general description as Hubert. The 6-year-old boy who was killed, PJ Avitto, had gotten into an elevator with friend Mikayla Capers in their Brooklyn housing complex before being attacked. Mikayla remains in critical condition.

final_summary:
In the midst of widespread discontent and frustration with authoritarian regimes across the Middle East, a pivotal moment of change unfolded in Tunisia, a country that had long been a bastion of stability in the region. The catalyst for this transformation was the self-immolation of Mohamed Bouazizi, a 26-year-old university graduate who had grown disillusioned with the government's treatment of vendors and the lack of opportunities for young people. His act of desperation sparked a wave of protests that swept across the country, fueled by grievances over rising food prices, corruption, unemployment, and the repression of dissent.

As the protests gained momentum, they began to resonate with people across the Arab world, who felt a sense of solidarity with the Tunisian people's struggle for freedom and dignity. Social media platforms, particularly Facebook, played a crucial role in amplifying the message of the protests and mobilizing support from around the globe. The hashtag #TunisiaRevolution trended on Twitter, with many users expressing their admiration for the courage and resilience of the Tunisian people.

The protests in Tunisia were marked by a sense of spontaneity and grassroots organization, with ordinary citizens taking to the streets to demand change. The movement was characterized by a diverse range of participants, including students, workers, and professionals, who came together to challenge the authority of the government. The protests were met with a heavy-handed response from the security forces, who used tear gas, batons, and live ammunition to disperse the crowds.

Despite the risks, the protesters persisted, and their determination eventually led to the resignation of President Zine El Abidine Ben Ali, who fled the country on January 14, 2011. The fall of Ben Ali sent shockwaves across the Arab world, inspiring similar movements in other countries, including Egypt, Yemen, and Libya. The Tunisian people's victory was hailed as a beacon of hope for democratic reform and human rights in the region.

However, the aftermath of Ben Ali's departure was marked by chaos and uncertainty. Looters and gangs roamed the streets, targeting businesses and properties associated with the former regime. The security forces, still loyal to Ben Ali, were accused of orchestrating some of the violence to discredit the protesters and justify harsh security measures. The interim government, led by Fouad Mebazaa, struggled to maintain order and establish a new sense of normalcy.

As the situation in Tunisia continued to unfold, the international community watched with great interest. The French government, which had long supported Ben Ali, asked his relatives to leave the country and launched an investigation into suspicious money transfers between Tunisia and France. The United Nations Security Council issued a statement calling for calm and urging the Tunisian authorities to respect human rights and fundamental freedoms.

In the midst of the turmoil, the Tunisian people remained resolute in their pursuit of democracy and justice. They demanded a new constitution, free and fair elections, and an end to the corruption and repression that had characterized the Ben Ali regime. As the country navigated this uncertain period, the people of Tunisia looked to the future with hope and determination, knowing that their struggle for freedom and dignity had inspired a new generation of activists and reformers across the Arab world.

reference: – Tunisia got a new interim president today—parliamentary leader Fouad Mebazaa—who is technically the third leader in 24 hours, notes the Los Angeles Times. He takes over from the previous interim president, who briefly held power when longtime leader El Abidine Ben Ali fled the country in the face of mass protests over rising prices, joblessness, corruption, repression, you name it. The storyline continues to shape up as one that could set a historical precedent—an Arab populace rising up against its leader. Anthony Shadid, New York Times: "The most enthusiastic suggested it was the Arab world’s Gdansk, the birthplace of Solidarity in Poland, which heralded the end to Communist rule in Eastern Europe. That seemed premature, particularly because the contours of the government emerging in Tunisia were still unclear." Shadi Hamid, Huffington Post: "No one should underestimate what happened yesterday in Tunisia. If the revolution succeeds, it may very well prove to be one of the most important moments in recent Arab history. It will alter the calculus not only for Arab regimes—who are watching very, very nervously—but for Western powers that have long oriented their Middle East policy around seemingly stable, autocratic governments." Click for the latest as chaos continues on the streets.

final_summary:
In the aftermath of the deadly attacks in Paris, the international hacking collective Anonymous declared "war" on ISIS, vowing to dismantle the terrorist group's online presence. This marked a significant escalation of Anonymous' efforts against ISIS, which began in earnest after the Charlie Hebdo attacks in January. Since then, the group has been actively working to identify and take down ISIS-linked websites, social media accounts, and email addresses. 

Anonymous' initial campaign, dubbed #OpISIS, aimed to expose and destroy online assets associated with ISIS, resulting in the shutdown of several websites and the exposure of thousands of Twitter accounts allegedly used by ISIS activists. Following the Paris attacks, Anonymous launched a new campaign, #OpParis, which has seen the group take down over 3,800 pro-ISIS Twitter accounts. Members of Anonymous are now organizing into teams for intelligence gathering, analysis, and dissemination, aiming to further degrade ISIS' online capabilities.

However, experts warn that Anonymous' methods may not be effective in disrupting ISIS' communication networks. Terrorist organizations, like ISIS, are adopting similar tactics used by hacktivists, making it challenging to take down their online presence. Moreover, Anonymous' actions may hinder government efforts to investigate and disrupt ISIS' activities. 

Ghost Security Group, a separate hacktivist collective, has been working closely with government agencies to provide valuable intelligence on ISIS' online activities. Their efforts have led to the disruption of a planned attack in Tunisia and have been recognized by government officials as a valuable resource in the fight against terrorism. 

In response to Anonymous' declaration of war, ISIS released a statement advising its supporters on how to protect themselves from cyber attacks. The statement, posted on the Telegram app, offered tips on how to avoid detection, including not opening suspicious links and changing locations using workaround technology. 

As the conflict between Anonymous and ISIS continues to unfold, the effectiveness of Anonymous' efforts remains uncertain. While the group has managed to take down numerous online assets associated with ISIS, the terrorist organization's ability to adapt and evolve makes it challenging to determine the impact of Anonymous' actions.

reference: – Anonymous is following through with its promise to target ISIS in a cyber war after the Paris terror attacks. Operating under the hashtags #opISIS and #opParis, the group has begun leaking the personal details of alleged ISIS recruiters, reports the Independent. Anonymous also has compiled lists of social media accounts linked to extremists in an attempt to have them removed, and at least one post reveals the physical address of a suspected recruiter in Europe. Another list, spotted by Tech Insider, includes more than 80 names, though the site notes that Anonymous has wrongly identified extremists before. The group is also working to take down ISIS-affiliated websites using denial-of-service attacks to overload servers. ISIS, however, seems unperturbed. "All they can do is hacking (sic) Twitter accounts, emails etc.," the terrorist group said in a statement Monday, calling Anonymous a bunch of "idiots." Still, ISIS shared tips with supporters about how to avoid cyberattacks, including altering email addresses, changing a computer's shared location, and ignoring suspicious links, per Newsweek. While Anonymous' attacks could hinder communications between ISIS supporters, analysts tell the Christian Science Monitor that they're unlikely to do much good. "What they are doing is more of simple harassment," one says. "In the end it is not going to help law enforcement to prevent an actual physical attack like what happened in Paris." (ISIS has a 24-hour help desk for jihadis having tech trouble.)

final_summary:
Deep beneath the surface of Greenland's ice sheet lies a 2.7-million-year-old secret - a remnant of a lush tundra landscape that existed long before humans set foot on the planet. Researchers led by Paul Bierman, a geomorphologist at the University of Vermont, made the groundbreaking discovery using a unique combination of techniques to analyze the ancient soil trapped within the ice.

The team extracted the soil from the GISP2 ice core, which was drilled in 1993 to reach the bedrock beneath Summit, the highest point on Greenland's ice sheet. By employing beryllium-10 dating, a method that measures the amount of a radioactive isotope produced by cosmic rays, the researchers determined that the soil had been exposed to the surface for between 200,000 and 1 million years before being buried under the ice.

The finding suggests that Greenland's landscape was remarkably stable before the onset of the ice sheet, with the tundra remaining relatively unchanged for millions of years. This challenges previous assumptions that the ice sheet would have eroded the underlying terrain, especially considering the significant warming event 130,000 years ago, which was as severe as the current climate change predictions.

Bierman's team believes that the key to the preservation of the ancient landscape lies in the nature of the ice sheet itself. Unlike warm-based glaciers that carve into their surroundings, the Greenland ice sheet appears to be frozen to its bedrock, allowing it to deform and preserve the underlying terrain. This phenomenon, known as a cold-based glacier, has been observed in other regions, including Alaska, where tundra landscapes have been dated to be around 800,000 years old.

The discovery has significant implications for our understanding of climate change and the behavior of large ice sheets. As the Greenland ice sheet remains frozen, it preserves a window into the past, providing valuable insights into the Earth's climate history. The researchers are now planning to explore similar sites in Antarctica, where they hope to uncover even older landscapes.

The findings also highlight the resilience of the Greenland ice sheet, which has endured despite periods of significant warming. According to Bierman, the ice sheet's ability to persist means that human activities may ultimately determine its fate, rather than natural climate fluctuations. The preservation of the ancient tundra landscape serves as a poignant reminder of the importance of protecting our planet's fragile ecosystems and mitigating the effects of climate change.

reference: – The soil under the highest point of the Greenland Ice Sheet, scientists have learned, is 2.7 million years old, LiveScience reports. In other words, the silt buried under thousands of feet of ice "has been preserved from beyond the dawn of humankind," says Paul Bierman, who determined the soil's age through a geochemical dating process. The findings also suggest Greenland wasn't always as frosty as it is today: Instead, it was a tundra for between 200,000 and one million years before it was encased in ice. It was once home to bits of forest, the scientists found using offshore plant DNA. In other words, as the International Business Times has it, "Greenland really was green." The Greenland Ice Sheet that's there now, however, has been remarkably durable: The findings imply that it made it through a strong period of warming some 130,000 years ago. "It's unlikely the ice sheet has disappeared for significant periods of time in the last three million years, at least at this one pinprick on this big island," Bierman says. "Now human activity may spell the end of it." (What else lies under Greenland's ice? A canyon that dwarfs the Grand Canyon, an aquifer holding 100 billion tons of water, and two huge lakes.)

final_summary:
Tamerlan Tsarnaev, the older brother involved in the Boston Marathon bombings, died on April 19 after a confrontation with police, where he was shot and run over by a car driven by his younger brother, Dzhokhar. His body remained unclaimed at the Boston medical examiner's office for nearly two weeks, prompting a funeral home to step in and arrange for a second, independent autopsy at the request of his relatives.

The death certificate, obtained by CNN, lists the cause of death as gunshot wounds to the torso and extremities, along with blunt trauma to the head and torso, and notes that Tsarnaev was pronounced dead on arrival. Despite the completion of the death certificate, Tsarnaev's burial remains uncertain due to various obstacles. 

His uncle, Ruslan Tsarni, initially condemned the alleged attacks and had a funeral parlor collect the body, but their parents in Dagestan have refused to bring his body back to Russia for burial. As a result, a funeral home in Worcester, Massachusetts, is working to find a suitable burial site within the state. However, several cemeteries in Massachusetts, New Jersey, and Connecticut have declined to accept Tsarnaev's body due to concerns about potential backlash from the public.

Peter Stefan, the owner of Graham Putnam & Mahoney Funeral Parlors, expressed frustration with the situation, stating that everyone deserves to be buried, regardless of the circumstances. He emphasized that his funeral home has experience with Muslim services and is prepared to handle the necessary procedures, including washing the body and preparing it for burial.

Stefan plans to seek assistance from the government if a burial site cannot be secured, citing that it is the responsibility of the community to provide a dignified burial for all individuals, even those involved in violent acts. Meanwhile, Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, the surviving brother, is being held at a federal prison facility in Massachusetts, facing charges related to the use of a weapon of mass destruction, which carries the death penalty.

An investigation into the bombings revealed that the brothers had originally planned to target the city's Independence Day celebrations but shifted their focus to the Boston Marathon due to the availability of explosives. Intelligence agencies are reviewing their handling of Tamerlan Tsarnaev's case prior to the attack, with President Barack Obama acknowledging that improvements can be made in sharing information between agencies.

Three friends of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev have been accused of assisting the brothers in discarding evidence related to the bombings, including a laptop and a backpack containing bomb-making materials. The FBI is currently analyzing the laptop for potential connections to terrorist organizations.
