reference: – The death certificate of Tamerlan Tsarnaev apparently will hold no surprises. While Massachusetts officials have yet to release it publicly, the owner of the Worcester funeral home with his body said it lists the cause of death as "gunshot wounds of torso and extremities" and "blunt trauma to head and torso," reports CNN. That gibes with what authorities have previously said: Tamerlan was hit by police bullets and run over by Dzhokhar as the younger brother fled their shootout in a stolen SUV. The certificate lists the time of death as 1:35am on April 19, reports AP. The funeral home, meanwhile, is still striking out in finding a cemetery willing to take the body. "There’s a fear there,” owner Peter Stefan of the Graham Putnam and Mahoney funeral parlor tells WBZ. "Will people want to go to the cemetery, or will people be upset that somebody who’s a terrorist will be buried next to their Uncle Freddy.”

final_summary:
On Tuesday afternoon, a woman in her 80s died by suicide in her room at the historic Berkeley City Club, prompting the evacuation of the building's guests and residents. Authorities received a call at 1:56 pm reporting a "person down" and upon arrival, they found evidence of a suicide involving hazardous materials. A suicide note was reportedly left outside the victim's apartment, alerting responders to the situation. 

Hazardous materials crews and bomb squad technicians from multiple agencies, including the Berkeley Police, Berkeley Fire, and the University of California Police Department, responded to the scene. They worked together to contain and remove the hazardous material, identified as sodium azide, which is a colorless salt commonly used in car airbag systems and becomes acutely toxic when mixed with water. 

The removal process led to the closure of Durant Avenue between Telegraph and Shattuck avenues for several hours, causing disruptions to traffic and pedestrian flow, as well as diverting AC Transit buses. The Berkeley City Club, located at 2315 Durant Avenue, was evacuated, and the surrounding area remained cordoned off until further notice.

According to Berkeley Police Sergeant Joe Okies, the death investigation was ongoing, and the coroner had not yet arrived at the scene by 6:30 pm. The Berkeley Fire Department initially responded to the incident but handed over control to the police due to the presence of hazardous materials. 

In a statement to its members, the Berkeley City Club clarified that none of its members, residents, or staff were involved or harmed in the incident. The club, designed by Julia Morgan, offers various amenities, including a hotel, indoor swimming pool, restaurant, hair salon, and meeting spaces.

Police in the California city say Hazmat teams and bomb squad technicians were called in after a note was found outside the woman's third-story apartment warning of the danger, the Oakland Tribune reports. Police say a "known quantity of what appeared to be a hazardous material has been removed" and steps are being taken to make the building—which houses both hotel guests and residents—safe, reports Berkeleyside. The woman is believed to have used sodium azide, a colorless salt used as the gas-forming component in car airbag systems. It becomes extremely toxic when mixed with water.

final_summary:
Samantha Bee, host of the TBS satirical-comedy program "Full Frontal," is organizing an alternative gala dinner in Washington, D.C., set to take place on April 29, coinciding with the annual White House Correspondents' Association (WHCA) dinner. The event, dubbed "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner," aims to provide a platform for comedians and journalists to poke fun at President Trump without the constraints of the traditional dinner format. Bee stated that the idea emerged after the 2016 presidential election, when she and her team discussed the possibility of the WHCA dinner changing or not happening under a Trump presidency. They decided to create their own event, allowing them to tackle topics and jokes that might be off-limits at the official dinner.

Bee emphasized that the goal is not to supplant the WHCA dinner but rather to offer an alternative, ensuring that Trump receives the roasting he deserves, regardless of whether the traditional dinner takes place. The event will be held at the Willard Hotel, and all proceeds will benefit the Committee to Protect Journalists, highlighting the importance of supporting journalism amidst the current tense relations between the White House and the press corps.

The concept of "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner" aligns with the evolving landscape of late-night television, where hosts like Stephen Colbert and Seth Meyers are experimenting with innovative ways to engage with their audiences and stay relevant in a fragmented media environment. Bee's initiative also reflects the need for comedians to adapt to the changing times, using various platforms, such as social media and live events, to connect with their viewers.

In recent years, late-night hosts have expanded their reach beyond traditional television broadcasts, incorporating digital content, interactive experiences, and collaborations with other artists. Examples include Colbert's introduction of new segments on repeat episodes and a Twitter feed pretending to be written by disaffected staff members, as well as Meyers' release of his popular "A Closer Look" segment on social media before its televised debut.

Bee's approach is distinct, however, as she focuses on creating a unique experience that combines humor, commentary, and support for journalism. By inviting "unsung journalists" and "super fans" to participate, the event aims to foster a sense of community and solidarity among those who value independent reporting and free speech.

The timing of the announcement comes as the WHCA dinner faces uncertainty due to the strained relationships between the Trump administration and the press corps. Past events have been marred by controversies, such as comedian Larry Wilmore's 2016 monologue, which used a racial slur. The dinner's future remains uncertain, with some speculating that Trump may skip the event altogether.

While the exact lineup for "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner" has yet to be revealed, Bee hinted at a diverse range of performers and guests, promising a memorable evening that will tackle the complexities of the Trump presidency and the state of journalism in America.

They decided to create their own event, allowing them to tackle topics and jokes that might be off-limits at the official dinner.

Bee emphasized that the goal is not to supplant the WHCA dinner but rather to offer an alternative, ensuring that Trump receives the roasting he deserves, regardless of whether the traditional dinner takes place. The event will be held at the Willard Hotel, and all proceeds will benefit the Committee to Protect Journalists, highlighting the importance of supporting journalism amidst the current tense relations between the White House and the press corps.

reference: – If Donald Trump chooses to roast journalists, celebrities, and politicians at the annual White House Correspondents' Dinner in Washington, DC, this year, it'll be with the knowledge that he's being brutally mocked not too far away. Samantha Bee of TBS' Full Frontal says she'll host celebrities, journalists, and comedians at a Trump roast at the Willard Hotel in Washington on April 29. Bee tells the New York Times that the goal of the event, dubbed "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner," is not to "supersede" the official dinner scheduled for the same night, but simply to "ensure that we get to properly roast the president." She promises "plenty of surprises," per Variety. "If you're not careful you just might learn something. Specifically, you'll learn how screwed we'd be without a free press," she adds. It's not yet clear when or how the event will be broadcast, or even who will attend, but Bee says proceeds will go to the Committee to Protect Journalists. Whether the official Correspondents' dinner will happen is also unclear given Trump's tumultuous relationship with the press, his history with the dinner, and "his oft-demonstrated thin skin," reports the Times. The Independent notes the dinner has been an annual occurrence since 1983.

final_summary:
Italian police have made a significant breakthrough in their investigation into the secretive 'Ndrangheta mafia organization after deciphering a coded text found during a probe into a high-profile murder in Rome last year. The cryptic document, which was discovered along with weapons and ammunition, is believed to be part of the oath-taking process used by the San Luca clan within the 'Ndrangheta. According to authorities, the script is a unique alphabet created by the mafia group, combining elements from various languages including Arabic, Cyrillic, and Chinese characters.

The decoded text reveals the formula that new members must recite to join the 'Ndrangheta, providing insight into the group's mystical initiation rituals. The translation is described as rich in symbolism, detailing how to identify a true mafioso, also known as a "Man of Honour", through subtle signs surrounding them. This discovery highlights the 'Ndrangheta's continued reliance on ancient practices despite its expansion into global organized crime and large-scale business ventures.

The finding is particularly significant given the 'Ndrangheta's rise to becoming Italy's most powerful mafia, surpassing the infamous Cosa Nostra from Sicily. With operations spanning across Europe and ties to Colombian drug cartels, the 'Ndrangheta's influence extends far beyond its roots in the Calabria region of southern Italy. The deciphered document serves as a rare glimpse into the inner workings of this secretive organization, shedding light on the mystique surrounding its recruitment processes.

reference: – "For the baptism, five people are needed—no more and no less," reads the coded instructions. But this "baptism" isn't the usual sort: It's for new members of the 'Ndrangheta crime syndicate in Italy, reports the Telegraph. Police discovered the instructions during a murder investigation, and two officers who like to solve puzzles got busy deciphering the text, which resembles ancient Egyptian and Greek. The big break came when they figured out the letter C, and the rest fell into place. It's oh-so-dramatic stuff: The initiation must take place in a "sacred, sacrosanct, and secure" location, and the newbie must cut his skin with a knife in order to swear a blood oath—"an eternal allegiance to the honored society.” The BBC reports that the 'Ndrangheta is thought to be the most powerful mafia in Italy, with a specialty in cocaine smuggling. "Finding such a document shows that even if they are projected towards big businesses and are a criminal group with a global presence, they still use archaic systems," says a police official in Rome. (The syndicate apparently isn't a fan of the new pope.)

final_summary:
A disturbing incident occurred at a home in Wiltshire where a tetraplegic patient, Jamie Merrett, 37, was being cared for on a life-support machine. Merrett, who was left paralyzed from the neck down in a 2002 road accident, had a bedside camera installed due to concerns over the quality of care he was receiving. The footage captured a critical moment in January 2009 when agency nurse Violetta Aylward mistakenly switched off Merrett's ventilator, leaving him without oxygen for 21 minutes. This prolonged period without ventilation resulted in severe brain damage to Merrett, significantly impairing his cognitive abilities.

According to Merrett's sister, Karren Reynolds, her brother's level of understanding post-injury has decreased to that of a young child, rendering his life "completely changed" and "nowhere near what it was before." Prior to the incident, Merrett was able to communicate effectively, operate a computer using voice-activated technology, and engage in in-depth conversations.

The agency supplying Aylward, Ambition 24hours, declined to comment on the matter, citing an ongoing internal investigation. However, a confidential report by Wiltshire social services revealed that the agency was aware of its responsibility to provide nurses trained in ventilator usage but lacked adequate systems to verify staff qualifications. 

NHS Wiltshire Primary Care Trust acknowledged the incident and stated that measures were taken to prevent similar occurrences in the future. The trust also expressed regret for the compromised care experienced by Merrett. Aylward has been suspended by the Nursing and Midwifery Council pending an investigation into the incident, which highlights the importance of adhering to professional standards and guidelines, including the requirement for nurses to work within their level of competence and possess necessary skills for assigned tasks.

reference: – A British quadriplegic who installed hidden cameras in his home because he was worried about the quality of his care was left severely brain damaged by a mistake that confirmed his suspicions. The cameras captured a nurse who lacked the proper training accidentally disconnecting his ventilator and then struggling to reattach it, AP reports. Jamie Merrett was starved of oxygen for 21 minutes before paramedics arrived. Merrett, 37, was paralyzed from the neck down after a car accident 8 years ago but had been able to talk, operate a wheelchair, and use a computer before the nurse's blunder. His sister says his level of understanding has now dropped to that of a small child. "He has an existence but it's nowhere near what it was before," she told the BBC. Officials for the local health authority say the nurse has been suspended and they have "put in place a series of actions to ensure such an event will not occur again."

final_summary:
On October 23, 2018, US President Donald Trump held a press conference in the Rose Garden to discuss the newly agreed upon trade deal between the United States, Canada, and Mexico. During this event, Trump called on ABC News reporter Cecilia Vega to pose a question, but before she could even receive the microphone, he made a comment suggesting she was surprised or shocked to be chosen. Vega responded by stating she wasn't in a state of shock, prompting Trump to retort that she rarely expresses gratitude, implying she doesn't think before speaking.

The initial White House transcript of the press conference, released several hours after the event, inaccurately reflected Trump's words, quoting him as saying "I know you're not thanking. You never do," instead of his actual statement. In reality, Trump had said "That's okay. I know you're not thinking, you never do."

Vega continued to press Trump for answers, asking a question regarding Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh, which Trump initially declined to address, citing a preference for trade-related inquiries. However, Trump eventually allowed Vega to reask her question, giving her the opportunity to pursue the topic further. The exchange sparked widespread attention online, with Vega taking to Twitter to express her perspective on the situation, emphasizing that a press conference allows journalists to ask any relevant questions they deem necessary under the First Amendment.

reference: – President Trump made headlines Monday when he delivered a slam to ABC News journalist Cecilia Vega. Then the White House kept the story alive with a controversial official transcript—one that toned down Trump's remark—before fixing it after an outcry. You can watch the original back and forth for yourself in this video. Trump selects Vega to ask the first question at a news conference, then jokes that she is shocked at that. "I'm not, thank you, Mr. President," Vega responded. Trump, apparently mishearing her, then says, "I know you're not thinking. You never do." However, Politico reports that the first version of the White House transcript, out later Monday, quotes Trump as saying: "I know you're not thanking. You never do." The transcript was spotted by Voice of America journalist Steve Herman, who tweeted the pertinent section and said he wasn't buying it. "I was sitting just behind her in the Rose Garden and we all clearly heard him say: "I know you're not thinking. You never do.” As critics accused the White House of whitewashing the exchange, a revised transcript appeared Tuesday morning, reflecting that Trump did indeed say "thinking," reports NBC News.

final_summary:
On March 18, a fatal altercation occurred at the east Erie residence shared by Windi C. Thomas, 44, and her boyfriend, 44-year-old Keeno Butler. According to authorities, Thomas attacked Butler, resulting in his death due to blunt force trauma to the neck and thoracic compression, exacerbated by blunt force trauma to the head. 

Thomas initially claimed to investigators that she used a folding knife to cut Butler's hand and struck him in the head with a table leg during a confrontation. However, she later admitted to placing her head on the couch so that a portion of her body would be on top of Butler's head, which ultimately led to his demise. Given her significant weight advantage – approximately 300 pounds compared to Butler's 120 pounds – this action likely proved fatal.

Following an investigation by the Erie police, Thomas was charged with multiple counts including aggravated assault, reckless endangerment, and possession of an instrument of a crime. As part of a plea deal, these charges were dropped in exchange for her guilty plea to third-degree murder, an unpremeditated killing with malice. This charge carries a maximum sentence of 20 to 40 years in state prison.

As part of the plea agreement, Thomas has agreed to a recommended sentence of 18 to 36 years in prison. This reduction of two to four years from the maximum sentence was deemed acceptable by Thomas' defense attorney, Mark Del Duca, who described it as a "good result" given the circumstances of the case. However, the victim's sister, Sandra Butler, expressed disappointment with the proposed sentence, stating that she believes Thomas should receive a longer term, potentially up to 40 years or even life imprisonment.

The case is set to proceed to sentencing on December 21, with Thomas currently being held without bond in the Erie County Prison. The incident has left the Butler family reeling, particularly Sandra, who revealed that her mother continues to struggle with grief, calling out for her deceased brother every night.

reference: – A 300-pound woman in Pennsylvania has pleaded guilty to murdering her boyfriend by stabbing him, hitting in the head with a table leg, and finally crushing the 120-pound man with her own weight. Windi Thomas, 44, pleaded guilty to third-degree murder in the March 18 death of Keeno Butler in exchange for a recommended sentence of 18 to 36 years in state prison, the Erie Times-News reports. Police say Thomas admitted crushing Butler after a fight at their Erie residence. She told them she positioned herself so that some of her weight would be on his head. The death was ruled a homicide "caused by respiratory insufficiency secondary to blunt force trauma to the neck and thoracic compression, exacerbated by blunt force trauma to the head," court papers state. Sandra Butler, the victim's sister, says Thomas should have gotten life. "My mother, she calls for him every night," she says. Thomas, who had been scheduled to go on trial next week, will be sentenced Dec. 21, the AP reports. (A Fitbit led to this man's arrest after he claimed an obese intruder killed his wife.)

final_summary:
Comedy Central confirmed that Jon Stewart, the host of "The Daily Show," will be leaving the anchor's chair after nearly 17 years. Stewart, 52, announced his departure during the show's taping on Tuesday, stating that he would remain with the program until later this year. The exact date of his exit is uncertain, with Stewart mentioning possible dates ranging from July to December. 

Stewart began hosting "The Daily Show" in January 1999, succeeding Craig Kilborn, and under his leadership, the show has won 20 prime-time Emmys and become a cultural touchstone for millions of fans. During his tenure, Stewart emerged as a leading political satirist and one of the toughest interviewers in the news media, earning praise for his unique voice and vision. The show's success led to the creation of other influential Comedy Central programs, including "The Colbert Report" and "The Nightly Show with Larry Wilmore."

Throughout his career, Stewart has directed several films, including "Rosewater," a drama about the detention of Iranian-Canadian journalist Maziar Bahari during Iran's 2009 election protests. He has also acted in various projects, such as "The Larry Sanders Show" and "Rosewater." Stewart's impact extends beyond the show, as he has advocated for causes like providing medical benefits for first responders from the 9/11 terrorist attacks and supporting Egyptian satirist Bassem Youssef.

Stewart's departure marks the latest change in the Comedy Central late-night lineup, following the exits of Stephen Colbert and John Oliver. Despite the uncertainty surrounding his future plans, Stewart expressed gratitude towards the show's staff and audience, stating that he loves the people and values the experience he gained while hosting "The Daily Show."

reference: – Jon Stewart is retiring from the Daily Show later this year. He reportedly revealed the big news during the taping of tonight's episode, reports the AV Club. In a statement to Politico, a Comedy Central exec confirms that Stewart will step down at a still-unspecified date after more than 15 years. "Through his unique voice and vision, the Daily Show has become a cultural touchstone for millions of fans and an unparalleled platform for political comedy that will endure for years to come," says network chief Michele Ganeless, which seems to suggest that the show will go on with a new host. This is one of those rare cases when a network's official statement is right in line with what most others are writing: "To a younger generation of viewers who stopped watching the news, his late-night 'fake' news show—which skewered politicians and the media—made him a trusted messenger to millions," writes Emily Yahr at the Washington Post. Business Insider: The show is a "cultural force." NPR: A "hugely influential figure in American politics." New York Times: A "nightly home for sharp-edged political satire and up-to-the-moment commentary on the news." (Stewart, of course, is a movie director now, too.)

final_summary:
On February 14, 2011, First Lady Michelle Obama launched a nationwide anti-obesity campaign to combat America's growing obesity problem. However, conservative media outlets began criticizing her efforts, labeling them as government overreach and personally attacking her weight. This criticism escalated into sexist attacks, with radio host Rush Limbaugh referring to her as "Michelle, My Butt."

Conservative commentator James O'Keefe, known for his provocative tactics, faced backlash after attempting to prank a CNN reporter, Abbie Boudreau, by luring her onto a boat under false pretenses and secretly filming her. A leaked document outlining the plan included explicit content, leading to widespread criticism.

O'Keefe defended himself by claiming the document was a draft and not intended to be carried out as written. He stated that he often receives numerous ideas and schemes, and while he may approve of the concept, he does not endorse every detail. However, evidence suggests that the document was drafted by Ben Wetmore, someone O'Keefe considers a mentor and has worked with extensively.

O'Keefe's defense rested on the claim that the plan was meant to be a form of "counter-seduction satire" and that Boudreau would have given consent before being filmed. He claimed that the explicit content in the document was not representative of his true intentions and that he would have been dressed conservatively during the encounter. However, other individuals involved in the plan, including O'Keefe's accomplice Izzy Santa, believed the goal was to creep Boudreau out, suggesting that the plan was not as innocuous as O'Keefe claimed.

Despite O'Keefe's denials, the leaked document raised concerns about his intentions and the potential harm caused by his actions. The controversy surrounding O'Keefe's behavior further highlighted the tension between conservative media outlets and their critics, with some accusing O'Keefe of hypocrisy and others defending his right to free speech.

reference: – Andrew Breitbart, fresh off the news of Shirley Sherrod's lawsuit against him, has gotten himself embroiled in another controversy. His Big Government website lashed out at Michelle Obama's anti-obesity campaign ... by basically calling her fat. A cartoon strip on the website shows an overweight Obama eating a huge pile of hamburgers while talking to her big-eared husband about the campaign, Salon reports. The punch line—"Shut up and pass the bacon!"—was originally "Shut up and pass the lard!" Media Matters notes. Click to see the cartoon.

final_summary:
Hurricane Isaac made landfall on the Gulf Coast on August 28, 2012, bringing with it powerful winds and heavy rainfall that caused widespread destruction and flooding in Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama. The storm's slow movement and large size exacerbated its impact, causing storm surges to push water over levees and flood homes in areas not protected by the federal levee system. In Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana, the storm surge overtopped a levee, resulting in significant flooding and stranding dozens of people in attics and on rooftops. Parish President Billy Nungesser estimated that 10 to 12 feet of water had accumulated in some homes, surpassing the levels seen during Hurricane Katrina seven years prior.

As the storm continued to move inland, it dumped heavy rainfall, causing flash flooding in metropolitan New Orleans and nearby coastal communities. The National Weather Service issued a flash flood warning for several Louisiana cities, including Timberlane, Metairie, Marrero, Kenner, Harvey, Avondale, New Orleans, New Orleans East, Chalmette, and Hahnville. The storm also produced a tornado warning for Hancock and Harrison counties in Mississippi, as well as a tornado watch for southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi.

The storm's impact was felt across the region, with over 815,000 customers losing power due to the hurricane. Entergy Louisiana reported that more than 256,000 customers were without power at one point, while Alabama Power reported that about 5,600 customers were without power, mostly on Dauphin Island. The Louisiana attorney general issued a statement reminding sex offenders that evacuating due to the storm does not exempt them from notifying law enforcement of their whereabouts.

As the storm continued to move inland, officials began to assess the damage and launch rescue efforts. The National Guard launched a rescue operation on the east bank of Plaquemines Parish, with the goal of reaching stranded residents and providing aid. However, the Army Corps of Engineers opposed the idea of cutting a hole in the Mississippi River levee to drain the Braithwaite area, instead opting to bring in pumps to alleviate the flooding.

In addition to the physical damage, the storm also caused emotional distress for many residents, particularly those who had experienced similar trauma during Hurricane Katrina. One resident, Joey Amann, recalled the devastation of losing his home to Katrina in 2005 and expressed concern that the current storm was worse than anticipated. Another resident, Bill Boesch, reflected on the painful memories of Katrina and the sense of unease that came with waiting for the storm to pass.

As the storm continued to move inland, officials urged residents to stay indoors and avoid traveling unless necessary. The city of New Orleans implemented a dusk-to-dawn curfew, and police were on high alert for looters. Mayor Mitch Landrieu reassured residents that the drinking water was safe and the sanitary sewer system was functioning properly, but warned that the cleanup phase would be critical and cautioned residents to be cautious.

The storm's impact was also felt economically, with gas prices spiking by nearly 5 cents per gallon nationwide due to refinery shutdowns along the Gulf Coast. However, experts predicted that the price increase would be short-lived, as the storm's winds were not expected to cause lasting damage to the refineries.

As the storm finally began to subside, officials began to assess the full extent of the damage and launch recovery efforts. The National Weather Service warned that the storm's aftermath would pose a significant threat, with isolated tornadoes possible along the central Gulf Coast region and parts of the lower Mississippi Valley through Wednesday. The storm's legacy would be remembered as a Category 1 hurricane that packed a powerful punch, leaving a trail of destruction and heartbreak in its wake.

history.draft:
Hurricane Isaac made landfall on the Gulf Coast on August 28, 2012, bringing with it powerful winds and heavy rainfall that caused widespread destruction and flooding in Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama. The storm's slow movement and large size exacerbated its impact, causing storm surges to push water over levees and flood homes in areas not protected by the federal levee system. In Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana, the storm surge overtopped a levee, resulting in significant flooding and stranding dozens of people in attics and on rooftops. Parish President Billy Nungesser estimated that 10 to 12 feet of water had accumulated in some homes, surpassing the levels seen during Hurricane Katrina seven years prior.

As the storm continued to move inland, it dumped heavy rainfall, causing flash flooding in metropolitan New Orleans and nearby coastal communities. The National Weather Service issued a flash flood warning for several Louisiana cities, including Timberlane, Metairie, Marrero, Kenner, Harvey, Avondale, New Orleans, New Orleans East, Chalmette, and Hahnville. The storm also produced a tornado warning for Hancock and Harrison counties in Mississippi, as well as a tornado watch for southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi.

The storm's impact was felt across the region, with over 815,000 customers losing power due to the hurricane. Entergy Louisiana reported that more than 256,000 customers were without power at one point, while Alabama Power reported that about 5,600 customers were without power, mostly on Dauphin Island. The Louisiana attorney general issued a statement reminding sex offenders that evacuating due to the storm does not exempt them from notifying law enforcement of their whereabouts.

As the storm continued to move inland, officials began to assess the damage and launch rescue efforts. The National Guard launched a rescue operation on the east bank of Plaquemines Parish, with the goal of reaching stranded residents and providing aid. However, the Army Corps of Engineers opposed the idea of cutting a hole in the Mississippi River levee to drain the Braithwaite area, instead opting to bring in pumps to alleviate the flooding.

In addition to the physical damage, the storm also caused emotional distress for many residents, particularly those who had experienced similar trauma during Hurricane Katrina. One resident, Joey Amann, recalled the devastation of losing his home to Katrina in 2005 and expressed concern that the current storm was worse than anticipated. Another resident, Bill Boesch, reflected on the painful memories of Katrina and the sense of unease that came with waiting for the storm to pass.

As the storm continued to move inland, officials urged residents to stay indoors and avoid traveling unless necessary. The city of New Orleans implemented a dusk-to-dawn curfew, and police were on high alert for looters. Mayor Mitch Landrieu reassured residents that the drinking water was safe and the sanitary sewer system was functioning properly, but warned that the cleanup phase would be critical and cautioned residents to be cautious.

The storm's impact was also felt economically, with gas prices spiking by nearly 5 cents per gallon nationwide due to refinery shutdowns along the Gulf Coast. However, experts predicted that the price increase would be short-lived, as the storm's winds were not expected to cause lasting damage to the refineries.

As the storm finally began to subside, officials began to assess the full extent of the damage and launch recovery efforts. The National Weather Service warned that the storm's aftermath would pose a significant threat, with isolated tornadoes possible along the central Gulf Coast region and parts of the lower Mississippi Valley through Wednesday. The storm's legacy would be remembered as a Category 1 hurricane that packed a powerful punch, leaving a trail of destruction and heartbreak in its wake.

reference: – Today is the 7th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, and for residents of Plaquemines Parish, it's deja vu all over again, according to the parish president. Billy Nungesser tells NPR that the parish, located some 95 miles from New Orleans, has already seen damage that rivals, and could even surpass, that heaped upon it by Katrina. With the levee overrun, Nungesser says parts of the parish that had escaped unscathed in previous hurricanes now sit under five feet of water. "I don't know who's calling this a Category 1, but this is no Category 1," Nungesser said. "My house has more damage than it did during Katrina." More on the situation in Plaquemines, and beyond: The parish's levees range from 8.5 feet to 12 feet in height. The Army Corps of Engineers estimates the storm surge hit 12.5 feet, but have yet to confirm a breach. This implies the levees, which are not part of the federal levee system, failed, reports the Times-Picayune. While the director of Plaquemines Parish's emergency preparedness says winds may have hit 110mph, CNN reports that Isaac has weakened a bit since, with maximum sustained winds of 75mph (the hurricane threshold is 74mph). As of about 10:30am ET, Isaac's center was located about 50 miles south-southwest of New Orleans. The AP reports that 500,000 are without power in the areas around New Orleans. In Southern Mississippi, a tornado warning has been issued.

final_summary:
Apple has announced the availability of the white iPhone 4, which will be released tomorrow in 16- and 32-gigabyte versions for $199 and $299, respectively, with a two-year wireless contract through Apple, Verizon, AT&T, and authorized resellers. The announcement comes after months of anticipation and speculation among consumers.

In a statement, Philip Schiller, Apple's senior vice president of Worldwide Product Marketing, expressed appreciation for those who "waited patiently" for the release, acknowledging that some individuals doubted its existence. The white iPhone 4's launch date was previously reported by various outlets, including Best Buy, which listed it as April 27, and other publications that suggested it would appear by the end of April.

Separately, Apple addressed concerns over the collection and use of location information by iOS devices. The company clarified that it does not track the location of customers' iPhones and has never done so. However, Apple acknowledged that it maintains a crowd-sourced database of Wi-Fi hotspots and cell towers near a device's location, compiled from tens of millions of iPhones sending geo-tagged locations anonymously and encrypted.

According to Apple, the location data collected is not the actual location of the iPhone but rather the surrounding Wi-Fi hotspots and cell towers, which can be hundreds of miles away from the device. The company emphasized that it cannot identify the source of this data, suggesting that it is not directly tied to individual users.

Following reports of a potential security vulnerability, Apple admitted to two bugs related to location services. Firstly, the company stored the Wi-Fi hotspot and cell tower database on the iPhone for an extended period, which has since been recognized as a bug. Secondly, the iPhone continued to update its location data from Apple's crowd-sourced database even after users turned off Location Services.

To address these concerns, Apple plans to release a free iOS software update within the next few weeks. The update will reduce the size of the Wi-Fi hotspot and cell tower database cached on the iPhone, encrypt the data, and delete it entirely when Location Services is disabled by the user.

These measures come in response to a class-action lawsuit filed against Apple, alleging invasion of privacy and computer fraud due to the alleged secret recording of iPhone and iPad users' movements. Regulatory bodies in France, Germany, Italy, and South Korea have also initiated investigations into the matter. Apple's response to these allegations includes a comprehensive Q&A session addressing the collection and use of location data by iOS devices.

reference: – Apple finally responded to the accusations that it was tracking iPhone users’ locations today, saying it “has never done so and has no plans to ever do so.” In a statement, which you can read on TechCrunch, the company explained that it was actually keeping a log not of the phone’s location, but of the location of WiFi hotspots and cell towers, “some of which may be located more than one hundred miles away from your iPhone.” The data is used to more quickly triangulate users’ position when they use location-based services, and is sent to Apple anonymously. But the company did admit to some “bugs” in the software—like that it keeps collecting data even after you’ve turned Location Services off, and that it stored years of data, instead of the week's worth it needs—and promised an iOS update to fix them. And, just to add some positive news to the cycle, it also announced that the rumored white iPhone will be available tomorrow, Time reports.

final_summary:
Celebrity chef and television personality Anthony Bourdain, known for hosting CNN's food-and-travel focused "Parts Unknown" series, died by hanging in a hotel room near Strasbourg, France on June 8, 2018, at the age of 61. According to a French judicial official, a completed toxicology report revealed that Bourdain had no narcotics in his system at the time of his death. However, a trace of a non-narcotic medicine in a therapeutic dose and a small amount of alcohol were detected. 

Bourdain had been open about his struggles with substance abuse in the past, including a heroin and cocaine addiction in the 1980s. He had entered rehab and gotten clean in the early 1990s, but continued to drink socially throughout his life. In recent interviews, Bourdain stated that he never felt the need to drink excessively and only consumed alcohol when socializing.

The investigation into Bourdain's death found no evidence of foul play or violence, with a medical expert detecting no signs of trauma on his body. French prosecutor Christian de Rocquigny de Fayel confirmed that nothing suggested the involvement of a third person in Bourdain's death.

Bourdain was in France filming an upcoming episode of "Parts Unknown" with his friend, French-born chef Eric Ripert, at the time of his death. He had also been dating Italian actress Asia Argento, whom he met while filming an episode of the show in Rome in 2016. Argento paid tribute to Bourdain on social media, describing him as her "love, my rock, my protector" and expressing her devastation over his loss.

reference: – With French police calling Anthony Bourdain's death a suicide, only one investigative question remained: What substances were in his body? The answer: none, save a therapeutic dose of nonnarcotic medicine, a French prosecutor tells the New York Times. "No trace of narcotics. No trace of any toxic products," says the prosecutor, per Reuters. The late celebrity chef, 61, had talked openly about his past substance abuse but said he had cleaned up in rehab in the 1990s and drank without any problems since, per People. Now Bourdain's family will likely hold a private ceremony with his ashes, his mother Gladys Bourdain tells the Times. "He would want as little fuss as possible," she says. Gladys never much liked his tattoos, which reflected his culinary adventures, but she's decided to get one herself—the word "Tony" on her inner wrist in small letters. She plans to use her son's tattoo artist and says she won't get any other tattoos. As news broke Friday of the French toxicology test, Today reports, Italian actress Asia Argento posted an Instagram photo of the pair smiling in front of sun-drenched water: "Two weeks without you," she wrote. (Rose McGowan wants people to stop blaming Argento for his suicide.)

final_summary:
Bear, a 2-year-old black Labrador, is a unique canine asset in the fight against cybercrime. As one of only five dogs in the United States trained to detect electronic data devices, Bear has played a crucial role in several high-profile investigations, including the arrest of former Subway spokesperson Jared Fogle on child pornography charges. 

In July, Bear's keen sense of smell led investigators to a thumb drive at Fogle's Indiana residence, which had gone undetected by human searches. This discovery was significant, as Fogle later pleaded guilty to possessing explicit images of minors and paying for sex with underage girls. 

Steven DeBrota, a prosecutor from the U.S. Attorney's office in Indianapolis, initially expressed skepticism about Bear's abilities but acknowledged their effectiveness after witnessing the dog's performance firsthand. DeBrota described Bear as a vital member of the investigative team.

Todd Jordan, Bear's handler and trainer, demonstrated the dog's capabilities by guiding him through an apartment while issuing the command "Seek!" Bear quickly located a kitchen drawer containing a device, earning praise and rewards from Jordan. 

Jordan revealed that Bear has participated in four previous investigations, including the recent arrest of Olympics gymnastics coach Marvin Sharp. The dog's exceptional skills have also caught the attention of the Seattle Police Department, which has acquired Bear to aid in internet crime investigations. 

As Bear prepares to relocate to Seattle, Jordan reflected on the emotional aspect of parting ways with his trusted companion, acknowledging that it will be "bittersweet" despite the rewarding nature of their work together. Jordan has been training dogs for over a year and has sold some of his trained canines to law enforcement agencies. Currently, he is training two additional dogs to join Bear in detecting electronic devices.

reference: – There are only five dogs in America trained to sniff out electronic devices, and one of them played a major role in Jared Fogle's downfall. Bear, a 2-year-old black Labrador, was able to sniff out a thumb drive that human investigators missed during a search of the former Subway spokesman's home last month, reports NBC News, which describes Bear as a "porn-sniffing dog." He can detect tiny memory cards easily and was trained with methods associated with "any other K-9 training, like with the narcotics or explosives or anything," his trainer tells WISH-TV. "You get the dog used to the odor and reward them as they indicate on it." The lead prosecutor on the Fogle case tells NBC that Bear is a "key part of the team," though he doubted the pooch's abilities at first. "I thought I was being punked, but it does work," he says. The evidence, along with that gathered by an FBI informant, helped secure Fogle's guilty plea on child-porn and underage sex charges. Bear has been a busy dog: NBC reports that he also played a role in the arrest of Olympic gymnastics coach Marvin Sharp. Bear's trainer, a deputy fire chief, sells dogs to law enforcement agencies when their training is completed, and Bear will soon be on his way to Seattle, where the police department contacted the trainer after hearing about the Fogle case. (Fogle's charitable foundation appears to have been a sham.)

final_summary:
Paige Warner, an 11-year-old girl from Roseville, California, tragically lost her life after suffering a severe allergic reaction during a family Thanksgiving trip to Hawaii. Paige, who had pre-existing food allergies and sensitivities to environmental conditions, had been enjoying her time in Hawaii with her family, particularly her excitement to experience the island for the first time. However, approximately 10-15 minutes after swimming in a pool with her sister Violet and a friend, Paige began experiencing symptoms of an allergic reaction, complaining of a burning sensation in her nose and swelling in her throat.

Her mother, Karin Carpenter, quickly recognized the severity of the situation and called 911. An EMT present at the scene administered Benadryl and epinephrine, but Paige's condition rapidly deteriorated, leading to cardiac arrest. Despite receiving CPR for over 20 minutes, Paige's heart eventually restarted, and she was rushed to Kapiolani Children's Hospital. Upon arrival, medical staff struggled to insert a breathing tube due to her constricted airways, and she remained unresponsive throughout her treatment.

Paige was placed on life support and cooled to 34 degrees Celsius using a specialized blanket to minimize brain damage. Her family, including her parents, grandparents, and biological father, maintained vigilance at her bedside, often going without rest to care for her. Over the next three days, Paige underwent multiple MRIs, showing significant brain damage, including to the brainstem controlling vital functions. As her condition worsened, her family held onto hope for her recovery, particularly her ability to regain artistic expression through drawing, a passion that defined her personality.

However, on December 4th, Paige failed to respond to further testing, and her doctors ultimately declared her brain activity non-existent, leading to her being medically pronounced dead at 9:25 a.m. Paige's family spent the following seven hours with her, holding and hugging her without medical equipment, cherishing the time they had left with their beloved daughter.

reference: – On Thanksgiving Day, Paige Warner and her family flew to Honolulu for the 11-year-old's first trip to Hawaii. She "was so excited," recounts mom Karin Carpenter, who explains that after a holiday dinner of white rice and soy sauce—Paige had "extensive" food allergies—her daughter got into the pool with her sister. Fewer than 15 minutes later, she was out of the pool, complaining of a stinging nose. Her condition quickly snowballed into something much more serious, involving difficulty breathing, and she was taken to the ER. On Dec. 4, after a week in a coma, the girl from Roseville, Calif., was taken off life support; it's still unclear what triggered the reaction, reports KTLA. As Carpenter writes on a GoFundMe page, she speculates about possible contributing factors: the plane, entering an environment full of allergens that were new to her, the area's large feral cat population, pesticide runoff in the pool from an adjacent golf course, or a bronchial spasm caused by the pool's temperature. The family is "devastated," but sister Violet is bringing healing, insisting that the family hang its Christmas decorations because Paige "loves Christmas." The GoFundMe campaign has raised more than $30,000 to date. The most heart-tugging line on the page, from Carpenter's description of the events immediately after Paige got out of the pool: "She looked into my eyes and screamed, and said, 'Mom, I'm dying. I know I am because I can see it in your eyes.'" (A teen's allergic reaction recently caused her to "burn from the inside out.")

final_summary:
Alessandra Perez, a 6-year-old girl who often stayed at her grandparents' home after school, witnessed disturbing scenes of abuse against her cousins, 10-year-old twins Victor and Nubia Doctor, who were adopted by Jorge and Carmen Barahona. She observed the twins being locked in a bathroom with a shower curtain taped to the walls to prevent escape, and on other occasions, being forced to stand in garbage bins. Alessandra was instructed to keep these incidents secret, but eventually confided in a therapist, prompting a brief child welfare investigation.

However, the investigation was short-lived, and it wasn't until Monday morning that the full extent of the abuse came to light. A Florida Department of Transportation employee spotted Jorge Barahona's red pickup truck parked on the side of Interstate 95 in West Palm Beach, with Victor, severely burned and disoriented, sitting next to a gas can. Unconscious and covered in chemicals, Barahona lay on the ground nearby. As emergency responders arrived, they discovered the body of Nubia, wrapped in a plastic bag, in the back of the truck.

Victor was rushed to the hospital, where doctors diagnosed him with severe burns to his abdomen, upper thighs, and buttocks, as well as prior injuries, including a fractured clavicle, a broken arm, scarring to his buttocks and lower abdomen, and ligature marks on both wrists. Despite receiving intensive treatment, Victor's condition worsened, with doctors struggling to treat him due to the unidentified chemical used in the attack. He was subsequently transferred to a specialized burn unit at Jackson Memorial Hospital in Miami.

An investigation revealed that Jorge Barahona had attempted to kill himself by pouring gasoline over his head, but abandoned the plan when he realized Victor was beside him. Barahona claimed he had given Victor sleeping pills to make him sleep, but police found no evidence of gasoline on the boy, only another unidentified chemical that had caused his severe burns. The police affidavit stated that Barahona's account of events did not match the severity of Victor's injuries.

As the investigation unfolded, it emerged that the Barahonas had a history of allegations of abuse and neglect. The Florida Department of Children and Families had previously investigated the couple on three occasions, with the most recent case beginning just four days before the discovery of the twins' abuse. A teacher had reported concerns about the twins being bound and forced to stand in a bathtub, and a subsequent investigation implicated Carmen Barahona in Nubia's disappearance.

In a dramatic hearing, a judge ordered the removal of the two other children living with the Barahonas – a 7-year-old girl and an 11-year-old boy – to a therapeutic foster home, citing concerns that those who knew about the abuse should not have contact with the children. Carmen Barahona appeared in court, shielding her face with a sheet of paper, and requested that the children be placed with a family friend, but the request was denied.

reference: – It's one of the more horrific allegations of a child abuse to come along for some time: Florida police found the corpse of a 10-year-old girl, covered in acid, in the back of her adopted father's truck. Her twin brother was in the front seat, nearly overcome by toxic fumes, and is recovering in intensive care from previous injuries including a broken arm and collar bone. Their adopted father, 53-year-old Jorge Barahona, was found outside the truck and has been charged with aggravated child abuse for now, reports CNN. The story coming together suggests the twins were adopted by Jorge and his wife and subjected to systemic abuse, including being bound by their hands and feet and forced to stay in the bathroom, reports the Miami Herald. State authorities had two previous reports of something amiss, including one on Feb. 10, but took no action against the parents. A heart-wrenching detail from the Herald: An investigation finally began when the couple's biological granddaughter told her therapist she wanted to break open her piggy bank to help the twins escape. Click for more.

final_summary:
A 19-year-old Chicago man, Adel Daoud, is facing charges of attempting to detonate a car bomb outside a downtown Chicago bar in September 2012. The incident occurred just four days before the expiration of the FISA Amendments Act, a sweeping government surveillance law that allows the National Security Agency to intercept foreign communications without an individual warrant. 

Sen. Dianne Feinstein, chairwoman of the Senate Intelligence Committee, cited the alleged plot as an example of the law's effectiveness in preventing terrorist attacks during a speech on the Senate floor in December 2012. However, when asked if the law was used to gather evidence against Daoud, the government refused to confirm or deny its involvement.

Daoud's lawyers argue that the government is withholding this information to avoid a constitutional challenge to the FISA Amendments Act, specifically Section 702, which permits the interception of foreign communications without a warrant. This section has been criticized for its breadth and lack of individual warrants, allowing the government to collect vast amounts of data on individuals, including those within the US.

In a motion filed by Daoud's lawyers, they claim that the government is being disingenuous in stating that the evidence against their client was gathered through a traditional FISA court order, rather than under Section 702. They point out that Sen. Feinstein's statement in 2012 suggests that the government did indeed use the FISA Amendments Act to gather evidence in the case.

The government's refusal to acknowledge its use of Section 702 has raised concerns among civil liberties groups, who argue that the law is unconstitutional and should be subject to judicial review. The American Civil Liberties Union has stated that the government's actions amount to a "game of three-card monte" with the privacy rights of millions of Americans.

Daoud's case highlights the tension between national security interests and individual rights, as well as the secrecy surrounding the government's surveillance programs. His trial is scheduled for February, and his lawyers will continue to push for transparency and accountability from the government.

The government's refusal to acknowledge its use of Section 702 has raised concerns among civil liberties groups, who argue that the law is unconstitutional and should be subject to judicial review. The American Civil Liberties Union has stated that the government's actions amount to a "game of three-card monte" with the privacy rights of millions of Americans.

Daoud's case highlights the tension between national security interests and individual rights, as well as the secrecy surrounding the government's surveillance programs. His trial is scheduled for February, and his lawyers will continue to push for transparency and accountability from the government.

reference: – Adel Daoud is a Chicago 19-year-old accused by the feds of trying to detonate a bomb outside a Chicago bar in 2012. His case, however, could serve as the first constitutional challenge to the government's sweeping surveillance techniques, reports the Washington Post. In a court filing yesterday, attorneys for the terror suspect demanded to know whether federal authorities used their expanded powers under a 2008 amendment to the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act to nab him. “Whenever it is good for the government to brag about its success, it speaks loudly and publicly,” they wrote, as per the Chicago Tribune. "When a criminal defendant’s constitutional rights are at stake, however, it quickly and unequivocally clams up under the guise of State Secrets.” Federal prosecutors say they're not obligated to say whether they used the law, and a judge will have the final say, reports WLS-TV. So did they use it? Here's a hint: Last year, when the Senate was debating whether to renew the 2008 law—it's formally known as the FISA Amendments Act—Dianne Feinstein made her case in favor by asserting that it had helped stop "a plot to bomb a downtown Chicago bar." If the judge rules that the government must acknowledge it used the FAA, Daoud's attorney's would then be able to challenge its constitutionality.

final_summary:
In a bold attempt to curb unruly behavior among tourists, Mayor Dario Nardella of Florence announced an innovative initiative to discourage visitors from eating and lounging on the steps of the city's historic churches. Starting on May 31st, 2017, city authorities began hosing down the steps of the Basilica of Santa Croce and the Chiesa di Santo Spirito during lunchtime, aiming to create an unpleasant experience for those who refuse to respect the city's cultural heritage. According to Nardella, the goal is not to target tourists in general, but rather to address the issue of disrespect shown by a minority of visitors who fail to follow basic rules of decorum.

The mayor emphasized that the city is not opposed to tourists, acknowledging that they bring significant revenue to the city, but rather seeks to maintain a balance between welcoming visitors and preserving the integrity of its UNESCO World Heritage Site. Nardella specifically targeted day-trippers, including those on cruise itineraries, who often spend only a few hours in the city and contribute to the problem of litter and disrespect for local customs.

To tackle the issue, the city has already implemented various measures, including regulations requiring restaurants in the historic center to serve food that is at least 70% locally sourced. Additionally, Nardella blocked a proposal by McDonald's to open a restaurant in the main square, following a successful social media campaign by locals. The city has also taken steps to reduce overcrowding, such as promoting lesser-explored areas and limiting tourist accommodations.

While some critics argue that the hosing-down initiative may deter tourists from visiting the city, others see it as a necessary measure to preserve the beauty and dignity of Florence's historic sites. As the peak tourist season approaches, the city is bracing itself for an influx of visitors, and Nardella's unorthodox solution aims to strike a delicate balance between accommodating tourists and protecting the city's cultural identity.

reference: – The mayor of Florence says some tourists have no "decorum" these days—something he hopes to fix with a water hose. Mayor Dario Nardella says a small number of the Italian city's 12 million annual tourists—particularly day-trippers from cruise ships—have taken to "camping out" on church steps at lunchtime. They "sit down on church steps, eat their food, and leave rubbish strewn on them," with no respect for "our cultural heritage," Nardella tells the Guardian. He says city officials thought about handing out fines to such tourists, but they decided instead to get creative. The plan: Use water hoses to soak church steps. That way, if tourists sit down, "they'll get wet," Nardella said in announcing the strategy Wednesday. Shortly after the announcement, the steps of both the Basilica of Santa Croce and the Chiesa di Santo Spirito were doused. In the future, this will be a daily occurrence, reports the Telegraph. "Instead of imposing fines, we thought this measure was more elegant," Nardella tells the Guardian, adding other areas might eventually get a wash as well. While some social media users joked that a free shower might be nice in the heat of summer, others have accused city officials of being "elitist" in targeting tourists who choose not to eat in Florence's restaurants, per the Local. Nardella counters that the city has plenty of outdoor seating for tourists. Churches, however, are no place for a picnic, he says. (Barcelona has an issue with its tourists, too.)

final_summary:
A groundbreaking study published in the journal Psychological Science has revealed a profound link between human emotions and color perception. Researchers from the University of Rochester conducted two experiments to investigate whether emotions influence color perception, specifically focusing on the impact of sadness on color identification. The study's lead author, Christopher Thorstenson, a psychologist at the University of Rochester, explained that the idea behind the research stemmed from the observation that people frequently use color terms to describe their emotions, suggesting a potential connection between mood and color perception.

In the first experiment, 129 college students were randomly assigned to watch either a sad or an amusing film clip. After viewing the clips, participants underwent an emotional evaluation and were then tasked with identifying desaturated colors on a computer screen. The results showed that individuals who watched the sad clip struggled to distinguish between shades along the blue-yellow color axis, whereas those who watched the amusing clip performed accurately. Notably, the sad participants' color perception remained unaffected along the red-green axis.

To confirm the findings, the researchers conducted a second experiment, where participants watched either a neutral clip or a sad clip, and their color perception was assessed. Similar to the first experiment, the results demonstrated that sadness impaired color perception along the blue-yellow axis, but not along the red-green axis. This consistent pattern across both experiments suggests that sadness specifically affects the processing of blue and yellow colors.

Thorstenson speculated that dopamine deficiency might contribute to the observed impairment in blue-yellow color perception, citing previous research linking dopamine to clinical disorders affecting color perception. However, the study did not directly measure dopamine levels, leaving this hypothesis open to further investigation.

The study's findings have significant implications for our understanding of the complex relationship between emotions, cognition, and perception. According to Thorstenson, "our results show that mood and emotion can affect how we see the world around us." This "top-down" effect, where higher-level cognition influences perception, challenges the traditional view of perception as a purely bottom-up process. The study's results join a growing body of evidence suggesting that emotions can shape our subjective experience of reality, making the world appear more or less vibrant based on our emotional state.

The researchers' work builds upon previous studies demonstrating the impact of color on cognition and behavior, such as the finding that exposure to the color red can impair performance on challenging tasks. By exploring the reciprocal relationship between emotions and color perception, the study sheds light on the intricate mechanisms underlying human perception and cognition.

reference: – Got the blues? You probably aren't seeing blues clearly. That's the takeaway from a new study that finds how a person views the color blue may actually depend on mood, reports Medical Daily. Not all colors were affected in the same way. Researchers at the University of Rochester asked 129 college students to watch either a stand-up comedy clip or the scene from The Lion King in which Mufasa dies, then recorded the participants' moods, per the Washington Post. Next, participants took a color accuracy test in which they had to look at 48 color patches that had been desaturated to look nearly gray, then identify whether each was red, yellow, green, or blue. People who were feeling down in the dumps performed worse than those who were amused. In other words, the data supports "the conventional wisdom that people's emotions influence how colorful the world looks to them," the researchers say. However, the sad group performed poorly only when quizzed about colors on what researchers call the blue-yellow axis. They could identify colors on the red-green axis just as well as others—perhaps due to the evolutionary need to see red as a sign of anger, reports Time. The results are "a reminder that our experience of the world is not as immediate and objective as we’d like to believe," observes the Post. Researchers suggest a sad mood messes up the ability of the neuron transmitter dopamine to process information about blue-yellow colors. (This woman sees 100 times more colors than you.)

final_summary:
On October 19, 2018, CNN White House correspondent Jim Acosta tweeted from a Trump campaign rally in Missoula, Montana, expressing frustration over the loud music being played, which made it difficult for him to conduct live TV coverage. He wondered if the high volume posed a security concern for the Secret Service or local law enforcement. Justin Caporale, a GOP consultant and former aide to First Lady Melania Trump, responded with a mocking tweet, "Dear Diary...". 

This exchange caught Acosta's attention, prompting him to send a direct message to Caporale with a profanity-laced reply, "F**k you." However, unbeknownst to Acosta, Caporale had shared their conversation with Daily Caller reporter Peter Hasson, who obtained screenshots of the direct message and confirmed its authenticity.

The incident sparked ridicule from conservative commentators, with many taking aim at Acosta's outburst. In response, Acosta apologized to Caporale, claiming he mistakenly believed the latter was an "old friend from the campaign days" and offering a conciliatory message. Despite the apology, Acosta proceeded to block Caporale on Twitter.

reference: – One of the reporters who tangles publicly with President Trump the most is himself in a bit of hot water over a tweet. Jim Acosta of CNN has apologized after tweeting "f--- you" (except he spelled it out) to a critic, reports Mediaite. Acosta also maintains that he mistook the person as an old friend, but not everyone is buying that. It all started Thursday night when Acosta tweeted a complaint that the Trump campaign turned up the music so loudly at a Trump rally that it was difficult to work—the implication being that it was deliberate. At that point, a former aide for Melania Trump, Justin Caporale, mocked Acosta with a tweet reading, "Dear Diary ..." That's when Acosta sent his controversial response—he did so in a private direct message, but Caporale then made it public. Acosta also blocked Caporale. As online criticism grew, and Caporale demanded an apology, Acosta responded: "Hey buddy I thought you were an old friend from the campaign days. I’m so sorry. Hope I didn’t offend you. Have a good night and take care." As the Wrap notes, the apology seems a bit odd given that Acosta also blocked Caporale, raising the question of why he would block a friend. Neither Acosta nor CNN has commented further. (Acosta also once tangled memorably with White House aide Stephen Miller.)

final_summary:
A devastating incident occurred on August 7, 2016, at the Schlitterbahn Waterpark in Kansas City, Kansas, resulting in the tragic death of 10-year-old Caleb Thomas Schwab, the son of Kansas state Representative Scott Schwab. Caleb was decapitated while riding the Verrückt waterslide, a 168-foot-tall attraction that had been operational for only two years. Two other women who were riding in the same raft as Caleb sustained facial injuries.

The Verrückt waterslide, which translates to "insane" in German, featured multi-person rafts that made a 17-story drop at speeds of up to 70 mph, followed by a surge up a hump and a 50-foot descent to a finishing pool. Riders were required to be at least 54 inches tall and were harnessed with two nylon seatbelt-like straps, which were secured with fabric fasteners instead of traditional buckles. Each strap was held in place by long straps that closed with fabric fasteners, and riders held ropes inside the raft. Additionally, riders were weighed to ensure each raft carried between 400 and 550 pounds.

Following the incident, Schlitterbahn Waterparks and Resorts issued a statement expressing their condolences and commitment to the safety of their staff and guests. They announced that the Verrückt waterslide would be permanently decommissioned and removed from the tower once the investigation is concluded and they receive permission from the court. This decision was seen as the only proper course of action following the tragedy.

Schlitterbahn has been cooperating with investigators and the victims' families and attorneys, stating that the safety of their staff and guests is their top priority. The company has also acknowledged that they have been heartbroken over the tragedy and have never experienced an incident of this magnitude in their 50 years of operating the waterpark.

The investigation into the incident is ongoing, with a homicide detective still examining the ride's rafts, netting, and metal frames. While criminal charges are unlikely, the case remains open. The possibility of a lawsuit or settlement is also being considered, with a limit on personal injury and wrongful death cases in Kansas set at $300,000 and $250,000, respectively. However, as Schlitterbahn is a Texas-based company, lawyers may choose to pursue legal proceedings in Texas.

Independent investigations are also underway, with a team of attorneys and experts inspecting the slide and gathering evidence. Personal injury attorney Matt Meyerkord explained that such investigations involve a thorough examination of every aspect of the ride, including testing and analysis of its components. The outcome of these investigations and potential litigation may ultimately determine what led to the tragic incident on the Verrückt waterslide.

reference: – The 10-year-old son of a Kansas lawmaker died on the world's tallest water slide this summer, and that ride is now closed for good. Caleb Schwab, the son of state Rep. Scott Schwab, suffered a "fatal neck injury" Aug. 7 on the 168-foot Verruckt slide at the Schlitterbahn Kansas City water park; the slide has been closed as investigators look into Caleb's death, the Kansas City Star reports. Once the probe is done and the park gets the legal go-ahead, the slide will be "decommissioned," with the ride permanently shuttered and the slide taken down from its tower, per a Schlitterbahn statement released Tuesday. "In our opinion, it is the only proper course of action following this tragedy," the park says, adding it remains "heartbroken" over Caleb's death. It's not clear whether the Schwab family will sue or settle with the park, or if criminal charges will be filed, though the Kansas City PD tells KSHB charges aren't likely. In Kansas, permanent amusement park rides have to undergo yearly inspections, but they can be done by private firms rather than the state, the AP reports. In Schlitterbahn's case, all of its rides received a thumbs-up in June after they received private inspections. But some people who had ridden the slide, operational since 2014, spoke out after Caleb's death and noted they'd had uneasy experiences on the ride, including shoulder harnesses coming loose and rafts flying into the air when they shouldn't. The park notes in its release that it will eventually announce what attraction will be built in Verruckt's place.

final_summary:
A long-standing physics problem has been revisited by a graduate student at McGill University in Montreal, Canada, yielding a revised estimate for the time it would take to fall through the center of the Earth. The traditional answer, widely taught in introductory physics courses, suggests it would take approximately 42 minutes to complete the journey. However, Alexander Klotz's more realistic analysis, published in the American Journal of Physics, puts the estimated time at 38 minutes and 11 seconds.

Klotz's study relies on seismic data to determine the varying density of the Earth's interior, rather than the commonly used assumption of a uniform density of around 5500 kilograms per cubic meter. According to the Preliminary Reference Earth Model, the actual density of the Earth ranges from less than 1000 kilograms per cubic meter at the surface to approximately 13,000 kilograms per cubic meter at the center of the core. By incorporating these variations in density, Klotz's calculations show that the force of gravity on an object falling through the Earth would change significantly, resulting in a shorter travel time.

Interestingly, Klotz discovered that even a simplified assumption – that the force of gravity remains constant throughout the journey – yields nearly the same result, with an estimated time of 38 minutes. This unexpected finding arises from the specific mass distribution within the Earth, where the force of gravity remains relatively consistent until reaching the outer core, after which it diminishes rapidly. As the object approaches the center of the Earth, its velocity becomes high enough that it spends minimal time in the core, making the constant-gravity approximation sufficient.

David Jackson, a physicist at Dickinson College and editor of the American Journal of Physics, praises Klotz's work, highlighting its ability to demonstrate the complexities of real-world physics problems. The study provides a valuable insight into the importance of considering the intricacies of the Earth's internal structure when solving seemingly straightforward problems.

reference: – It's a common hypothetical in physics classrooms: How long would it take to fall through a tunnel from one side of the Earth to the other? The new answer: 38 minutes and 11 seconds, reports Science. The result is courtesy of McGill University graduate student Alexander Klotz, whose calculations take four minutes off the previously accepted answer and appear in the American Journal of Physics. The reason for the shorter time? Unlike previous calculations, Klotz's take into account that the Earth's layers have different densities, and those differences would affect the hypothetical falling man, notes Phys.org. "The way the Earth is structured, the gravity increases slightly as you go deeper towards the dense core, to about 110% of its surface value, before getting weaker as you move through the core, reaching zero at the center,” says Klotz, as quoted in Forbes. (If it's all a little too hypothetical, try physics-inspired pasta instead.)

final_summary:
In a heated debate at the Orange County Convention Center in Orlando, Florida, Republican presidential hopefuls Mitt Romney and Rick Perry clashed over various issues, including immigration, healthcare, and entitlements. The debate, sponsored by Fox News, Google, and the Republican Party of Florida, saw the two frontrunners engaging in a series of tense exchanges, with Romney accusing Perry of flip-flopping on several key issues. 

Perry, who has been leading in national opinion polls, faced intense scrutiny over his stance on immigration, particularly his support for a Texas law that allows children of undocumented immigrants to receive in-state tuition rates at public colleges and universities. Romney criticized the policy, calling it a "magnet" that attracts people to the US for educational benefits. Perry, however, defended the policy, stating that it was unfair to punish children who entered the country through no fault of their own. 

The debate also saw Romney and Perry trade barbs over their respective stances on Social Security and healthcare. Romney accused Perry of advocating for privatization of Social Security, citing a passage from Perry's book "Fed Up!" Perry denied the accusation, claiming that he had never supported privatization. Romney also accused Perry of flip-flopping on healthcare, pointing out that Perry had previously praised Romney's Massachusetts healthcare plan, known as Romneycare, in his book "No Apologies". 

Perry shot back, accusing Romney of editing his book to remove passages that contradicted his current stance on healthcare. The exchange highlighted the tension between the two candidates, with Romney seeking to portray Perry as inconsistent and Perry attempting to paint Romney as a flip-flopper. 

Other candidates on stage, including Michele Bachmann, Rick Santorum, and Newt Gingrich, also weighed in on various issues, including the economy and gun rights. However, the focus remained firmly on the Romney-Perry showdown, with the two candidates dominating the debate and overshadowing their fellow contenders. 

Despite the intensity of the debate, it remains unclear whether Perry's efforts to knock Romney off his stride were successful. While Perry may have gained some ground, Romney emerged relatively unscathed, maintaining his reputation as a consistent and reliable conservative. The debate marked the third confrontation between the two frontrunners in as many weeks, with the stakes continuing to rise as the Republican primary season heats up.
