reference: – Unless a surprise challenger emerges at the last minute, the race to replace Eric Cantor as the House's second-ranking Republican is already over. House Rules Committee chief Pete Sessions says he has dropped his bid to become majority leader "after thoughtful consideration and discussion" with colleagues, leaving Majority Whip Kevin McCarthy as the only lawmaker known to be seeking the position, reports Reuters. Cantor—the first majority leader ever to lose a primary—is stepping down at the end of July following his shock defeat at the hands of little-known Tea Party challenger David Brat. "It became obvious to me that the measures necessary to run a successful campaign would have created unnecessary and painful division within our party," Sessions said. The leadership election will be held by secret ballot next week but the more competitive race is now the one to succeed McCarthy as whip, the Hill finds. McCarthy's chief deputy, Rep. Peter Roskam, is in the running, along with Rep. Steve Scalise, chairman of the conservative Republican Study Committee, and Rep. Marlin Stutzman, a hardline conservative who was punished last year for defying House leaders, the Indianapolis Star notes.

final_summary:
Former Speaker of the House Newt Gingrich has been wrapping himself in the mantle of Ronald Reagan, claiming to have worked with the president to defeat communism and the Soviet empire. However, this portrayal is misleading, as Gingrich frequently criticized Reagan, his top aides, and their policies during the Reagan years. Despite voting with the president regularly, Gingrich's rhetoric was consistently negative, and he was proven wrong in his predictions that Reagan's policies would fail.

During the battles against Soviet expansionism in the Third World, including in Angola, Grenada, Afghanistan, and Central America, Reagan's top team, including William Casey, Caspar Weinberger, and George Shultz, understood the importance of dealing a significant blow to the Soviet empire. While few officials predicted the complete collapse of the Soviet Union, they recognized the significance of the struggle. In contrast, Gingrich's criticisms of Reagan's efforts were vocal and persistent.

On Capitol Hill, the battle against Soviet expansionism was particularly contentious, with leading Democrats such as Ted Kennedy, Christopher Dodd, Jim Wright, and Tip O'Neill using various tactics to undermine Reagan's initiatives. Meanwhile, Republicans like Henry Hyde, Dick Cheney, Dan Burton, Connie Mack, and Tom DeLay stood in support of the president's efforts. Gingrich, however, voted with the caucus but chose to attack Reagan instead of working with him.

A notable example of Gingrich's criticism came in a floor statement on March 21, 1986, during the fight over funding for the Nicaraguan contras. Gingrich declared that Reagan's administration was failing to effectively counter the Soviet empire and that the president's policies were weak and inadequate. He also accused the CIA, State Department, and Defense Department of lacking strategies to defeat the empire.

Former Speaker of the House Tom DeLay has publicly stated that Gingrich is not a true conservative, comparing him to Bill Clinton. DeLay notes that Gingrich has a history of making promises he fails to keep and has a tendency to shift his positions to suit his audience. Gingrich's critics argue that he lacks the charm and charisma of Clinton, but shares his narcissistic tendencies and a penchant for controversy.

Gingrich's presidential campaign has been marked by a series of gaffes and controversies, including his criticism of Paul Ryan's budget proposal as "right-wing social engineering" and his subsequent reversal on the issue. He has also been accused of hypocrisy for advocating for a guest worker program that would allow undocumented immigrants to work in the US while simultaneously opposing amnesty for those already living in the country.

In contrast to Gingrich, Mitt Romney has a track record of consistency on conservative issues, including his opposition to abortion, support for entitlement reform, and pledge to repeal Obamacare. Romney has also been praised by conservative commentators such as John Bolton and Larry Kudlow for his fiscal conservatism and commitment to reducing government spending.

Ann Coulter has been a vocal critic of Gingrich, labeling him a "Clintonian fraud" and warning that re-electing Obama would be preferable to nominating Gingrich. Other conservative commentators, including the editors of the National Review and Bob Dole, have also expressed concerns about Gingrich's candidacy, citing his lack of discipline and his tendency to alienate potential allies.

Overall, Gingrich's record and behavior have raised serious questions about his fitness for the presidency, and his critics argue that he lacks the integrity and character necessary to lead the country.

reference: – The conservative media have apparently figured out that Newt Gingrich could actually win the Republican nomination, and they are responding with a breathtaking collective attack on the candidate. Politico rounds up the highlights, noting that "this is just a sampling of what's hitting Newt." In the National Review, Elliott Abrams writes that Gingrich often undermined Ronald Reagan, in what Politico calls "a devastating takedown." In another recent National Review piece being circulated by conservatives, the editors ramp up their opposition to Gingrich and urge conservatives not to support him. R. Emmett Tyrrell Jr. of the American Spectator compares Gingrich to Bill Clinton, calling them both "1960s generation narcissists" and noting that, for "girl hopping" Gingrich, "there are private encounters with the fair sex that doubtless will come out." On her website, Ann Coulter minces no words: "Re-elect Obama, Vote Newt!" Her column blasts Gingrich's "hotheaded arrogance." The Drudge Report linked to the Abrams piece with a banner reading, "Insider: Gingrich Repeatedly Insulted Reagan." The site, which has been viewed as pro-Romney in the past, also linked prominently to Tyrrell and Coulter. Tom DeLay told KTRH, "He’s not really a conservative. I mean, he’ll tell you what you want to hear. … When he was speaker, he was erratic, undisciplined." A top figure in the conservative media tells Politico that Republican leaders and media are beginning to realize, "It could happen, and it would be a disaster. All of us who were around and saw how he operated as speaker—there’s no one who’s not appalled by the prospect of what could happen." Politico notes that dozens of GOP lawmakers, governors, and other VIPs have said the same, privately, in recent weeks. Click for more on why tonight's debate is crucial for Gingrich.

final_summary:
A controversy has erupted over a "Win an Inn" essay contest organized by Janice Sage, the retiring owner of the 210-year-old Center Lovell Inn in Maine. The contest, which drew thousands of entrants from across the country, awarded the inn to Prince and Rose Adams, a couple from the US Virgin Islands who run a Caribbean-style restaurant on St. John. According to the contest rules, the winner would be chosen by an independent panel of local judges based on a 200-word essay submission accompanied by a $125 entry fee.

However, numerous unsuccessful contestants have come forward alleging that the contest was unfairly rigged in favor of the Adamses, citing deceptive advertising practices. The group, known as the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, claims that Sage misrepresented the contest as a fair competition when, in reality, she allegedly favored business owners. This assertion is supported by the fact that Sage herself won the inn in a similar contest 22 years ago after submitting a winning essay.

Maine State Police are currently investigating the matter to determine whether the contest violated state law on games of skill, which prohibits operators from partially or completely controlling the outcome. The police investigation is focused specifically on this aspect of the law and does not extend to potential federal laws or civil liabilities. Despite receiving fewer than the targeted 7,500 entries, Sage reportedly secured enough funds for retirement through the contest, which required a $125 entry fee from each participant.

Janice Sage, the contest organizer, declined to comment on the allegations, as did the Adamses, the winners of the contest. However, Kelley Prass Collins, founder of the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, expressed outrage over the situation, stating that Sage's actions were tantamount to coercing thousands of people into entering a contest they had little chance of winning. The investigation is expected to conclude within a week, and the contestants are advised to seek private counsel to explore further action.

reference: – Maine State Police are investigating whether an innkeeper violated state law in an essay contest with her 210-year-old country inn as the prize. Maine Department of Public Safety spokesman Steve McCausland said today an investigation was opened into whether the "Win an Inn" contest violated laws governing games of chance. The Boston Globe first reported that some contest losers felt the odds were stacked against them, contending that Center Lovell Inn owner Janice Sage marketed the contest to "dreamers" but instead awarded the prize to a couple with hospitality business experience in the Virgin Islands. "One of the many allegations against Janice Sage and the contest she sponsored is that the advertising of the contest ... was illegally deceptive and violated consumer-rights regulations, intentionally coercing thousands of people to enter a contest that they never had an actual chance of winning," says the founder of the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, a group made up of dozens of unhappy contestants. The entry fee was $125. Sage told the Globe she received fewer than the 7,500 entries she sought, but it was still enough to fund her retirement.

final_summary:
The United States and China, the world's two largest economies and emitters of greenhouse gases, have made a joint commitment to tackle climate change by setting ambitious targets to reduce their carbon emissions. According to a joint announcement by Presidents Barack Obama and Xi Jinping, the two countries aim to address the pressing issue collectively, acknowledging that neither nation can single-handedly resolve the problem. Despite accounting for approximately 40% of global emissions, the US and China recognize that their combined efforts are crucial to making a meaningful impact.

The US plans to decrease its net greenhouse gas emissions by 26-28% below 2005 levels by 2025, doubling the pace of carbon reductions compared to the previous decade. This target is based on an extensive analysis of emission-reducing potential across various sectors, with projected benefits for public health, clean air, and energy security. Building upon the Obama administration's 2009 pledge to lower emissions by 17% by 2020, the US is on track to meet this goal while fostering job creation and economic growth through the expansion of the clean energy sector.

China, meanwhile, has announced a landmark commitment to peak its carbon dioxide emissions around 2030, with the possibility of reaching this milestone earlier. This represents a significant shift, given that China has been responsible for approximately 60% of global carbon dioxide emissions growth over the past 15 years. To achieve this goal, China aims to increase the proportion of its energy consumption derived from non-polluting sources, such as renewables and nuclear power, to around 20% by 2030. This will necessitate the development of an additional 800-1000 gigawatts of new power generation capacity from sources like wind, solar, and nuclear energy by 2030, equivalent to the current capacity of all coal-fired power plants in China or nearly the entire electricity generation capacity of the US.

By working together, the US and China hope to inspire other nations to adopt similarly ambitious targets, ultimately contributing to a robust global climate agreement in 2015. Their joint commitment serves as a pivotal moment in the US-China relationship, marking a collaborative effort to address the pressing issue of climate change and pave the way for a more sustainable future.

reference: – The US and China rank as the world's two largest polluters, but a surprise deal is turning them into the world's two largest pollution fighters: The nations announced that they'll be working in tandem on long-term greenhouse-gas controls that would cut emissions by nearly a third over the next 15 years or so, CNN reports. "As the world's two largest economies, energy consumers, and emitters of greenhouse gases, we have a special responsibility to lead the global effort against climate change," President Obama said in a joint press conference with Chinese President Xi Jinping today at the end of the APEC summit. The move, which was months in the making, came as a surprise to experts expecting a much more limited scope, the Wall Street Journal reports. Calling it a "game changer," Mother Jones notes that it could heavily influence next year's climate talks in Paris. Key mandates include the US reducing emissions by 26% to 28% by the year 2025 (compared to the year 2005), as well as doubling the rate of emissions reduction each year between 2020 and 2025 to an average 2.3% to 2.8%, the AP reports. China is pledging to stop increasing emissions by 2030 (even earlier, if possible), as well as increase energy from zero-emission sources such as wind, solar, and nuclear to 20% by 2030. Although some worry about recent GOP gains in government as a possible roadblock, a senior administration official tells CNN that "we believe we can proceed with the authority we already have." Secretary of State John Kerry writes in a New York Times op-ed that "this is … a milestone in the United States-China relationship," a "fresh beginning," and "the first step toward a world that is more prosperous and more secure."

final_summary:
The 2015 Academy Award nominations sparked widespread disappointment and frustration within the film industry, particularly due to the notable omissions of several critically acclaimed films and individuals. Among the most egregious oversights was the exclusion of Ava DuVernay from the Best Director category for her work on Selma, a film that received widespread acclaim for its portrayal of the civil rights movement. Despite being one of the most talked-about films of the year, Selma was nominated for only two awards, including Best Original Song and Best Picture.

This lack of recognition is particularly striking given the film's impact on audiences and critics alike, as well as its historical significance in shedding light on the struggles faced by African Americans during the civil rights era. The decision to nominate Selma for Best Picture, but overlook its outstanding achievements in other categories, raises questions about the Academy's priorities and commitment to diversity.

Furthermore, the snubbing of Ava DuVernay serves as a stark reminder of the ongoing issues of racial and gender inequality within the film industry. As a black female director, DuVernay faces significant barriers to success, and her omission from the Best Director category sends a damaging message about the value placed on her contributions to cinema. The Academy's failure to recognize her work is especially disheartening given the strides made by other black directors, such as Steve McQueen, who won the Best Director award for 12 Years a Slave in 2014.

The lack of diversity in the nominations is also evident in the absence of other notable black actors and actresses, including David Oyelowo, who delivered a standout performance as Martin Luther King Jr. in Selma. His omission from the Best Actor category is particularly puzzling, given the high praise he received from critics and audiences alike.

The Academy's demographics, with 93% of its voting members identifying as white and 76% as male, contribute to the problem of underrepresentation. Despite President Cheryl Boone Isaacs' efforts to promote diversity, the nominations fail to reflect the rich tapestry of voices and perspectives within the film industry. The consequences of this oversight extend beyond the immediate disappointment of fans and artists, potentially affecting the future opportunities available to marginalized groups within the industry.

The snubbing of Ava DuVernay and the limited recognition of Selma serve as a poignant reminder of the ongoing struggle for representation and equality within the film industry. The Academy's failure to acknowledge the excellence of these works perpetuates a cycle of marginalization, making it increasingly challenging for underrepresented groups to break through and achieve recognition.

reference: – The Oscar nominations come once a year, and so too the avalanche of stories on the biggest snubs. But in terms of 2015, Richard Lawson at Vanity Fair puts it thusly: "Don’t the nominations this year feel so wrong?" Our roundup of this year's particularly vocal snub-coverage: Director Phil Lord is grabbing a ton of headlines for his "touché" response to the Lego Movie somehow not being nominated for Best Animated Feature: "It's okay. Made my own!" he tweeted along with an image of an Oscar made entirely from yellow Legos. He's nearing 12,000 retweets. How Jezebel sums it up: 2015 Oscar Nominations Snub Basically Everybody Who Isn't a White Dude. Mashable notes "this will be the whitest group since 1998." In that vein, Scott Mendelson at Forbes zeros in on one "egregious" omission: Selma director Ava DuVernay. His lengthy piece explores why her snub matters. Mashable notes that no black female director has ever gotten a nod; only three black male directors have (John Singleton, Boyz n the Hood; Lee Daniels, Precious; Steve McQueen, 12 Years a Slave). None won. Lawson looks beyond Selma's Best Director snub, expanding it: "That the movie’s wonderful acting, directing, writing, editing, and cinematography were shut out is a real oversight, sad in the story it tells and troubling in how grimly predictable it was." (Its only nods were Best Picture and Best Original Song.) For a meaty rundown, check out Huffington Post's list of today's 29 biggest snubs.

final_summary:
A devastating mass poisoning outbreak in Irkutsk, Russia, has claimed at least 58 lives, making it the worst such incident in modern Russian history. The tragedy occurred when residents, seeking a cheap alternative to alcohol, unknowingly consumed a highly toxic hawthorn-scented liquid masquerading as a safe alcoholic beverage. The liquid, labeled as containing ethyl alcohol, was actually a mixture of poisonous methanol, which can cause blindness and death.

The victims, mostly aged between 25 and 62, included a doctor and a kindergarten teacher, with nearly half of the survivors facing a grim prognosis due to delayed medical treatment. Many of those who perished were discovered in their homes, having succumbed before they could call for help. The poisonings were linked to a network of black marketeers producing and distributing the lethal concoction, which also included several brands of vodka.

According to eyewitness accounts, the victims often purchased the fake liquor from private stores, unaware of the danger lurking within. One survivor, a 33-year-old man, recalled drinking just a single shot of the tainted liquid before waking up blind the next morning. He managed to seek medical attention and later learned of the widespread poisoning affecting his community.

Investigations revealed an illicit workshop where the poisonous bath oil was manufactured, alongside bootleg vodka. Authorities have since confiscated over 500 liters of the hazardous liquid and arrested several individuals connected to the operation. The incident highlights the alarming prevalence of surrogate alcohol consumption in Russia, with up to 12 million citizens resorting to household products like perfume, aftershave, and cleaning solutions due to economic hardship exacerbated by two years of Western sanctions.

Methanol, the toxic ingredient responsible for the poisonings, is a chemical commonly found in antifreeze. When ingested, it breaks down into formaldehyde and formic acid, causing severe symptoms including nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, respiratory distress, dizziness, blindness, and coma. Even small amounts – as little as 1 tablespoon – can be fatal, underscoring the need for prompt medical intervention.

As the investigation unfolds, officials are working to prevent similar tragedies in the future, emphasizing the importance of proper labeling and regulation of household products to prevent misuse. The Irkutsk disaster serves as a stark reminder of the dangers of unregulated markets and the devastating consequences of consuming counterfeit or adulterated substances.

reference: – "They came to doctors too late. … Only a miracle can save them." Those were the words to the Siberian Times from the Irkutsk region's health minister regarding those sickened from ingesting a deadly bath oil, with dozens already dead from the mass poisoning—said to be the "worst such case in modern Russian history." The BBC reports at least 58 have died, per local media, and notes the death toll is expected to rise, as many of the more than three dozen poisoned are in serious condition. The concoction that caused the deaths was a black-market mixture promoted as a safe, hawthorn-scented alcoholic beverage, with a label that said it contained ethyl alcohol, which is OK to consume. However, it actually was made with methanol, which Live Science notes is a harmful chemical used in antifreeze that (besides death) can also cause a slew of other medical problems, including blindness. A market worker tells Reuters she usually uses the oil to remove grease and remnants of old price tags still stuck on surfaces. The BBC—which notes millions of cash-strapped people in Russia often resort to drinking cheap "surrogate" booze, including perfume and window cleaner—says the owners of the shop where the bath oil was made have been arrested. (Beer wasn't officially considered alcohol in Russia until a few years ago.)

final_summary:
Mexican President Enrique Peña Nieto unveiled a comprehensive anti-crime plan aimed at tackling the country's rampant lawlessness and corruption, particularly in the wake of the disappearance of 43 college students in the southern town of Iguala two months prior. The proposal involves placing local police forces under the control of state governments, dissolving municipalities infiltrated by drug gangs, and establishing a single, nationwide emergency telephone number. This move marks a shift in focus for Peña Nieto, who initially sought to concentrate on economic and legal reforms upon taking office in 2012.

The plan, announced on November 27, 2014, targets four of Mexico's most troubled states – Guerrero, Michoacán, Jalisco, and Tamaulipas – where federal police and other security forces will be deployed to combat organized crime. The initiative aims to address the complex division of powers between federal, state, and local authorities, with some local police refusing to act against federal crimes such as drug trafficking.

Peña Nieto acknowledged the influence of the Iguala tragedy on his decision, stating that the "cruelty and barbarity" of the incident had "shocked Mexico." The president emphasized that "Mexico cannot go on like this" and that the country must change. His announcement came as authorities discovered the decapitated, partly burned bodies of 11 men near the Guerrero city of Chilapa, further highlighting the need for action.

The proposed reforms, which will be formally presented to Congress next week, also involve assigning national identity numbers or documents to Mexicans and simplifying the handling of crimes at different levels of government. While some analysts welcomed the initiative, others expressed skepticism, citing previous failed attempts to tackle corruption and crime in Mexico. Security analyst Alejandro Hope questioned the effectiveness of the plan, suggesting that it may follow a familiar pattern of short-term solutions without lasting impact.

The disappearance of the 43 students, who were detained by municipal police in Iguala, has sparked widespread outrage and protests across the country. The official investigation suggests that the students were murdered by a drug gang in collaboration with the mayor of Iguala, José Luis Abarca, who has been arrested. However, relatives of the students and their supporters remain unconvinced by the official explanation, demanding proof of their deaths through forensic analysis.

In recent years, thousands of people have gone missing or been killed in Mexico due to drug-related violence, with several killings or suspected kidnappings reported in the past week alone. The situation has put pressure on Peña Nieto to take decisive action, and his anti-crime plan represents a significant departure from his initial focus on economic and legal reforms.

reference: – Mexican President Pena Nieto has promised sweeping reforms to the country's police amid nationwide outrage over the massacre of 43 students abducted by a local police force working with gang members. "Mexico must change," he said yesterday, unveiling plans to dissolve all 1,800 of the country's local police forces—many of which are notoriously corrupt and infiltrated by gang members—and replace them with state-wide forces, the BBC reports. The president's proposal would also allow the federal government to take over town governments corrupted by cartels. Most of Mexico's police officers belong to local forces and most of them make less than $500 a month, encouraging the corruption Pena Nieto aims to stamp out. But critics say the president is merely repacking initiatives already in progress and trying to repeat a few failed ones, and question is whether things will be any better if the police are put under unified control. "The root problem is the quality of the police, not who commands them," a public security expert tells the Wall Street Journal. "Many state forces are also a disaster." The president's plan will focus on four of the most violent states at first, including Guerrero, where the students were kidnapped and where 11 decapitated bodies were found dumped by a road yesterday, the AP reports.

final_summary:
Terry Wayne Ward, a 71-year-old man from DeMotte, Indiana, received a fitting tribute at his funeral service, which perfectly captured his playful and humorous personality. His daughter, Jean Lahm, wrote a unique and entertaining obituary that went viral on social media and was shared by numerous news outlets nationwide. The obituary, which Lahm penned without informing her family beforehand, highlighted her father's quirky nature, including his 39-year tenure at AT&T, where he accumulated approximately 3,000 rolls of black electrical tape, using it for various purposes such as covering open wounds and labeling buttons.

The funeral service, held at Geisen Funeral Home in Crown Point, mirrored the tone of the obituary, filled with laughter and anecdotes about Ward's life. Lahm shared several stories, including one about a chance encounter with her father while he was working on a project near her house, where he quipped about being colorblind despite the array of colorful wires surrounding him. The service also featured a photo collage and a reception where attendees shared memories and toasted Ward's memory with a Heineken.

Ward's obituary not only celebrated his life but also brought people together, with over 700 individuals signing the online guestbook and numerous strangers expressing interest in attending the funeral. A couple from Mundelein, Illinois, even traveled to pay their respects, leaving a heartfelt note wishing they had met Ward. The outpouring of support and affection for Ward was a testament to the impact of his daughter's creative and heartfelt obituary, which humanized him beyond the typical formalities often found in traditional obituaries.

Lahm's decision to write a humorous obituary was inspired by her father's lighthearted spirit, which she believed was essential to honoring his memory. As she noted, "I love that humor resonates with people in regards to an obituary... Being in the funeral industry, I've seen that when families can come together, celebrate a life, and truly laugh, it's what helps them heal."

In the end, Ward's obituary served as a poignant reminder of the importance of celebrating life and finding joy in the face of loss. As one reader commented, "Even though I didn't know him, I know he was a great guy. This helped me remember my dad in this way as well." Ward's legacy lives on through the countless people whose lives he touched, both directly and indirectly, through his daughter's remarkable obituary.

reference: – "He lived to make other people laugh," says an Indiana woman of her father, who died last Tuesday after suffering a stroke. Terry Ward is now generating plenty of chuckles thanks to the obituary daughter Jean Lahn wrote for the Army veteran. Fox59 reports she's a little more familiar with obits than most, as she works at a funeral home where she comes across plenty of routine and formulaic remembrances. The 71-year-old doesn't get that from the get-go. His obit kicks off by saying Ward "escaped this mortal realm ... leaving behind 32 jars of Miracle Whip, 17 boxes of Hamburger Helper and multitudes of other random items that would prove helpful in the event of a zombie apocalypse." She charts his progress from high school ("only three of his teachers took an early retirement after having had him as a student") to marriage ("in the fall of 1969, perfectly between the Summer of Love and the Winter of Regret") to his 39 years of work with telephone companies, during which "he accumulated roughly 3,000 rolls of black electrical tape." Lahn wrote that he "despised 'uppity foods' like hummus, which his family lovingly called 'bean dip' for his benefit, which he loved consequently." Speaking of loves, his included "shooting guns, Bed Bath & Beyond, starlight mints, cold beer, free beer, The History Channel, CCR ... and above all, his family." As for those who loved him, they're invited to make donations in his name to "your favorite watering hole, where you are instructed to tie a few on and tell a few stories of the great Terry Ward." Read the obit in full here, or read some more tales about him, courtesy of the Post-Tribune.

final_summary:
On February 8, 2017, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) unveiled the first high-resolution images captured by its newly launched GOES-16 satellite, previously known as GOES-R. Launched on November 19, 2016, from Cape Canaveral, Florida, the $1.2 billion satellite is considered a game-changer in weather forecasting due to its advanced technology and capabilities. The satellite's Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) instrument, built by Harris Corporation, produces images at four times the resolution of existing GOES spacecraft, allowing forecasters to pinpoint the location of severe weather with greater accuracy.

The images released by NOAA showcase the satellite's capabilities, providing a full-disc view of the Western Hemisphere in high detail. These images demonstrate the satellite's ability to capture various weather patterns, including storms, dust movement, and cloud formations. The satellite can provide a full image of Earth every 15 minutes and one of the continental U.S. every five minutes, scanning the Earth at five times the speed of NOAA's current GOES imagers.

According to Stephen Volz, director of NOAA's Satellite and Information Service, the images are "the most sophisticated technology ever flown in space to predict severe weather on Earth." The satellite's enhanced capabilities will enable more accurate and timely forecasts, watches, and warnings, ultimately saving lives. The satellite's six new instruments will also generate new or improved meteorological, solar, and space weather products once it becomes operational.

GOES-16 is currently situated in geostationary orbit 22,300 miles above Earth, awaiting its final deployment. In May 2017, NOAA plans to announce the satellite's operational location, with a scheduled deployment as either GOES-East or GOES-West by November 2017. The success of GOES-16 marks the beginning of a new era in weather satellites, with the potential for even more advanced technology in the future. The second satellite in the series, GOES-S, is currently undergoing environmental testing and is expected to be operational within the next few years.

The images released by NOAA demonstrate the significant improvement in weather imaging technology, showcasing the satellite's ability to capture high-resolution images of various weather patterns. The satellite's enhanced capabilities will undoubtedly improve weather forecasting, enabling forecasters to issue more accurate warnings and predictions, ultimately saving lives and reducing the impact of severe weather events.

reference: – GOES-16, the fancy new satellite developed by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, is sending its first photos back to Earth since it lifted off from Cape Canaveral on Nov. 19, and the high-res results are causing astronomers and meteorologists to squeal with delight. One developer compared it to seeing a newborn baby's first pics—"it's that exciting for us," NOAA reports. Space.com calls the super high-res images "jaw-dropping," while NASA reports that the first images, from 22,300 miles up, catch details including dust blowing off the coast of Africa, shallow waters in the Caribbean, and mountain wave clouds in Argentina. (See a gallery here.) The hope is that viewing our own planet and the atmosphere around it in such detail ("high definition from the heavens," per NOAA) will provide data for more accurate weather forecasts that could save lives. AccuWeather reports that the "stunning" images boast resolution that is four times greater than the capability of the best current satellites. NOAA says the second (GOES-S) in a series of four sophisticated satellites is undergoing a year of testing at Lockheed Martin’s Corporation facility in Littleton, Colo., and NASA reports that it is expected to launch in 2018. GOES-16 captures a full image of Earth every 15 minutes and one of the continental US every five minutes, and it can scan five times faster than current GOES imagers. (Here's how NASA scouts asteroids.)

final_summary:
On October 18th, Darnell Barton, a 37-year-old bus driver for the Niagara Frontier Transportation Authority's Metro Bus system, witnessed a distressing scene while driving his bus southbound on Elmwood Avenue towards Buffalo State College. A young woman, estimated to be in her 20s, was standing on the narrow ledge outside the railing over the Scajaquada Expressway, putting her life at risk. Despite the chaos surrounding her, including passing traffic and pedestrians, Barton remained composed and quickly sprang into action.

He pulled up alongside the woman, opened the bus door, and shouted, "Ma'am, are you okay?" When she didn't respond, he approached her cautiously and offered assistance. The woman was described as distraught, distant, and disconnected, but Barton's gentle approach eventually broke through to her. He wrapped his arm around her and asked if she wanted to come to the safe side of the guardrail; she agreed, and he carefully lifted her to safety.

After ensuring the woman's well-being, Barton sat with her on the sidewalk and engaged in conversation, trying to gather more information about her situation. However, she remained tight-lipped, only sharing that she appreciated the scent of his cologne. Concerned citizens, including a corrections officer and a crisis counselor, soon arrived at the scene, providing additional support to the woman.

As the situation unfolded, Buffalo police, firefighters, and other emergency responders arrived to assist. Meanwhile, Barton's bus passengers, a group of 20 McKinley High School students, waited patiently for their ride home. When they finally boarded another bus, they surprised Barton with a round of applause, acknowledging his selfless act.

Despite his heroism, Barton downplayed his role, crediting the students for their exemplary behavior and maturity during the ordeal. He also expressed admiration for the way the students interacted with him, shaking his hand as they exited the bus. The incident served as a testament to Barton's character and his commitment to helping those in need, earning praise from his employer, the NFTA, and the community at large.

history.draft:
On October 18th, Darnell Barton, a 37-year-old bus driver for the Niagara Frontier Transportation Authority's Metro Bus system, witnessed a distressing scene while driving his bus southbound on Elmwood Avenue towards Buffalo State College. A young woman, estimated to be in her 20s, was standing on the narrow ledge outside the railing over the Scajaquada Expressway, putting her life at risk. Despite the chaos surrounding her, including passing traffic and pedestrians, Barton remained composed and quickly sprang into action.

He pulled up alongside the woman, opened the bus door, and shouted, "Ma'am, are you okay?" When she didn't respond, he approached her cautiously and offered assistance. The woman was described as distraught, distant, and disconnected, but Barton's gentle approach eventually broke through to her. He wrapped his arm around her and asked if she wanted to come to the safe side of the guardrail; she agreed, and he carefully lifted her to safety.

After ensuring the woman's well-being, Barton sat with her on the sidewalk and engaged in conversation, trying to gather more information about her situation. However, she remained tight-lipped, only sharing that she appreciated the scent of his cologne. Concerned citizens, including a corrections officer and a crisis counselor, soon arrived at the scene, providing additional support to the woman.

As the situation unfolded, Buffalo police, firefighters, and other emergency responders arrived to assist. Meanwhile, Barton's bus passengers, a group of 20 McKinley High School students, waited patiently for their ride home. When they finally boarded another bus, they surprised Barton with a round of applause, acknowledging his selfless act.

Despite his heroism, Barton downplayed his role, crediting the students for their exemplary behavior and maturity during the ordeal. He also expressed admiration for the way the students interacted with him, shaking his hand as they exited the bus. The incident served as a testament to Barton's character and his commitment to helping those in need, earning praise from his employer, the NFTA, and the community at large.

reference: – A Buffalo bus driver is getting the hero's treatment after stopping his bus to get a suicidal young woman off a bridge overpass, reports WIVB-TV. Darnell Barton, who had just picked up about two dozen high school students, spotted the woman on the wrong side of the ledge, seemingly about to jump into traffic, reports the Buffalo News. He stopped his bus, asked her if she needed help, then slowly made his way toward her. "She was distraught, she was distant, she was really disconnected," he recalls. "I grabbed her arm and put my arm around her and said, 'Do you want to come on this side of the guardrail,' and that was actually the first time she spoke to me. She said yes." He then sat down with her on the sidewalk until authorities arrived. “Darnell won’t tell you this, but when he went back on his bus, the McKinley students gave him a round of applause,” says a spokesman for the local transit agency.

final_summary:
Business Email Compromise (BEC) scams, also known as CEO fraud, have become a significant threat to companies worldwide, resulting in substantial financial losses. In France alone, an estimated €465 million has been lost since 2010, with 15,000 firms falling victim to the scam, including prominent companies like Michelin, KPMG, and Nestle. Globally, the FBI estimates that over $2.3 billion has been lost to BEC scams since 2013, with a 270% increase in identified victims and exposed losses since January 2015.

These scams typically begin with the attackers either phishing an executive or sending targeted emails from a look-alike domain name, often researching the target organization's relationships, activities, and interests to make the emails appear convincing. The scammers may also scrape employee email addresses and other information from the target's website to create more convincing messages. Once inside the company's system, the attackers search for keywords related to wire transfers, such as "invoice," "deposit," and "president."

Unlike traditional phishing scams, BEC scams rarely trigger spam traps due to their targeted nature and the time taken to research the target organization. The scammers may also use social engineering tactics to pressure employees into acting quickly, without questioning the authenticity of the email. In some cases, the attackers may even pose as a trusted vendor or consultant to gain the trust of the employee.

The average loss per scam is estimated to be between $25,000 and $75,000, although some incidents have resulted in losses of millions of dollars. Companies like Mattel, Ubiquiti, and The Scoular Co. have fallen victim to BEC scams, losing $3 million, $46.7 million, and $17.2 million respectively.

To combat these scams, the FBI recommends adopting two-step or two-factor authentication for email, establishing alternative communication channels to verify significant transactions, and exercising caution when publishing employee information online. Educating employees on basic security precautions and conducting regular security awareness training can also help prevent these types of scams.

Meanwhile, in India, a startup called StayUncle has emerged to provide a solution for unmarried couples seeking private accommodations. Founded by Sanchit Sethi, the company allows couples to book hotel rooms for short durations, often for just 8-10 hours, at affordable prices. Despite facing challenges from hoteliers who fear backlash from local authorities, StayUncle has managed to tie up with several hotels in Delhi and Mumbai, catering to over 200 couples in the past three months.

However, the success of StayUncle highlights the need for greater awareness and acceptance of unmarried couples in Indian society. The company's efforts to promote a more liberal attitude towards love and relationships are crucial in a country where couples often face harassment and intimidation from vigilantes and law enforcement agencies.

In contrast to the challenges faced by StayUncle, the global fight against BEC scams requires a concerted effort from governments, businesses, and individuals to raise awareness and implement robust security measures. By understanding the tactics employed by scammers and taking proactive steps to prevent these attacks, companies can reduce the risk of falling victim to these sophisticated scams.

reference: – A relatively new email scam is raking in millions of dollars, and it's got nothing to do with long-lost relatives in Nigeria. Instead, as Quartz explains, the "CEO Email Scam" dupes employees into wiring money by using bogus messages from the boss. The scammers do their homework: They assume the identity of a company CEO, or sometimes simply send an email so close to the correct email that the recipient never notices the difference. The requests go to someone in the company authorized to deal with money, usually demanding quick action—and confidentiality—because of a pending business deal. It's been so successful that the FBI issued an alert last week, citing complaints in every state and in 79 countries. "On the surface, business email compromise scams may seem unsophisticated relative to moneymaking schemes that involve complex malicious software," writes Brian Krebs of Krebs on Security. But the CEO scam is especially effective because of one crucial factor: "In traditional phishing scams, the attackers interact with the victim’s bank directly, but in the CEO scam the crooks trick the victim into doing that for them." When it works, a typical haul is between $25,000 and $75,000. The known haul worldwide is $2.3 billion, says the FBI. One cybersecurity expert in France warns that it won't go away any time soon. "It will spread because it's too good to be ignored," he tells the BBC.

final_summary:
On Tuesday, a letter containing a white granular substance was intercepted at the US Capitol's off-site mail facility in Washington, DC, prompting an ongoing investigation by the Capitol Police and the FBI. Preliminary tests revealed the substance to be ricin, a highly toxic substance derived from castor beans, although further analysis is required to confirm the findings. The letter was addressed to Senator Roger Wicker (R-Mississippi), who has been assigned a protective detail, and was postmarked from Memphis, Tennessee, with no return address. 

According to Senate Sergeant-at-Arms Terrance Gainer, the Senate mail facility has been closed for two to three days for testing and further investigation, with all mail bound for Capitol Hill being redirected through a US House facility. The discovery of the ricin-laced letter comes nearly two decades after the 2001 anthrax attacks, which targeted several senators, including then-Majority Leader Tom Daschle and Vermont Senator Patrick Leahy, resulting in the deaths of five people and infecting 17 others.

Senator Claire McCaskill (D-Missouri) stated that a suspect has been identified in the incident, although no one has been taken into custody. The FBI has confirmed that additional testing is necessary to determine the presence of the biological agent, which typically takes 24-48 hours to complete. The agency plans to conduct further tests at the Army's biomedical research laboratory at Fort Detrick, Maryland.

This is not the first time ricin has been detected in the Capitol building; in 2004, a letter containing the toxin was found in the office of then-Senate Majority Leader Bill Frist (R-Tennessee), forcing 16 employees to undergo decontamination procedures without any reported adverse effects. Ricin is a highly potent substance that can be produced easily and cheaply, and its use has been linked to extremist groups worldwide. Despite its potential as a bioterrorism agent, experts consider it less effective as a weapon of mass destruction due to its limited range and potency.

The incident has prompted warnings to state offices to be vigilant for similar letters, and postal workers have been screening mail at an off-site location since the 2001 anthrax attacks. Senator Wicker, a relatively low-profile senator in his second term, was first appointed to the Senate in 2007 and previously served as a US Representative from 1995 to 2007.

reference: – Scary story breaking out of DC: A letter addressed to Republican Mississippi Sen. Roger Wicker tested positive for the poison ricin, reports Politico. The envelope never got to him, reports CNN, which says it was intercepted at an off-site facility that sorts mail for the Capitol. Three separate tests turned up positive for the highly toxic poison. Authorities have identified a suspect, said Sen. Claire McCaskill, adding only that it was someone who writes lots of letters to lawmakers. It's not clear why the apparent target was Wicker, described by the Washington Post as a "low-profile senator in his second term." (He replaced Trent Lott in 2008.) The envelope got detected at a facility set up in 2001 after some senators were sent letters tainted with anthrax. "The bottom line is, the process we have in place worked," said McCaskill. FBI chief Robert Mueller and Homeland Security chief Janet Napolitano briefed senators today, though it's not clear when the letter was received.

final_summary:
On March 8, 2014, a disturbing photo emerged from an off-campus party at the College at Brockport, State University of New York, showing a black Labrador Retriever, identified as Mya, being held upside down above a beer keg, with the nozzle of the keg in her mouth. The photo, initially posted on the Twitter account @SUNYPartyStory, sparked widespread outrage and concern about animal abuse. 

The account, which shares photos from recent parties at SUNY schools, has over 68,000 followers and often features images of college students engaging in reckless behavior, including underage drinking and public intoxication. However, the image of Mya, a beloved pet, being forced to perform a keg stand, drew particular attention.

Following an investigation by the Brockport Police Department, along with the SUNY Brockport Police and the Sweden Dog Warden, two students were identified as suspects in the incident. Robert Yates, a 20-year-old resident of West Seneca, was charged with taking the photo and posting it online, while Shane Oliver, a 20-year-old from Bergen, was accused of holding Mya during the incident. Both students were issued appearance tickets and will face charges in Sweden Town Court on April 2.

According to authorities, Mya's owner was not present at the time of the incident, and the dog was taken into protective custody by the local dog warden. Fortunately, Mya was found to be in good physical condition despite the ordeal.

School officials at the College at Brockport expressed alarm upon learning of the incident and stated that they would investigate further and take necessary actions. While they could not comment on specific student discipline due to confidentiality laws, they pointed to the Student Code of Conduct, which allows for disciplinary action against students involved in off-campus incidents that reflect poorly on the institution.

As the incident gained national attention, many students and community members spoke out against the cruel treatment of Mya, with some calling for harsher penalties for those responsible. The incident serves as a stark reminder of the importance of treating animals with respect and kindness, particularly in situations involving vulnerable individuals like pets.

reference: – A Twitter photo of a dog held upside-down over a beer keg has a pair of college students in Western New York facing animal cruelty charges. Shane Oliver and Robert Yates, both 20-year-olds at SUNY Brockport, have been charged with torturing and injuring an animal. Oliver held the dog while Yates took and posted the picture, police say, per the Democrat and Chronicle. The picture, which featured a person wearing a college T-shirt, was posted on a Twitter account called SUNYPartyStories before making the rounds online. It was captioned "Keg stands n Bits," Syracuse.com reports. Mya, whose owner wasn't home during the incident, is in good condition with the local dog warden.

final_summary:
According to a recent ranking by U.S. News & World Report, the best diets for various health and fitness goals vary depending on individual objectives. In terms of weight loss, Weight Watchers took the top spot, closely followed by Jenny Craig and the Raw Food Diet. However, when considering overall health benefits, the DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet emerged as the winner, with the Mediterranean Diet ranking second. This ranking was determined through an analysis of 20 diets, each assessed by a panel of 22 experts in diet and nutrition, diabetes, and heart disease across seven key categories: short-term weight loss, long-term weight loss, ease of adherence, nutritional completeness, diabetes prevention and management, and heart disease management.

In addition to its overall ranking, the DASH diet was also found to be the most effective for managing diabetes, surpassing the Mayo Clinic diet. Meanwhile, the Ornish Diet secured the top position for heart-healthy diets, with the Therapeutic Lifestyle Changes (TLC) diet placing second. Interestingly, the report noted that while exercise plays a crucial role in maintaining a healthy lifestyle, it falls outside the scope of a traditional diet's primary focus, which remains centered on food choices.

These findings come at a time when obesity rates continue to pose a significant concern in the United States. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, approximately 34% of American adults aged 20 and over are either overweight or obese, highlighting the need for effective dietary solutions to address this pressing issue.

It stands for Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension and it's generally meant for adults looking to curb high blood pressure. It's big on "whole grains, fruits, vegetables, low-fat and nonfat dairy foods, lean proteins, nuts and limited amounts of fats and sweets," explains the Los Angeles Times, which writes about it for a different reason: A new study suggests it slows weight gain in teen girls. Back on the US News & World rankings: The Mediterranean diet came in second for overall diet, while Jenny Craig and the Raw Food diet were close runners-up for weight loss, Reuters reports. “Our analysis put hard numbers on the common-sense belief that no diet is ideal for everybody,” says the magazine. It all depends on your aim. Click for details on the specific diets and complete rankings.

final_summary:
Chicago has secured the rights to host the Lucas Museum of Narrative Art, a $1 billion interactive museum showcasing George Lucas' vast collection of artwork and movie memorabilia. The museum, which will be built on a 17-acre site between Soldier Field and McCormick Place, is expected to open in 2018. Lucas, the creator of Star Wars, chose Chicago over Los Angeles and San Francisco after a fierce competition, citing the city's central location, ease of access to public transportation, and its reputation for embracing the arts and architecture.

The museum will feature a collection of over 100 years of storytelling history, including Norman Rockwell paintings, special effects from Industrial Light & Magic, and iconic movie memorabilia such as the Darth Vader costume. The museum's CEO, Laurie Norton Moffatt, described the collection as "still an emerging and growing" one, with a focus on the power of the visual image.

Lucas, who is married to Chicago businesswoman Mellody Hobson, had initially considered building the museum in his hometown of San Francisco, where he grew up and built his film career. However, after the Presidio's board rejected his proposal for a site on Crissy Field, Lucas turned his attention to Chicago. Mayor Rahm Emanuel played a key role in securing the deal, touting the city's tourist attractions and economic benefits.

The museum will be entirely self-funded by Lucas, who has committed to accumulating a $400 million endowment over time. Construction costs will be covered by Lucas himself, and the museum will not receive taxpayer subsidies. The city will lease the site to the museum for $1 per year, with the parking lots being relocated underground at Lucas' expense.

While the museum has drawn praise from city officials, it has also faced opposition from open-space advocates, including Friends of the Parks, who argue that the site violates the city's Lakefront Plan and Protection Ordinance. The organization has threatened to sue the city if the plans move forward.

Despite these concerns, Emanuel expressed enthusiasm for the project, highlighting its potential to create hundreds of jobs and stimulate economic growth. Lucas, meanwhile, expressed gratitude for the opportunity to bring his vision to life in Chicago, saying, "I am humbled to be joining such an extraordinary museum community and to be creating the museum in a city that has a long tradition of embracing the arts and architecture."

reference: – Chicago will soon be home George Lucas' collection of art, film memorabilia, animation, and more—though the city wasn't necessarily Lucas' first choice for his private museum. The filmmaker proposed a $700 million plan earlier this year to build the museum in San Francisco, but his chosen bayside location was rejected, the New York Times reports. The city's Presidio Trust suggested he put the museum near his former film studio. Instead, he opted for Chicago, the Chicago Tribune reports. Chicago mayor Rahm Emanuel had battled to win the museum for the city, where Lucas lives part time; his wife is from Chicago. "I am humbled to be joining such an extraordinary museum community and to be creating the museum in a city that has a long tradition of embracing the arts and architecture,” Lucas says. “Choosing Chicago is the right decision for the museum, but a difficult decision for me personally because of my strong personal and professional roots in the Bay Area." The museum, set to open in 2018, will display items as varied as Norman Rockwell paintings, a Darth Vader suit, movie posters, and special-effects technology, the Tribune notes. The Chicago Sun-Times calls it a $1 billion project, noting that it will be built on the city's lakefront. (It's not the first time Lucas has had to retool a massive building project.)

final_summary:
Eric Hites, a 560-pound man from Danville, Indiana, embarked on a remarkable journey to bike across the United States, motivated by a desire to lose weight, regain his health, and win back his estranged wife. Weighing over 250 pounds more than average, Hites felt like a "miserable failure" and was struggling to hold a job due to his weight. However, after calling his wife and promising to change, he started hitting the gym, preparing for his epic adventure.

On June 10, Hites began his journey from Falmouth, Massachusetts, with a $170 bike and a trailer carrying 300 pounds of equipment. Initially, the journey was tough, with a bike that wasn't suitable for his weight and frequent setbacks, including a bent rim that forced him to stop in Tiverton, Rhode Island. Despite these challenges, Hites persevered, posting updates on his blog and Facebook, and slowly gaining traction.

As he rode, Hites encountered various individuals who were inspired by his story, including a personal trainer in Martha's Vineyard and a police officer who helped him recover his stolen belongings. His wife, too, took notice of his efforts and eventually acknowledged that he was genuinely committed to changing his life. The couple's relationship began to mend, with Hites' wife expressing admiration for his determination.

However, just as things seemed to be looking up, Hites faced another setback when his bike broke down in Tiverton. Yet, instead of giving up, he received an outpouring of support from the local community, including offers of new bicycles, donations, and encouragement. The story went viral, and soon, Hites was receiving national attention, with the Beach Body network offering to partner with him on a nationwide fitness campaign.

With his newfound fame, Hites found himself becoming an inspiration to others who were struggling with similar issues. People reached out to him, sharing their own stories of struggle and seeking advice on how to overcome their challenges. Hites, now dubbed the "Fat Guy," discovered that his journey had become about more than just his own transformation – it was about helping others achieve theirs. With a renewed sense of purpose, Hites continued his journey, determined to reach his destination and inspire countless others along the way.

reference: – Eric Hites recently hit 40 and decided it was time to make some serious lifestyle changes. Having reached 560 pounds thanks to soda and a whole lot of sitting around, the DJ says both his health and his marriage are at stake, reports the Newport Daily News. Unfortunately, after getting off to a good start in Falmouth, Mass., (Tiverton-Little Compton Patch reports his dad dropped him and his $170 bike "on the side of the road") and losing 60 pounds in those first two weeks, the Indiana man hit a bump in the road, reports Fox 59: He bent the rim of one of his wheels in Tiverton, RI. "I thought it would take four months, but [having started in June] I’m only in Rhode Island," he says. Fortunately the owner of nearby Newport Bicycle, who says it "seems like a really good cause," built him a new bike by fitting a frame with mountain bike wheels that should support his weight all the way to California; he'll reportedly hit the road again today. Having first made a name for himself when he published the cookbook Everybody Loves Ramen, a compilation of 50 recipes from his college days (he studied graphic design but never graduated), Hites hopes to publish again, this time about his journey. He's traveling with his gear in tow, and writes on his website Fat Guy Across America that first and foremost he's got "to prove things to my wife and love." Patch reports the two were estranged when he set off, and that his wife, who was widowed once before, didn't want to repeat the experience. She has reportedly told him his quest is "the most romantic thing." Hites says he doesn't know his exact weight now (he dresses in size 70 pants), but he may weigh himself on the big scale at the junkyard up the road before getting back on the saddle. He says he hopes to drop down to a chubby but healthier 300 pounds. (When it comes to marriage, this is apparently the best age to wed.)

final_summary:
Netflix had anticipated a negative reaction from customers regarding their recent price hike, which will split their services into two separate plans starting in September. Managers informed customer-service representatives a day prior to the announcement that they could expect angry calls, prompting the company to add extra staff to handle the increased volume. Despite this preparation, hundreds of employees were still unable to keep up with the demand, resulting in long wait times for frustrated callers.

A Wall Street analyst, Rich Greenfield, conducted a test of Netflix's customer service department after the announcement and reported that the company was overwhelmed by calls, with typical wait times ranging from 9-15 minutes. Greenfield also expressed disappointment with the response from Netflix employees, who suggested to callers threatening to cancel their subscriptions that they should wait until September instead of offering any discounts or promotions to retain their business.

When questioned about the backlash, Netflix spokesperson Steve Swasey stated that the company had anticipated the reaction and was not caught off guard. Swasey emphasized that Netflix had conducted research and testing prior to the announcement, knowing that some customers would be upset by the price change.

In addition to addressing the controversy surrounding the price hike, Netflix announced that they have secured a multi-year licensing agreement with NBC Universal, retaining existing content and adding new titles, including films like "Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind" and TV shows like "The Office" and "30 Rock". However, the agreement also includes higher licensing fees, contributing to the price increase.

reference: – Well, now we know what a portion of those new, higher Netflix fees could be used for: to pay call center reps to deal with angry customers. Customer service reps have been telling callers that the company not only told them to be ready for a backlash, it hired extra employees to take the calls, CNET reports. “We tested, we researched, we analyzed. We knew what the reaction was going to be,” a spokesman confirmed. “We are not surprised.” Despite the extra manpower, the phone lines have been overwhelmed. One analyst found that it took representatives nine to 15 minutes to get to the many callers on hold. When they did pick up, their suggestion was to just cancel in September, when the price increase hits. “There was simply no promo or 'save' technique to … retain our business,” he complained, indicating that “Netflix is simply not concerned with the prospect of losing customers.” But hey, it’s not all bad news, Netflix users: The company has renewed its deal with NBC Universal, so at least you can stream new episodes of 30 Rock.

final_summary:
As the world welcomed the New Year 2013, numerous state laws took effect across the United States, often flying under the radar amidst the focus on federal legislation. According to Robert Stern, former president of the Center for Governmental Studies in California, lawmakers enact various laws ranging from significant to seemingly trivial during each legislative session. However, the number of laws being passed has decreased in recent years, primarily due to the influence of the tea party movement, which campaigned on reducing government intervention in citizens' lives. 

In 2009, over 40,000 state laws were passed, whereas in 2012, less than 30,000 made it through. Many of these new laws aim to rectify past errors or abolish outdated regulations, such as Kentucky's deletion of provisions regarding pensions for Confederate soldiers from its constitution. Other legislation reflects current societal concerns, including the aftermath of high-profile cases like the conviction of former Penn State assistant coach Jerry Sandusky for child abuse, prompting California to mandate child abuse reporting among school coaches and administrators. 

California also implemented "Caylee's Laws," which impose severe penalties on parents or guardians who fail to report a child's disappearance or death within 24 hours, inspired by the case of Caylee Anthony. Additionally, gay marriage became legal in Maryland, following Maine's similar decision, and same-sex couples from other states are now recognized in Maine. New Hampshire banned partial-birth abortions, while Montana required parental notification for minors seeking abortions. 

Other notable laws include California becoming the first state to grant expanded authority to investigate mortgage fraud and prohibit large lenders from foreclosing on homeowners requesting loan modifications. Pennsylvania now mandates the use of the E-Verify system to verify employees' citizenship on public-works projects, and Montana will withhold state services from undocumented immigrants. Illinois also made it unlawful for employers to demand job applicants' social media passwords. These developments demonstrate that state governments are addressing pressing issues, despite voter dissatisfaction with their legislatures.

reference: – While everyone fretted about the fiscal cliff, thousands of new state laws quietly took effect at midnight. The Christian Science Monitor, the Washington Post, and Mediaite run down a few notable ones, from the weird (feral hogs?) to the more conventional: Caylee's Law: Legislation named after Caylee Anthony took effect in California and Illinois. Parents now face big penalties if they don't report the disappearance or death of a child within one day. Abortion: Partial-birth abortions are now banned in New Hampshire. In Montana, minors wanting an abortion must notify their parents. Illegal immigration: Employers in some states are now required to use the E-Verify system to confirm citizenship of their employees. Facebook: Employers can no longer ask job applicants for Facebook or Twitter passwords. Food safety: In Maryland, it's now illegal to use chicken feed that includes arsenic. Though it's the first state to pass such a ban, the practice is already illegal in Canada and the EU. Driverless cars: Are now legal on California roads, though a human must always be in the passenger seat. Weirder laws: No releasing feral hogs in Kentucky, and no using a dog to chase a bear or a bobcat in California. Strip clubs in Illinois must now charge $3 at the door and donate it to rape crisis centers. Also in Illinois, motorcycle wheelies are now banned. Also today, same-sex marriage became legal in Maryland.

final_summary:
On April 18, 2016, Australian actor Chris Hemsworth, known for playing Thor in the Marvel cinematic universe, had a harrowing experience when he lost his wallet during a dinner outing with his family in a "rough" area of Los Angeles. Unbeknownst to him, the wallet contained a substantial amount of cash. 

Seventeen-year-old Tristin Budzyn-Baker stumbled upon the wallet and, instead of keeping it for himself, took the initiative to track down its rightful owner. Upon discovering the wallet, Budzyn-Baker showed it to his mother, exclaiming, "Mom, do you know who this is?" only to reveal that it belonged to none other than Thor himself. 

Budzyn-Baker then contacted Hemsworth's management team, informing them about the recovered wallet and, in a bold move, requested tickets to The Ellen DeGeneres Show, where Hemsworth was scheduled to appear soon. His request was granted, and he was awarded tickets along with his mother to attend the show.

During the episode, Hemsworth shared the story of how he lost his wallet and expressed gratitude towards Budzyn-Baker for his honesty. As a token of appreciation, Hemsworth handed over a significant sum of money from the wallet to Budzyn-Baker, stating that he had expected it to be empty but was pleased to find it still contained a substantial amount of cash. Furthermore, Hemsworth presented Budzyn-Baker with a letter of recommendation for his pursuit of the Eagle Scout award, highlighting his exceptional character and integrity.

The generosity did not end there; Ellen DeGeneres surprised Budzyn-Baker with an additional $10,000 reward, commending him for being an "amazing young man" and showcasing the importance of honesty. This heartwarming encounter serves as a testament to the value of integrity and the positive outcomes that can arise from making the right choices.

reference: – A 17-year-old kid who returned Thor's missing wallet is an even bigger Boy Scout than Captain America—literally. Mashable reports actor Chris Hemsworth was eating with his family in a "rough" area of Los Angeles when he forgot his wallet. "I left my wallet on the table and thought, 'I'm never going to get it back, I'm never going to see it again,'" Hemsworth said on Monday's episode of The Ellen DeGeneres Show. But he didn't count on Tristin Budzyn-Baker, who found the wallet. "I looked up at my mom and was like, 'Ma, do you know who this is?' We found Thor's wallet,'" Budzyn-Baker told Ellen. Budzyn-Baker wrote a letter to Hemsworth's management, asking only for tickets for his mom to see Hemsworth on Ellen's show. He got that and more, USA Today reports. "I expected it was going to be empty, and all the cash was in there, so I want to give you the cash—there's a little extra in there," Hemsworth told Budzyn-Baker during Monday's show, handing the teen a wad of cash. It didn't stop there. Budzyn-Baker got $10,000 from Ellen for being "such an honest guy," and Hemsworth wrote the Boy Scout a letter of recommendation for the Eagle Scout award. It seems it pays to be a hero's hero.

final_summary:
On December 18, 2013, William Morris Endeavor Entertainment (WME), a leading talent, entertainment, and media company, announced an agreement to acquire IMG Worldwide (IMG), a market leader in college and professional sports, event management, client representation, fashion, and multi-media rights management. This acquisition combines two prominent players in the entertainment industry, creating a unique global sports and entertainment platform operating across North America, Europe, Asia, South America, and Africa.

The combined entity will boast an unparalleled client roster, a broad relationship base with sponsors, brands, and broadcasters, and marquee assets in sports, events, film, and television, and fashion. WME's Co-CEOs, Patrick Whitesell and Ariel Emanuel, will serve as Co-CEOs of the merged company. 

Silver Lake, a global leader in technology investing, partnered with WME to facilitate this acquisition, with Egon Durban, Managing Partner of Silver Lake, expressing enthusiasm for the deal, citing IMG's position in growing end markets and its potential for accelerated growth through digital expansion. Mubadala Development Company will become a minority investor in the transaction.

The acquisition marks another step in the evolution of WME, which was formed in 2009 through the merger of the William Morris Agency and Endeavor. With over 1,000 employees and offices in several major cities worldwide, WME represents a diverse range of artists and content creators across various mediums, including film, television, music, literature, theater, and digital media.

Silver Lake, with approximately $20 billion in combined assets under management, has invested in numerous high-profile technology and tech-enabled companies, including Alibaba, Dell, and Skype. The firm's involvement in the acquisition underscores its commitment to supporting innovative businesses in rapidly evolving industries.

The terms of the transaction were not disclosed, although reports suggest that the deal value remains confidential. The acquisition is subject to customary closing conditions, with WME and IMG set to form a powerful alliance in the entertainment industry.

reference: – What do Oprah Winfrey and Maria Sharapova have in common? Soon, they could both be represented by the same company. Hollywood talent titan William Morris Endeavor Entertainment has reached a deal to buy the massive sports agency IMG Worldwide, the companies announced today. "IMG has incredible strategic value," WME's co-CEOs said in a statement. IMG's "leadership across sports, fashion, and media are a strong complement to our business." WME, and its private equity partner Silver Lake, will pay about $2.4 billion, sources tell the Wall Street Journal, beating out a bid from another private equity firm, CVC Capital Partners, for about $2 billion. The deal is in part a sign that movie stars aren't what they used to be, the Journal explains; Hollywood agencies are diversifying in part because contracts for A-list stars have fallen off significantly. The fall of sitcoms and rise of original cable shows have also cut into once massive syndication revenues.

final_summary:
Scientists have long sought to determine the smallest entity in the universe, a quest that has led them down a rabbit-hole of increasingly mind-bending concepts. Historically, humans believed grains of sand to be the fundamental building blocks of our reality, only to discover atoms, which were initially thought to be indivisible, yet later found to comprise protons, neutrons, and electrons. However, even these subatomic particles have been broken down further, with quarks and electrons appearing to be the next level of fundamental matter. Physicist Andy Parker, a professor of high-energy physics at Cambridge University, questions whether quarks and electrons might themselves be divisible, leaving open the possibility of even smaller entities existing within the universe.

One such contender for the smallest thing in the universe is the singularity at the center of a black hole, a point of infinite density where gravity overwhelms all else. While many experts assume this singularity to be infinitely dense, Parker suggests it may be significantly smaller, potentially measuring in the order of a million million times smaller than the distances currently observable. This estimate aligns with the concept of superstrings, hypothetical loops of string that could underlie all particles, including those comprising black holes.

The notion of superstrings raises additional questions regarding the nature of space and matter. If particles are indeed strings rather than point-like objects, it resolves issues related to infinite forces and energies that arise when attempting to approach a point. However, the lack of empirical evidence supporting string theory leaves its validity uncertain. An alternative explanation proposes that space itself consists of discrete, granular units – often referred to as space-time foam – which would prevent particles from approaching each other infinitely closely.

Another critical aspect of the discussion revolves around the Planck length, a scale of approximately 1.6 x 10^-35 meters, representing the theoretical limit of measurable length. This boundary marks the transition between general relativity and quantum mechanics, where the universe becomes probabilistic and indeterminate. The Planck length is thought to be the size at which quantum gravity begins to dominate, allowing for phenomena such as black hole formation through the energy of the gravitational field.

Considering these findings, it is possible that all the universe's smallest entities – superstrings, singularities, and even space-time grains – converge on the Planck length as their common denominator. This scale, however, remains inaccessible to measurement due to the uncertainty principle, rendering it a theoretical construct rather than an empirically verifiable quantity. As scientists continue to probe the mysteries of the universe, they may ultimately need to revise our understanding of time and space to accommodate the strange and counterintuitive nature of the smallest things in existence.
