reference: – The tiniest thing in the universe is not a grain of sand, as people once thought, nor a proton or neutron, or even a quark or electron—as small as they all are. In fact, experts today balk at naming any point-like object "the smallest" because of a theoretical conundrum: When you're infinitely near any point, the forces that act on it turn infinitely huge. Hoping to duck the whole "infinite" thing, scientists have invented novel theories around it, physicist Andy Parker tells Space.com. Among the work-arounds: Superstring theory, which posits that particles are like loopy strings rather than points. This would enable scientists to get infinitely close to something but always remain further from another part of it. "Space-time foam," the theory that space is made of pixels or grains. This would keeps particles from being infinitely close to each other, because the grain create a natural distance. One candidate for "smallest" that Parker discounts is black hole singularities, although he concedes that "they are maybe a million million times or even more than that smaller than the distances we've seen so far." See excerpts of Parker's BBC show on the subject.

final_summary:
In a shocking revelation, former Florida Republican Party chairman Jim Greer accused top party officials of orchestrating a scheme to suppress the black vote during his tenure as chairman. According to a 630-page deposition obtained by the media, Greer claimed that party officials, including Senate President Mike Haridopolos and Senator John Thrasher, met to discuss ways to limit minority voting rights. Specifically, Greer alleged that a December 2009 meeting with party officials involved discussions about voter suppression and preventing African Americans from casting ballots. This claim directly contradicts the long-standing Republican stance that allegations of voter suppression are baseless and intended to discredit their efforts to prevent election fraud.

Greer's testimony also revealed a culture of corruption and deceit within the party, with officials prioritizing their own interests over transparency and accountability. He stated that the party's budget and audit committee was comprised of "whack-a-do, right-wing crazies" who sought to take control of the party and undermine then-Governor Charlie Crist's leadership. Greer further alleged that legislative leaders, including Haridopolos and Thrasher, used party credit cards for personal expenses, with one official reportedly stating, "We eat what we kill," implying that they operated outside of party oversight.

Greer's deposition also shed light on the circumstances surrounding his departure from the party. He claimed that he was forced out due to his disagreements with Crist and legislative leaders, particularly regarding the appointment of a liberal African American judge to the Florida Supreme Court and Crist's endorsement of John McCain for president in 2008. Greer's severance package, worth $130,000, was allegedly negotiated but never paid, leading to a lawsuit against the party and its officials.

The deposition also touched on Greer's creation of Victory Strategies LLC, a company that received nearly $200,000 from the party. Greer denied owning any interest in the company initially but later admitted to doing so when questioned privately. Several party officials claimed they were unaware of Greer's involvement in the company, which led to the filing of criminal charges against him.

Throughout the deposition, Greer expressed deep-seated anger towards the party officials who he felt wronged him, including Haridopolos and Thrasher. He claimed that they orchestrated an investigation by the Florida Department of Law Enforcement and the statewide grand jury to avoid paying him the severance package. Greer's family has suffered financially as a result of the ordeal, with his children relying on food stamps and some of his possessions being repossessed.

Thrasher responded to Greer's allegations, calling them "absurd" and claiming that Greer's actions were motivated by a desire to deflect attention from his own wrongdoing. However, Greer's testimony provides a damning indictment of the Florida Republican Party's internal dynamics and raises serious concerns about the party's commitment to democratic values and fair elections.

reference: – Liberals have been accusing recent GOP-led voter ID laws of aiming to disenfranchise minorities, who tend to vote Democrat. Now former Florida GOP chairman Jim Greer is saying on record that, yes, those laws are actually about stopping black people from voting, reports Salon, picking up on a story in the Tampa Bay Times. “I was upset because the political consultants and staff were talking about voter suppression and keeping blacks from voting,” Greer said, recalling a 2009 meeting with party officials. Greer also said the party's budget committee had been taken over by "whack-a-do, right-wing crazies." Greer is hardly without an axe to grind, though. His statements came from a 630-page deposition made in May as part of his trial on corruption charges. Greer paid a company he owned nearly $200,000 from GOP coffers while he was the chairman, although he contends it was authorized and made for services rendered. Florida state Sen. John Thrasher, who succeeded Greer as party chairman, called Greer's comments about voter suppression "baseless accusations on other people in an effort to divert attention from himself.''

final_summary: Pacific Life, an insurance company, announced it would be pulling its advertisements from Fox News' "Tucker Carlson Tonight" after the host made inflammatory comments about immigration during his opening monologue. The decision came shortly after one of the company's ads aired on the show, promoting Pacific Life as a provider that has protected generations of families for 150 years. Following the airing of the ad, Pacific Life faced backlash on social media, with many calling for the company to distance itself from the program due to Carlson's comments. In response, Pacific Life stated on Twitter that they strongly disagreed with Carlson's statements, which described the US immigration crisis as making the country "poorer and dirtier and more divided." The company emphasized that their customer base and workforce reflect the diversity of the nation, something they take great pride in. As a result, Pacific Life decided to pull all ads from the show in the coming weeks while they reassess their relationship with the program. Fox News responded to the controversy, labeling it an "unfortunate and unnecessary distraction" caused by left-wing advocacy groups using social media to stifle free speech. However, this stance was met with criticism from critics on Twitter, who continued to call for sponsors to drop the program following Carlson's comments. Specifically, he referred to immigrants as "cynical shake-down artists who have been watching too much CNN," rather than the traditional description of them as "huddled masses yearning to breathe free."

Following the airing of the ad, Pacific Life faced backlash on social media, with many calling for the company to distance itself from the program due to Carlson's comments. In response, Pacific Life stated on Twitter that they strongly disagreed with Carlson's statements, which described the US immigration crisis as making the country "poorer and dirtier and more divided." The company emphasized that their customer base and workforce reflect the diversity of the nation, something they take great pride in. As a result, Pacific Life decided to pull all ads from the show in the coming weeks while they reassess their relationship with the program. Fox News responded to the controversy, labeling it an "unfortunate and unnecessary distraction" caused by left-wing advocacy groups using social media to stifle free speech. However, this stance was met with criticism from critics on Twitter, who continued to call for sponsors to drop the program following Carlson's comments. Specifically, he referred to immigrants as "cynical shake-down artists who have been watching too much CNN," rather than the traditional description of them as "huddled masses yearning to breathe free."

reference: – Comments about immigrants that Tucker Carlson made on his Fox News show Thursday have just caused a major advertiser to give pause. The Hill reports that insurance company Pacific Life is, at least for "the coming weeks," yanking its ads from Tucker Carlson Tonight as it "[reevaluates] our relationship with his program," per a statement posted on Twitter. What Carlson said that prompted Pacific Life's retreat: Per HuffPost, the Fox host was taking issue with "previous" US leaders who insisted that Americans "shut up and accept" immigrants. Then, in a continued mocking of those previous leaders, Carlson went on to say that "we have a moral obligation to admit the world's poor, they tell us, even if it makes our own country poorer, and dirtier and more divided." Pacific Life's ad aired immediately after the opening segment in which Carlson made those remarks. "As a company, we strongly disagree with Mr. Carlson's statements," Pacific Life says. "Our customer base and our workforce reflect the diversity of our great nation, something we take great pride in." In its own statement to the Hill, Fox News lamented the "unfortunate and unnecessary distractions" it says were spurred by "left wing advocacy groups [that] under the guise of being supposed 'media watchdogs' weaponize social media against companies in an effort to stifle free speech." Newsweek notes that, before this incident, Carlson has never been boycotted by advertisers, though other Fox News hosts like Laura Ingraham and Bill O'Reilly (who's no longer with the network) have.

final_summary:
Seven-year-old Bana Alabed, a Syrian girl known globally for her tweets from besieged East Aleppo, has penned an open letter to newly inaugurated US President Donald Trump, urging him to intervene on behalf of Syrian children caught in the midst of the ongoing civil war. Bana, who fled Aleppo with her family in December, currently resides in Turkey, where she continues to advocate for peace through her Twitter account, which gained widespread attention due to her harrowing accounts of life under siege. 

In her heartfelt letter, addressed to President Trump, Bana recounts her experiences in war-torn Aleppo, where she witnessed the destruction of her school and the loss of friends due to bombings. She expresses her longing for a peaceful life, now possible in her new home in Turkey, where she hopes to attend school. However, Bana emphasizes that millions of Syrian children continue to suffer in various parts of the country, victimized by the actions of adults.

The young girl appeals directly to President Trump, stating that Syrian children are akin to his own and deserve peace, much like he does. She offers to become his friend if he commits to taking action to protect the children of Syria. Bana's plea coincides with a joint pledge by Iran, Russia, and Turkey to enforce a three-week ceasefire in Syria amidst ongoing peace talks. Despite this development, the fate of the agreement remains uncertain due to the lack of consensus between the Syrian government and rebel forces.

President Trump's stance on Syria remains ambiguous, with his administration signaling openness to collaborating with Russia, a key ally of Syrian President Bashar al-Assad. The US has faced criticism for its involvement in the conflict, particularly regarding its decision to cease aid to rebel groups. The Syrian conflict has resulted in over 300,000 fatalities, including nearly 2,000 children in 2016 alone, according to reports from the Syrian Network For Human Rights. As the situation in Syria continues to deteriorate, Bana's poignant letter serves as a powerful reminder of the human cost of the conflict and the urgent need for international intervention to safeguard the lives of innocent civilians, especially children.

reference: – A 7-year-old Syrian girl whose tweets from Aleppo showcased the horrors of war has appealed to President Trump to "do something for the children of Syria." Now living in Turkey, Bana Alabed wrote a letter to the new president days before his inauguration, the BBC reports. "Dear Donald Trump," she writes, "I lived in Syria my whole life (and) ... I am part of the Syrian children who suffered from the Syrian war." Although she is now safe, "millions of Syrian children are not like me," she writes. "They are suffering. … I know you will be the president of America, so can you please save the children and people of Syria?" Bana's mother, Fatemah, sent the text to the BBC. (Read the full letter here.) Bana opted to make her plea in writing after seeing Trump "many times on the TV," Fatemah says. Like Trump, Bana has a penchant for tweeting—she has 362,000 followers—although hers drove home the terror in besieged Aleppo before her family was evacuated last month as government forces regained control of the city. More than 300,000 people, including 150,000 children, have been killed in the six-year civil war, reports the Guardian. Trump's position on Syria is not yet known. A White House spokesman said the president would "work with any country" committed to fighting ISIS, per the Guardian. The Pentagon, meanwhile, denied on Monday that US warplanes aided a Russian mission in Syria. Russia supports the government of President Bashar al-Assad. (Bana "befriended" JK Rowling.)

final_summary:
A violent crime spree unfolded in Kansas City, Missouri, leaving a family shaken and a community searching for justice. On Wednesday evening, a 53-year-old father and his 55-year-old wife, along with their 16-year-old son, responded to a Craigslist advertisement for a 2007 Hyundai Sonata. The family met the seller, who identified himself as "AJ," at an apartment complex in the 5500 block of East 84th Street. After taking the car for a test drive, the trio returned to discuss purchasing the vehicle, during which AJ excused himself to make a phone call, claiming he needed to speak with his wife.

As AJ walked away, two masked individuals emerged from nowhere, brandishing black handguns and demanding money from the unsuspecting family. When the wife revealed they had no cash, the suspects threatened them, stating, "I know you have the money, now, give me the money." In a heroic act, the father stepped in front of his wife to protect her, prompting the suspects to open fire. The father sustained four gunshot wounds to his chest, stomach, and legs, while his wife suffered a shattered right upper arm. Miraculously, their 16-year-old son escaped unharmed.

The suspects, described as three black men in their late 20s or early 30s, seized the family's purse containing $3,000 and fled the scene in the Hyundai. The vehicle was later discovered abandoned in the 4200 block of East 68th Street. The suspects remain at large, with police urging anyone with information to come forward.

This brazen crime is believed to be linked to an earlier armed robbery, which occurred just hours prior, also involving a Craigslist transaction. In that incident, a man was robbed of an Xbox and accessories after responding to an advertisement. Both incidents share striking similarities, including the involvement of three suspects and the use of black handguns.

In response to the tragic events, Liberty-based Ferrellgas has donated $5,000 to increase the reward for information leading to the capture of the perpetrators, bringing the total reward to $7,000. The company's contribution was motivated by the fact that the injured father works for Ferrellgas and exemplifies the values of kindness and selflessness that the company strives to uphold.

As authorities continue to investigate these crimes, they warn potential victims to exercise extreme caution when engaging in online transactions, advising them to meet in public areas, avoid carrying large amounts of cash, and use alternative payment methods such as money orders or cashier's checks. Those with information regarding the suspects can contact the TIPS Hotline at 816-474-TIPS.

reference: – A Kansas City couple who went to check out a car they saw on Craigslist were shot and robbed Wednesday night, USA Today reports. They had wanted to purchase the vehicle for their 16-year-old son, who was with them but not injured. Police say the 53-year-old dad and his son had test-driven a 2007 Hyundai Sonata and were discussing payment when the seller said he had to call his wife and momentarily walked away, according to KCTV. That’s when two other men reportedly approached the family and demanded cash, then started shooting. As the dad tried to keep his wife and son out of the barrage of bullets, he was shot four times in the chest, stomach, and legs, while his 55-year-old wife took a bullet to her upper right arm, which was “shattered,” reports the TV station. The men took the wife's purse, which had $3,000 in cash for the car purchase, say police. Authorities believe the incident may be related to an earlier armed robbery that day in which a Kansas City man says he tried to sell his Xbox and gaming accessories to a man who saw his ad on Craigslist, reports the Kansas City Star. As the potential buyer was examining the wares, two other men reportedly arrived sporting a gun; the three men then lifted the Xbox and other items and ran into nearby woods, police say.

final_summary: A dramatic incident occurred involving a boat fire, where a quick-thinking individual intervened to prevent further damage. The situation was severe enough to warrant concern about the safety of those involved, prompting the need for immediate action. A bystander, described as a "speedy good Samaritan," took swift action by using a fire extinguisher to combat the blaze. Their efforts were successful in putting out the fire, preventing potential harm to people and property.

reference: – No firefighters around when your boat goes up in flames? How about hiring this freethinker to douse it with a speedboat "and look like a badass doing it," reports Gizmodo. Bystanders with a video camera recorded the flaming boat at Lake Lyndon in New Zealand but apparently just stood around and watched. One of them even dissed the boater's makeshift firefighting technique, saying, "F--k that" and "What are you doing?" But by the look of it, the boater's four passes did accomplish something: putting out the fire.

final_summary:
Washington, D.C. dominated the national English-language television ratings for the 2014 FIFA World Cup, with its high rating continuing into the group-round stage where the Ghana-USA broadcast on ESPN earned an 11.8 overnight rating, the highest in the country. This marked the highest overnight rating ever for a World Cup group-round match in the Washington market, surpassing previous records held by other major cities such as New York, Hartford/New Haven, Boston, Columbus, and Baltimore.

The nation's capital led the way for average World Cup ratings on the ESPN networks through 14 matches, with a rating of 5.0, followed closely by San Francisco with a rating of 4.4. Washington also topped the list for individual matches, including the Brazil-Croatia opener with a rating of 5.1, Chile-Australia with a rating of 3.8, and Spain-Netherlands with a rating of 4.7.

In comparison, the city's sports teams, the Capitals, Wizards, and Nationals, have struggled to achieve similar ratings, with the best-rated Caps game in the Ovechkin era earning an 8.63 rating and the best-rated Wizards playoff game this season earning an 8.51 rating. However, the Washington Redskins, the city's NFL team, averaged a significantly higher rating of 25.8 across three networks.

The World Cup match between the United States and Ghana drew a massive audience, with 15.9 million viewers tuning in between the two channels carrying the game, ESPN and Univision, setting new records for both networks. The event also garnered a 7.0 overnight rating, making it the highest overnight rating for a men's World Cup match on ESPN or ESPN2.

reference: – ESPN and Univision have scored with their World Cup coverage: The US victory over Ghana Monday night set World Cup ratings records for both broadcasters, with a total audience of 15.9 million viewers. For ESPN, it was the highest-rated men's soccer game ever. The ratings were way ahead of the 6 million that watched hockey's Stanley Cup final on Friday night, and weren't that far behind the NBA final between the San Antonio Spurs and the Miami Heat, which pulled in 17.9 million viewers, the Wall Street Journal finds. Among English-language viewers, the World Cup so far has the highest average ratings in the Washington, DC, area, with San Francisco, Boston, New York, and Orlando rounding out the top five, the Washington Post reports.

final_summary:
On January 2, 2016, Otto Frederick Warmbier, a 21-year-old University of Virginia student from Cincinnati, Ohio, was detained at Pyongyang International Airport in North Korea while attempting to depart the country after a five-day New Year's trip. Warmbier, who was born in 1994 and majored in economics with a minor in global sustainability, had traveled to North Korea as a tourist with the China-based travel company Young Pioneer Tours. 

According to North Korea's official KCNA news agency, Warmbier was detained for committing a "hostile act against the state" and had entered the country with the aim to "destroy the country's unity". However, the exact nature of the alleged hostile act remains unclear. The U.S. State Department stated that they were aware of the reports but declined to provide further information due to privacy concerns.

Warmbier's detention occurred just four days before North Korea conducted its fourth nuclear test, which drew international condemnation from the United Nations and the United States. The incident marks the third instance of a Westerner being detained in North Korea, following the cases of Hyeon Soo Lim, a Canadian pastor of South Korean origin, who was sentenced to life imprisonment in December for allegedly plotting to overthrow the government, and a Korean-American who was accused of spying.

Young Pioneer Tours, the company that organized Warmbier's trip, confirmed that he was detained on January 2 and stated that they were in contact with Warmbier's family and the U.S. State Department to secure his release. The Swedish Embassy in Pyongyang, which represents U.S. interests in North Korea, is also involved in the efforts to resolve the situation.

Warmbier's family and friends described him as a kind and adventurous individual who had previously traveled to Cuba, Ireland, and Israel. His detention has sparked concern among U.S. lawmakers, with Representative Brad Wenstrup tweeting that his office was seeking assistance from the State Department to facilitate Warmbier's return home.

The incident has heightened tensions between the United States and North Korea, which has a history of using detained foreigners as leverage to extract concessions from its adversaries. The U.S. government strongly advises against traveling to North Korea, and the country's tourism industry has faced criticism for promoting trips to the isolated nation despite its human rights record.

reference: – North Korea says it has detained an American student from the University of Virginia who visited the country as part of a "New Year's Party Tour." A rep for Chinese travel company Young Pioneer Tours says undergraduate commerce student Otto Frederick Warmbier, 21, was arrested at Pyongyang airport on Jan. 2 after a five-day tourist trip. North Korea's official news agency says he visited with the "aim to destroy the country's unity" and "was caught committing a hostile act against the state," which was "tolerated and manipulated by the US government," per Reuters. The US Embassy in Seoul says it's aware of Warmbier's arrest, per CNN. The tour group says it's working with the State Department and the Swedish Embassy in Pyongyang, which represents US interests there. "We would appreciate Otto's and his family's privacy being respected and we hope his release can be secured as soon as possible," a rep tells the BBC. Reuters notes about 6,000 westerners travel to North Korea each year; the "New Year's Party Tour" Warmbier was participating in promised a fireworks display in Kim Il Sung Square. One other American citizen is being held in North Korea. Kim Dong Chul, who says he's a naturalized US citizen who used to live in Virginia, was arrested in October.

final_summary:
On Monday, a 15-year-old boy from Detroit allegedly attempted to rob a Chase bank on the city's west side. According to Detroit Police Officer Dan Donakowski, the teen walked into the bank located in the 15000 block of West 7 Mile around 2 p.m., handed a teller a note threatening to detonate a bomb unless she handed over cash, and then demanded money. The teller complied, giving the teen an undisclosed amount of money before hitting the automatic door locking mechanism, trapping the suspect inside. 

In a separate incident, another 16-year-old boy from Detroit attempted to rob a Chase bank on the city's east side approximately 45 minutes later, around 2:30 p.m. This time, the teen approached the counter and claimed to be armed with a bomb, prompting the teller to hand over money. However, when the teen attempted to exit the bank, he found himself trapped between the inner and outer automatic doors due to the "man-trap" security system, which prevents individuals from escaping once activated. The teller had engaged this feature, leaving the teen confined until police arrived to apprehend him. In both incidents, it remains unclear whether the suspects possessed actual weapons or were merely using notes as a means of intimidation. As a result of these events, the teens were taken into custody and transported to respective juvenile facilities; their potential charges, whether as juveniles or adults, have yet to be determined.

reference: – A teenage would-be bank robber in Detroit found himself imprisoned—and presumably regretting one or two life choices—within seconds of being handed money at a bank on Monday, police say. A police spokesman tells the Detroit Free Press that the 15-year-old gave a teller a note claiming he had a bomb and demanding money. As he left with the cash, the teller hit a button that trapped the youth between two sets of doors. The security system, known as a "man-trap," keeps intruders caged until police arrive, MLive reports. Police say the teen, who didn't have any explosives on him, was taken to a juvenile detention facility. (A man who stole a restaurant's tip jar ended up losing money.)

final_summary:
In late January, 16-year-old Lebanon High School student Emily Gipson created a viral YouTube video titled "Welcome to Lebanon High School," which garnered nearly 600,000 views and sparked controversy within her school community. The video was inspired by a classmate's October suicide and serves as a scathing critique of the school's culture, particularly its handling of bullying incidents. Gipson's free-verse poem expresses her frustration with the school's lack of support for victims of bullying and its failure to hold perpetrators accountable.

The video specifically targets the school's administration, accusing them of turning a blind eye to bullying and instead promoting superficial solutions such as the "Stop It" app, which was introduced after the classmate's death. Gipson also criticizes the school's emphasis on maintaining a positive atmosphere, suggesting that this approach trivialized the seriousness of bullying and its consequences.

Gipson's punishment for creating the video was a two-day in-school suspension, which she claims was due to the school's assertion that she had recorded the video in a classroom without permission. However, Gipson disputes this claim, stating that she had obtained permission from two coaches. Principal Scott Walters acknowledged that he took issue with the content of the video, citing its potential to hurt his feelings and those of teachers, although he also expressed appreciation for Gipson's perspective and acknowledged that it may have value.

Emily's grandfather and guardian, Kenneth Gipson, supports his granddaughter's actions, praising her for speaking out against bullying and acknowledging that the video has brought attention to a critical issue. Despite the controversy surrounding the video, Gipson remains committed to using her platform to raise awareness about bullying and its effects, posting a follow-up video titled "Have I Made a Difference Yet?" which encourages teenagers to speak up against bullying.

Despite the controversy surrounding the video, Gipson remains committed to using her platform to raise awareness about bullying and its effects, posting a follow-up video titled "Have I Made a Difference Yet?" which encourages teenagers to speak up against bullying.

reference: – "We're expected to forgive the bullies because the authorities are sure they didn't mean it," Emily Gipson says in a YouTube video posted Jan. 22. "Sometimes I wonder how many kids it takes dying to make a difference." The 16-year-old sophomore at Tennessee's Lebanon High School made the video after a classmate's apparent suicide, the AP reports. The video went viral—surpassing 800,000 views—and Gipson has been hit with a two-day suspension. In the video, she says her school is an "emotional prison" and a place "where creativity is put down, where the people who make fun of others never get punished because 'There's no proof,' or 'There's nothing we can do about it,' or, my favorite, 'Kids will be kids.'" Principal Scott Walters says his feelings and those of teachers were hurt by the video, but that's not why Gipson was suspended. Gipson says the school administration accused her of "trying to incite violence," but Walters says she was suspended for filming in a classroom after school without the teacher's permission or knowledge, the Tennessean reports. Gipson says she had permission from two coaches, and in a statement quoted by CNN, Wilson County Schools spokesperson Jennifer Johnson confirms that Gipson asked a coach if she could use the classroom only to have the coach later be "mortified by the nature of her message." Johnson adds that it's "patently false" to say Gipson was punished for inciting violence and there's "no evidence whatsoever" that the student who died was bullied, despite the claims of other students. Gipson says she wanted "to be a voice for as many people as possible" and ends the video by imploring others "do not be the bully ... do not be the one that takes their own life."

final_summary:
On June 2, 2017, the Rock am Ring music festival in Nuerburg, Germany, was abruptly halted due to concerns of a potential terrorist threat. Approximately 90,000 attendees were evacuated from the venue after Koblenz police received concrete indications suggesting a possible terrorist threat, prompting them to inform the festival organizers to shut down the event for the day. The decision was made despite heightened security measures already in place, including an additional 1,200 staff, following the Manchester bombing on May 22, which claimed 22 lives.

According to police, three individuals suspected of being affiliated with a Salafist group in the neighboring state of Hesse were initially detained, although they were later released without charges. One of these individuals, described as someone not of German origin with ties to the Islamist terror network, had gained access to backstage areas, sparking further investigation.

The festival's organizers stated that after a thorough search of the premises, no evidence of an imminent danger was found, allowing the police to give the green light for the event to resume at 1:30 pm local time. Interior Minister Roger Lewentz defended the evacuation decision, emphasizing that the authorities cannot afford to take risks given the current security climate in the country.

Germany remains on high alert following the December 19, 2016, jihadist attack on a Berlin Christmas market, where 11 people lost their lives and dozens were injured. The Rock am Ring festival had also faced disruptions in the past, including last year when violent storms forced the cancellation of part of the program, injuring several attendees.

reference: – A "possible terrorist threat" shut down Rock am Ring, one of Germany's biggest music festivals, Friday near Nuerburg, AFP reports. Police say they have "concrete elements, in the light of which a possible terrorist threat cannot be ruled out." But no other details were released. According to the Guardian, thousands of people were asked to leave the festival calmly "in order to help police investigations." It's unclear how many people were in attendance Friday, but about 90,000 were expected over the three-day festival. Organizers hoped the festival would resume Saturday. Rock am Ring had already increased security, adding 1,200 additional staff, after the recent Manchester bombing at an Ariana Grande concert. (Rock am Ring was also suspended last year after dozens were injured by lightning.)

final_summary:
In the early hours of Friday morning, a series of coordinated attacks left eight members of the Rhoden family dead in Pike County, Ohio. The victims included seven adults - 40-year-old Christopher Rhoden Sr., 44-year-old Kenneth Rhoden, 38-year-old Gary Rhoden, 37-year-old Dana Rhoden, 20-year-old Clarence "Frankie" Rhoden, 20-year-old Hannah Gilley, and 19-year-old Hanna Rhoden - and 16-year-old Christopher Rhoden Jr. The brutal killings took place across four separate locations, with some victims discovered as they slept.

Pike County Sheriff Charles Reader revealed that the family was deliberately targeted, warning surviving relatives to arm themselves for protection. The sheriff expressed gratitude towards fellow Ohio sheriffs for providing support and resources during the investigation. Meanwhile, Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine acknowledged that a Facebook threat targeting one of the victims, Christopher Rhoden Jr., was being examined as part of the inquiry.

DeWine stated that the investigation is complex and may involve multiple suspects, citing the sophistication of the operation and the apparent attempt to hinder the investigation. Over 100 personnel are working tirelessly to unravel the mystery behind the mass killings. As the probe unfolds, authorities have discovered marijuana grow operations at three of the crime scenes, suggesting a potential link between the cultivation of cannabis and the murders. However, DeWine emphasized that no direct connection has been established.

Law enforcement officials have collected over 200 marijuana plants from one of the grow operations, which could yield approximately one pound of high-quality marijuana per plant, valued at around $2,000 on the black market. This discovery has led investigators to explore various motives, including a possible Mexican drug cartel connection or a local drug turf war.

As the community grapples with the trauma, residents in Piketon have expressed fear and anxiety, with some choosing to stay indoors due to concerns about the perpetrator's presence. A $25,000 reward has been offered for information leading to the capture and conviction of the killer or killers. The investigation remains ongoing, with authorities urging anyone with information to come forward and share their knowledge with the police.

reference: – A new development in Ohio: One of the eight family members killed in a horrific mass slaying had already been threatened on Facebook, CBS News reports. The threat was directed at Christopher Rhoden Jr., who at 16 was the youngest of the victims. "I'm aware of the Facebook threat," says Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine. "Every piece of information is valuable and our investigators are certainly taking that into consideration." Now Pike County Sheriff Charles Reader has told surviving Rhoden family members to be careful. "I cautioned them, told them we will be there," Reader says. "I told [them] to be armed." One surviving family member says the Rhodens are "torn up" about the killings. "And you know what I mean, kinda freaked out because we don't know," says the relative, who asked not to be identified. "There's still killers on the loose, or killer." The slayings happened in four rural homes near Piketon, Ohio, a town of about 2,200 where the Dogwood Festival on Sunday had a smaller-then-usual turnout. "People are scared to come out," says a resident. "What if that person who shot all those people is running around here." Now autopsies of all eight victims have been completed, News Net 5 reports, and a chart of the Rhoden family relationships can be seen here. (Marijuana-growing was found at three of the four murder sites.)

final_summary:
As the 2012 US presidential election drew to a close, attention began shifting towards the next presidential election in 2016. On the Democratic side, several prominent figures were being considered as potential candidates, including Hillary Clinton, who was still reeling from her unsuccessful bid for the nomination in 2008. Clinton, who had served as First Lady, Senator, and Secretary of State, remained a strong contender due to her widespread popularity and experience.

Other notable Democrats mentioned as potential candidates included Vice President Joe Biden, who hinted at a possible run but faced criticism for his tendency to make gaffes. Maryland Governor Martin O'Malley, a relatively unknown figure within the party, was also being considered. Additionally, New York Governor Andrew Cuomo, who had executive experience and a reputation for balancing liberal and conservative policies, was seen as a viable option.

On the Republican side, the field was equally crowded, with several prominent figures vying for the nomination. Former Florida Governor Jeb Bush was considered a strong contender due to his name recognition and financial resources. Other notable Republicans mentioned as potential candidates included Wisconsin Congressman Paul Ryan, who had impressed with his performance in the 2012 campaign, and Florida Senator Marco Rubio, who was seen as a rising star within the party.

New Jersey Governor Chris Christie, known for his bombastic style and bipartisanship, was also being touted as a potential candidate. Meanwhile, former Governor Mike Huckabee and Senator Rick Santorum were also considering runs, while Sarah Palin and Rick Perry were also mentioned as possibilities. The Republican Party was expected to undergo significant changes following the Romney loss, with various factions vying for control.

The possibility of an unknown candidate emerging in the aftermath of the 2012 election was also discussed, highlighting the unpredictability of the US electoral process. Some observers noted that the country's two-party system was outdated and in need of reform, citing issues such as underfunded voter registration, outdated voting machines, and corporate campaign financing.

Foreign observers, including those from Europe and China, expressed curiosity about the US electoral process, with some suggesting that the country's system was in need of modernization. The prospect of Hillary Clinton running in 2016 generated significant excitement on social media, with the hashtag #Hillary2016 trending worldwide shortly after the 2012 election results were announced. However, Clinton herself maintained that she intended to retire from politics, stating that she wanted to have her own time back and be her own person.

reference: – And so it begins: Speculation about Election 2016 is already running rampant, mere hours after President Obama was re-elected. Some of the frontrunners for a presidential run next time around, courtesy of ABC News and US News & World Report: Hillary Clinton: Not surprisingly, she's at the forefront of many minds right now ("Hillary2016" was trending on Twitter early today, the Hill notes), and is widely considered the No. 1 Democrat contender. Of course, she also keeps insisting she won't run. Joe Biden: The world took notice when he said yesterday that he'd probably vote for himself again someday—but he later joked that he just meant he'd be running for county councilman in the future. Andrew Cuomo: The New York governor has access to lots of money, and his liberal stance on social issues combined with a more fiscally conservative stance could make him a formidable contender. But don't expect him to run if Hillary does; he served under Bill Clinton and is loyal to the family. Other possible Democrats: Maryland Gov. Martin O'Malley, Los Angeles Mayor Antonio Villaraigosa, and Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand. Paul Ryan: He fared well as Romney's running mate and got himself re-elected to boot, so clearly he's a top contender. Marco Rubio: The charismatic Florida senator could help the GOP increase its appeal to Latino voters. Chris Christie: His tough talk has made him quite popular, and he's seen as more of a bipartisan figure now that he's praised the federal response to Hurricane Sandy. Other possible Republicans: Jeb Bush, Mike Huckabee, plus pretty much anyone who ran in the primary this time around. But in the International Herald Tribune, Harvey Morris has just one piece of advice: "Take a breath, America! Give yourselves time to recover from what seemed the longest and certainly was the most expensive campaign in US political history before starting on the next one."

final_summary:
Psychologist Richard Bootzin introduced the concept of "stimulus-control" in 1972, proposing that the bed should be reserved for sleep and sex only to train the brain to associate the bed with sleep. However, this approach conflicted with the allowance for sex, which is a stimulating activity. Despite this contradiction, the stimulus-control approach proved effective in managing insomnia in several studies.

Fast forward to 2013, when Charles Czeisler, a professor of sleep medicine at Harvard Medical School, discovered that around 90% of Americans use electronic devices within an hour before bed, correlating with poor sleep quality. He hypothesized that overstimulation caused by devices might impede the natural withdrawal of sympathetic nervous system activity necessary for sleep onset. Czeisler also suggested that light and electromagnetic transmissions from devices could contribute to sleep disturbances.

Other researchers pointed to the influence of light exposure on sleep patterns, citing the role of photoreceptors in the retina that regulate melatonin production. Melatonin, released by the pineal gland, typically peaks during the night and decreases in the morning, controlled by daily light-dark cycles. Abnormalities in these cycles can lead to shift-work sleep disorder.

To investigate the specific impact of screen-based stimulation on sleep, Czeisler and colleagues conducted a study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. They recruited 12 participants who lived in a monitoring unit at Boston's Brigham and Women's Hospital for two weeks. Participants were divided into two groups: one read on an iPad for four hours before bed, while the other read printed books in dim light. The groups then switched roles after a week.

Results showed that iPad users reported feeling less sleepy and less alert the next morning, despite getting eight hours of sleep. They also took longer to fall asleep and spent less time in the REM phase of sleep. Blood tests revealed that melatonin secretion was delayed by an hour and a half in iPad users. The study's lead author, Anne-Marie Chang, noted that similar results would be expected from other devices emitting similar blue light patterns, such as tablets, phones, and laptops.

Czeisler emphasized that the study's findings suggest that the impact of screens on sleep is more profound than previously thought, potentially leading to long-term health consequences, including increased cancer risk, obesity, and diabetes. He argued that electronic devices should undergo health evaluations similar to those required for pharmaceuticals.

A separate study published in the same journal found that nearly 90% of adults and 75% of children have at least one electronic device in their bedrooms, with many reporting multiple devices. This widespread use of electronic devices in bedrooms might exacerbate sleep disruptions, particularly among older teenagers who are more likely to use devices at night.

Overall, the research suggests that reading on electronic devices before bed can disrupt the body's circadian clock, making it harder to fall asleep and become alert in the morning. The type of short-wavelength enriched light emitted by devices, rather than cognitive stimulation, appears to be the primary culprit behind these sleep disturbances.

reference: – Your iPad or smartphone could well be hurting your sleep patterns, even if you're getting in a full eight hours of shuteye, researchers say—and the effects could be long-term. Over a five-night period, researchers in Boston had some subjects read for four hours a night on an iPad; others did their reading on printed books, with the lights low, the Washington Post reports. Among members of the iPad group, levels of melatonin, a chemical behind sleepiness, decreased. It took them longer to drift off by an average of 10 minutes, the Wall Street Journal reports. And once they did, their periods of rapid eye-movement sleep were shorter. The effects weren't limited to the nighttime. Their circadian rhythms were affected, and the next day—even if they'd slept eight hours—they reported being more tired. And in the long term, reduced melatonin may boost the risk of various forms of cancer (prompting the rather frightening headline "Reading an iPad in Bed May Increase Cancer Risk," the Atlantic notes). At issue is what's known as "blue light," the kind often given off by backlit gadgets, the Journal adds. "We introduce these devices that have medical and biological effects without requiring any health studies on their impact ... They don't have to go through any evaluation like a drug would, for safety and efficacy," says a researcher. "I think it's time to rethink that." (As far as sleep goes, here's your "optimal" amount.)

final_summary:
Jonas Bevacqua, the 34-year-old co-founder and creative director of the Irvine-based clothing company LRG (Lifted Research Group), was found dead in his Laguna Beach home on a Monday morning. The Orange County Coroner's Office confirmed the death, but the cause remains undetermined pending further investigation. The Laguna Beach Police Department stated that the death appears to be non-suspicious.

LRG, which stands for Lifted Research Group, was founded by Bevacqua and his business partner Robert Wright in 1999. The company quickly gained popularity among young people, particularly in the skateboarding, surfing, and hip-hop communities. LRG's designs often featured witty messages, humor, and references to an elevated state of mind, reflecting the diverse cultural influences of its founders. Bevacqua himself drew inspiration from his unique upbringing, having grown up in a multicultural household with seven siblings, including brothers and sisters of various ethnicities.

Bevacqua's entrepreneurial journey began after dropping out of college, where he spent only two semesters before drifting through California, working odd jobs and honing his skills as a DJ. He eventually returned to his parents' home in Laguna Beach, where he started LRG with Wright, whom he met while working as a DJ. With initial funding from friends of his adoptive father, they began creating clothes that catered to their interests in skateboarding, surfing, and hip-hop, filling a gap in the market that they felt existed.

Under Bevacqua's leadership, LRG experienced rapid growth, reaching annual sales of $150 million by 2006 and ranking fifth on Entrepreneur magazine's list of the 500 fastest-growing companies that year. The company expanded beyond clothing to sell electronic devices, music, and other items, positioning itself as a "creative lifestyle" brand that reflects its founders' passions for fashion, environmental causes, and other pursuits. LRG also sponsored a skateboarding team and promoted underground recording artists.

Bevacqua's personal life included a fiancée, a son, and seven siblings, as well as a strong sense of social responsibility. He often referenced his own adoption and the importance of giving back, citing his company's slogan "Adopt Children, Not Style." Bevacqua's legacy extends beyond his business success, as he leaves behind a community of fans and supporters who admired his creativity, passion, and commitment to his values.

reference: – The co-founder of the hugely popular hip-hop and skateboarding clothing brand LRG has been found dead in his Laguna Beach home. Jonas Bevacqua was 34. There was no obvious cause of death, and an autopsy will be conducted, AP reports. LRG, which the designer founded when he was barely out of his teens, branched out from clothing into music and other items, which supported up-and-coming recording artists. Bevacqua, who grew up in a multi-racial California family, said he founded LRG because no other company was creating clothing that reflected his and co-founder Robert Wright's interest in surfing, skateboarding, and hip-hop. "I grew up in a pretty unique environment and was exposed to a lot of different things," Bevacqua told the Orange County Register in 2009. "I didn't feel there was a clothing company to bridge the gap among all these different things that we were into—that spoke for that melting pot of what was going on. That's what LRG was all about."

final_summary:
Donald Trump has nominated former Texas Governor Rick Perry to lead the Department of Energy, an agency he once vowed to abolish and famously forgot the name of during a 2011 presidential debate. Perry, a climate skeptic and former 2016 presidential primary rival, is viewed by conservatives as having management experience and a willingness to question the agency's status quo. 

Perry's nomination has raised eyebrows due to his past statements on climate change, which he has described as "unsettled" and a "phony mess." He has also questioned the scientific consensus on human-caused climate change, comparing it to the persecution of Galileo by the Catholic Church. As governor of Texas, Perry oversaw a significant expansion of wind power, but he has also expressed opposition to extending the federal tax credit for wind power and has refused to accept the scientific consensus on climate change.

Perry's ties to the fossil fuel industry are also a concern, as he sits on the board of Energy Transfer Partners, the company behind the Dakota Access Pipeline. The pipeline has faced intense opposition from environmental groups and Native American tribes, who argue that it poses a risk to their water supply and sacred lands.

As Energy Secretary, Perry would oversee an agency responsible for maintaining the nation's nuclear arsenal, regulating energy production and consumption, and advancing renewable energy technologies. The agency has played a crucial role in the Obama administration's efforts to address climate change, including the Iran nuclear deal and the promotion of green energy projects.

Perry's nomination has sparked alarm from environmental groups, who fear that he will shift the department away from renewable energy and toward fossil fuels. The League of Conservation Voters has described Perry as "completely unfit" for the position, citing his climate denial and ties to the fossil fuel industry.

In contrast, some supporters of Perry argue that he has a track record of balancing environmental regulations with domestic energy production. As governor of Texas, Perry implemented policies that encouraged the growth of wind energy, and he has expressed support for the development of renewable energy technologies.

The Department of Energy has undergone significant changes since its creation in 1977, shifting from a focus on weapons maintenance and regulations to a broader mission that includes addressing climate change. The agency has invested billions of dollars in renewable energy projects, including loans and loan guarantees for companies like Tesla and Google.

If confirmed, Perry would join a Cabinet that has been marked by controversy and polarization. His nomination is the latest example of Trump's willingness to defy conventional wisdom and challenge established norms in his selection of Cabinet nominees.

reference: – News that Donald Trump has settled on Rick Perry to run the Energy Department prompted a slew of stories Tuesday with one common refrain, and the former Texas governor must be thrilled with it. Here's a sampling: NBC News: "President-elect Donald Trump has selected former Texas Gov. Rick Perry—who famously once forgot that he wanted to abolish the Energy Department—to be secretary of energy ..." New York Times: "Trump plans to name former Gov. Rick Perry of Texas to lead the Energy Department, elevating a man who once could not remember the name of the agency he wanted to eliminate to the cabinet post, secretary of energy, that will run it." Washington Post: "Trump has picked Rick Perry to head the Energy Department, said two people familiar with the decision, seeking to put the former Texas governor in control of an agency whose name he forgot during a presidential debate even as he vowed to abolish it." Politico: "On a presidential debate stage five years ago, Rick Perry blanked on the Energy Department's name when trying to include it in a list of agencies he promised to abolish—memorably concluding with 'oops.' Now Trump has chosen the former Texas governor ..." ABC News: "Trump is expected to choose a man to lead the Department of Energy who infamously forgot its name." BBC: "Trump is expected to pick ex-Texas Governor Rick Perry as his energy secretary, a department whose name he famously forgot in a TV debate." NPR: "It's a good thing former Texas Gov. Rick Perry once forgot he wanted to eliminate the Department of Energy, because President-elect Donald Trump is nominating him to lead the agency. That's according to reports by multiple news outlets." And so on, even here.

final_summary:
Prime Minister David Cameron is facing a critical 18-week period leading up to the general election, where he must convince voters that he is the best candidate to lead the country. Despite a recent domestic emergency involving unblocking drains at his Oxfordshire home, Cameron remains confident in his ability to tackle the nation's economic challenges. He emphasizes that the choice between him and Labour leader Ed Miliband is a straightforward one - competence versus chaos. 

Cameron vows to introduce reforming legislation on education and welfare within the first 50 days of a new term, if re-elected. He warns German Chancellor Angela Merkel that he is prepared to take Britain out of the EU if necessary, stating that he rules out nothing if his demands for greater control over certain policy areas are not met. 

In a personal capacity, Cameron has enjoyed a relatively quiet Christmas, marked by quality time with his family, including his wife Samantha, whom he credits with providing valuable counsel on difficult decisions. He describes their marriage as built on mutual love and respect, with Samantha possessing excellent judgment and being his trusted advisor. 

Cameron's relationship with US President Barack Obama is close, with the latter referring to him affectionately as "bro." The Prime Minister attributes the strength of the special relationship between the two nations to shared values and a deepening friendship. 

Cameron's campaign focuses primarily on the economy, highlighting the £13.5 billion in additional interest payments that Labour's budget plans would incur. He contrasts this with the Conservative Party's approach, emphasizing responsible stewardship of the economy and a commitment to reducing debt. 

While Cameron praises his colleagues, including Boris Johnson and Theresa May, he has taken steps to manage potential leadership challenges within the party. He authorized the removal of May's aide, Nick Timothy, from the Tory candidates list due to non-participation in the Rochester by-election. Cameron also expresses caution regarding Johnson's potential return to Westminster, suggesting that he will need to consider opportunities for the London Mayor in the future. 

As the election approaches, Cameron recognizes the increasing importance of social media in shaping public opinion. He acknowledges the risks associated with relying on selfies and other forms of digital engagement, but sees them as a means to connect directly with voters and convey his message effectively.

final_summary:
Susan Schneider Williams, wife of the late actor Robin Williams, shares her personal account of living with and caring for her husband during his battle with Lewy Body Disease (LBD), a lesser-known neurodegenerative disorder that affects approximately 1.5 million Americans nationwide. In a heartfelt letter, she recounts the harrowing experience of watching her husband struggle with increasingly debilitating symptoms, including paranoia, delusions, insomnia, memory loss, and anxiety, which ultimately led to his tragic death in 2014.

Robin Williams was first diagnosed with Parkinson's disease (PD) in May 2014, but the autopsy report revealed that he actually suffered from diffuse Lewy Body Disease, a condition characterized by the accumulation of abnormal protein clumps called Lewy bodies in the brain. The disease's progression was marked by a rapid decline in cognitive and motor functions, despite his efforts to manage symptoms through various treatments, including therapy, medication, and lifestyle changes.

Throughout his illness, Robin's behavior became increasingly erratic, with episodes of paranoia, delusions, and anxiety that were exacerbated by the unpredictability of the disease. His wife, Susan, describes the emotional toll of witnessing her husband's struggles, stating that she felt "powerless" and "frozen" in the face of his deteriorating health. Despite her best efforts to support him, she couldn't shake the feeling that she was losing the man she loved.

As the disease progressed, Robin's symptoms worsened, including a persistent tremor, difficulty with speech and movement, and a gradual loss of cognitive function. His sleep deprivation became a significant concern, leading to a recommendation that they sleep apart to ensure they both got adequate rest. Tragically, Robin passed away on August 11, 2014, leaving behind a grieving widow and a legacy of awareness about the devastating effects of LBD.

In the aftermath of her husband's passing, Susan dedicated herself to learning more about the disease that took his life. She met with medical professionals who reviewed his medical records and brain scans, and they concurred that his was one of the worst cases of LBD they had seen. Her research led her to the American Academy of Neurology and the American Brain Foundation, where she now serves on the Board of Directors.

Through her experiences, Susan implores researchers and scientists to continue their work towards finding a cure for LBD and other brain diseases. She believes that Robin's suffering should not be in vain and that his story can inspire others to pursue breakthroughs in the field. Her letter serves as a poignant reminder of the importance of continued research and advocacy in the fight against neurodegenerative disorders.

reference: – In what Discover calls a "unique and touching paper" published in the journal Neurology Thursday, Robin Williams' widow shares the heartbreaking story of her husband's final 10 months and asks neurologists not to give up in their quest toward brain disease cures. Susan Schneider Williams refers to Lewy Body disease, the neurodegenerative disorder that ultimately led to Williams' suicide in August 2014, as "the terrorist inside my husband's brain." She describes how, toward the end of his life, "Robin was losing his mind and he was aware of it." He was struggling with paranoia, anxiety, delusions, insomnia, and other symptoms both physical and mental, and for months, he and his wife could get no answers about what was happening to him. He was ultimately diagnosed with Parkinson's disease, but as his symptoms continued to worsen, he grew "weary" and it felt like they were both "drowning." Then, ultimately, he took his own life. It was only after his death that an autopsy revealed Williams actually had Lewy Body disease—his clinical symptoms mirrored Parkinson's, but his brain pathology showed that almost all of the neurons throughout his brain and brainstem had been besieged by Lewy bodies, Schneider Williams writes. When she found out, she wasn't surprised: "The mere fact that something had invaded nearly every region of my husband's brain made perfect sense to me," she writes. She has spent the time since Williams' death learning all she can about Lewy Body disease, for which there is no cure, and she now serves on the Board of Directors for the American Brain Foundation. Her full piece is worth a read.

final_summary:
Frank Romano, a man traveling with his 6-year-old Bull Terrier, Ty, experienced a harrowing ordeal when Ty went missing during a flight from Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) to Tampa on a Delta Airlines flight. According to Romano, Ty was placed in a carrier purchased from PetCo in the cargo area, but an hour before departure, a Delta employee informed Romano that Ty had chewed through the metal door and escaped. However, Romano expressed skepticism about the explanation, citing the strength required to bite through the kennel's hard plastic and metal components.

The incident sparked concerns about Delta's handling of pets in transit, revealing a pattern of mishaps involving the airline. In 2011, a New York man's puppy was temporarily misplaced during a flight from Alabama to New York, while a German Shepherd heading to Germany got loose in Atlanta and was later killed after being struck by a vehicle. Another instance involved a couple who adopted a stray dog in Mexico, which Delta claimed had escaped, but ultimately failed to produce the dog.

Delta's issues extend beyond pet losses; in 2010, the airline mistakenly sent two children to different destinations – one to Boston and the other to Cleveland. Furthermore, a puppy destined for Maine was flown to California instead, with Delta providing little information about the circumstances surrounding the error.

In response to the criticism, Delta issued a statement acknowledging the incident and promising to investigate. However, the airline's track record suggests a lack of accountability and transparency in addressing pet-related incidents. Despite multiple instances of lost or mishandled pets, Delta has often provided inadequate compensation, such as offering vouchers or refunds, rather than taking concrete steps to rectify the situations.

A Change.org petition with over 2,000 signatures demands that Delta takes responsibility for its actions and updates its policies and procedures regarding animal transportation. The case of Frank Romano's missing dog, Ty, remains unresolved, highlighting the need for greater accountability and improved protocols within the airline industry to ensure the safety and well-being of animals in transit.

reference: – A man is trying to find his best friend after Delta Airlines lost track of the 6-year-old bull terrier Friday night. "I keep praying and wishing for him to come back," Frank Romano tells ABC News. Delta has told him they're looking for the dog, Ty, who seems to have gone missing at LAX when Romano and his family were headed to Tampa. After Romano boarded the plane, Delta staff told him Ty was missing, having bitten through the door of his kennel. Romano finds that confusing "when it had a metal door and was made of hard plastic," he tells the LAist. He searched the airport without finding the dog. The next day, he and his family headed to Tampa, where he is now. Meanwhile, he says he's received mixed reports from Delta, who at first told him they'd found the dog; then, "a few minutes later," said they hadn't. "They give us the run-around," he says. "Even gave us a number to the cargo place at Tampa airport saying he was there. We called and (they) said they had no dogs." Delta has lost enough dogs in the past to prompt a Change.org petition, LAist notes, pointing to three different incidents. "If I could I would go back to LA in a heartbeat," Romano says. "I bet I could find Ty faster than Delta can."

final_summary:
In a candid interview with Access Hollywood, Demi Lovato has shared her harrowing experiences with substance abuse, revealing that she struggled with cocaine addiction and hid her habit by smuggling the drug onto airplanes and sneaking away to use it. At just 19 years old, Lovato reached rock bottom after checking into a rehab facility in Illinois for "emotional and physical issues" in 2010. She confessed that she required cocaine every 30 minutes to an hour and would often sneak away to the bathroom to use it, even while accompanied by a sober companion.

Lovato's addiction led her to engage in manipulative behavior, lying to those around her to conceal her struggles. She recalled bringing a Sprite bottle filled with vodka to the airport at 9 am, throwing up in the car, and still managing to convince others that everything was fine. This behavior was a stark contrast to her public image as a young star on the rise.

Lovato's mother, Dianna, also spoke candidly about her own struggles with addiction, revealing that she had been in denial about her daughter's substance abuse. Dianna had suspected that Demi was using drugs but chose to ignore the signs, hoping against hope that her daughter was telling the truth. However, after Demi's 2010 rehab stint, the two grew closer as a family, with Dianna seeking help for her own eating disorder and depression.

Lovato's struggles with addiction began long before her teenage years, with her developing an eating disorder at around age 8 or 9. She described a cycle of overeating, followed by starvation and purging, which took a devastating toll on her physical and mental health. Lovato eventually sought help and has since spoken publicly about the importance of addressing mental health issues.

Following their respective recoveries, Demi and her mother have become closer and more supportive of each other. Lovato expressed her gratitude for her mother's support, stating, "I'm so... proud of her it makes me so happy. I love her so much."

final_summary:
A manhunt is underway across the US states of Florida and Alabama for 44-year-old William "Billy" Boyette Jr. and his 38-year-old accomplice Mary Rice, who are suspected of embarking on a multi-state killing spree that has claimed the lives of three women and left one critically injured. The investigation began after the discovery of two bodies at the Emerald Sands Inn in Milton, Florida on January 31, where 30-year-old Alicia Greer and 49-year-old Jacqueline Moore were found deceased. 

The pair is believed to have targeted their victims seemingly randomly, with no apparent connections between the victims and the suspects. On February 3, Boyette allegedly murdered 52-year-old Peggy Broz at her home in Lillian, Alabama, and stole her vehicle. The following day, Boyette shot 28-year-old Kayla Crocker in a home invasion in Beulah, Florida, leaving her critically injured and stealing her car.

Evidence suggests that Boyette and Rice have been evading capture by changing their appearances - Boyette is believed to have shaved his head and goatee, while Rice dyed her hair orange. They were spotted at a Hardee's restaurant in the early hours of February 6, shortly after the home invasion. The pair is thought to be driving a 2006 white Chevy Cobalt with a skull and crossbones sticker, a damaged right rear passenger door, and a Florida license plate number 9613BJ.

Rice was initially considered a person of interest in the attacks but was upgraded to a suspect on February 6, after authorities gathered sufficient evidence pointing to her involvement. The Escambia County Sheriff's Office has increased the number of deputies on the streets and is urging residents to remain vigilant, describing Boyette as "armed and extremely dangerous." 

Boyette has a history of violent behavior, with a string of arrests dating back to 2002 in Santa Rosa County, including charges related to domestic violence, drug trafficking, and assault. He was released from jail just four months prior to the start of the current killing spree. In previous incidents, Boyette has been accused of threatening to kill his partners and physically abusing them, often using intimidation tactics to silence them.

Law enforcement officials are taking Boyette's threats to evade capture seriously, given his history of making statements that he will not be taken alive. Deputies are searching a wooded area near Nine Mile Road, where footage shows Boyette and Rice, and are utilizing dogs to search for potential encampments. The sheriff's office has offered a reward of $10,000 to anyone providing information leading to the capture of the duo.

As the search continues, authorities are warning residents to exercise extreme caution and to contact the police immediately if they spot Boyette or Rice.

reference: – Florida is facing a "running nightmare" with two people on a killing spree that has so far left three women dead and another in critical condition. William "Billy" Boyette and Mary Craig Rice are wanted in the Jan. 31 murders of 30-year-old Alicia Greer—who was in a relationship with Boyette—and 39-year-old Jacqueline Jeanette Moore at a hotel in Milton. Police believe the suspects then drove to Lillian, Ala., killed 52-year-old Peggy Broz in her front yard, and fled with her vehicle on Friday. In the latest incident, the pair are accused of shooting 28-year-old Kayla Crocker and stealing her vehicle in Pensacola on Monday. Crocker was taken to a hospital in critical condition but is expected to survive, reports the Pensacola News Journal. Authorities say Boyette, 44, and Rice, 38, have managed to elude capture by staying "very mobile." They're believed to have camped in wooded areas; two photos circulated by police show the pair walking in woods with supplies and an apparent handgun, reports WKRG. "What we are experiencing is a running nightmare, quite honestly," a police rep says, noting all victims apart from Greer appear to have been selected at random. Another adds Boyette—accused of stabbing and strangling a girlfriend in 2015—has told "many, many people that he will not be taken alive." Authorities "take those threats and those admonitions very seriously," the rep says. Police say the couple are armed and dangerous and should not be approached.

final_summary:
A recent study conducted by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and the Pew Charitable Trusts reveals that nearly a third of antibiotics prescribed in outpatient settings in the United States are unnecessary, translating to around 47 million prescriptions annually. The study, which analyzed data from 2010 to 2011, found that the majority of these unnecessary prescriptions are for conditions that do not respond to antibiotics, such as colds, sore throats, bronchitis, flu, and other viral illnesses.

According to the study, approximately 13% of all outpatient visits in the US result in an antibiotic prescription, with over 40% of these prescriptions being written to treat patients with acute respiratory conditions. However, half of these prescriptions are deemed unnecessary due to the viral nature of the illnesses. The study's lead author, Katherine Fleming-Dutra, attributes the overprescription of antibiotics to clinician concerns about patient satisfaction and the pressure from patients or parents demanding antibiotics.

The study's findings have significant implications for the effectiveness of antibiotics, as the overuse of these life-saving drugs contributes to the development of drug-resistant bacteria. The CDC has warned that the overuse of antibiotics has led to the rise of "nightmare bacteria" that are increasingly resistant to even the strongest antibiotics, posing a growing threat to hospitals and nursing homes nationwide.

In response to the study's findings, the White House had previously set a target of reducing inappropriate antibiotic use in outpatient settings by 50% by 2020. According to the study, achieving this goal would require a 15% reduction in overall outpatient antibiotic use, resulting in approximately 23 million fewer antibiotics prescribed annually by 2020.

Experts emphasize that better communication between healthcare providers and patients can lead to more appropriate prescribing practices. Simple steps, such as displaying posters in patient waiting rooms, can also help reduce antibiotic overuse. The study's findings highlight the need for a concerted effort to address the issue of antibiotic overuse and promote responsible prescribing practices in outpatient settings.

The study's data also underscores the importance of addressing the root causes of antibiotic overuse, including clinician concerns about patient satisfaction and patient demands for antibiotics. By understanding these factors and implementing targeted interventions, healthcare providers can work towards reducing unnecessary antibiotic prescriptions and preserving the effectiveness of these life-saving medications.

The study's findings have sparked renewed calls for action to address the issue of antibiotic overuse. Experts stress that reducing unnecessary antibiotic use is crucial to slowing the emergence of antibiotic-resistant microbes and preserving the effectiveness of these critical medications. As the CDC continues to monitor the issue, policymakers and healthcare providers are urged to prioritize responsible prescribing practices and work towards achieving the goal of reducing inappropriate antibiotic use in outpatient settings.

reference: – Scientists have been concerned for some time about "Phantom Menace"-type superbugs that aren't fazed by meds. The latest CDC data finds that 2 million people a year in the US are infected with bacteria resistant to antibiotics, and at least 23,000 die directly from those infections. And a new report from the CDC and the Pew Charitable Trusts—the first research to quantify this issue, per the Washington Post—isn't encouraging. A study published Tuesday in JAMA finds nearly one in three antibiotic prescriptions (around 47 million per year) are given needlessly to people with viruses such as bronchitis and the common cold, as well as for ear and sinus infections and sore throats not caused by bacterial infections—meaning the Rx not only won't vanquish their illness, but can potentially build up antibiotic resistance, the St. Louis Post-Dispatch reports. Besides the drug resistance, overuse can up the risk for drug allergies and even an unpleasant-sounding diarrhea that can be fatal. The researchers analyzed 184,032 outpatient visits to ERs and medical clinics in 2010 and 2011 and found that 12.6% walked out with an antibiotic script, per the Pharmacy Times. Out of an estimated annual antibiotic-prescription rate of 506 per every 1,000 people, only about 353 of those who received prescriptions should likely have received them. The Times notes President Obama's initiative to cut down on unnecessary outpatient antibiotic use by 50% by 2020; researchers say that, based on this study, we'll need to slash overall antibiotic use by 15% to meet that. "If we continue down the road of inappropriate use we'll lose the most powerful tool we have to fight life-threatening infections," CDC Director Tom Frieden says. "Losing these antibiotics would undermine our ability to treat patients with deadly infections [and] cancer, provide organ transplants, and save victims of burns and trauma." (Here's hoping teixobactin can help us out with superbugs.)

final_summary:
A bizarre incident unfolded in the small mining town of Newman, Western Australia, where a 37-year-old man allegedly taxied a Beechcraft two-seater prop-driven plane without wings and a steering wheel down the main street to the local pub, the Newman Hotel, also known as the Purple Pub. The incident occurred on October 31st at approximately 2:10 pm, during a busy Friday afternoon when children were walking home from school. 

Eyewitnesses reported that the plane, which had its propeller running, was seen making its way from one end of the town to the other, eventually coming to rest outside the pub. Local resident Beau Woolcock witnessed the scene unfold, stating that he and his five-year-old daughter watched as the plane was towed away. Woolcock described the surreal moment, saying that even a police officer poked his head into the plane as if asking for a license, adding to the absurdity of the situation.

Police were alerted to the incident at 2:00 pm and upon arrival, found the plane parked in one of the pub's bays with no one inside. The officers spoke to the plane's owner, who claimed to have purchased the aircraft from someone else in town and was taking it home before stopping at the pub. However, an investigation revealed that the plane's fuel line was attached to a jerry can in the cabin, allowing it to continue running even after the engine was turned off.

The plane's lack of wings and steering wheel made the situation extremely hazardous, particularly given the presence of children in the area. Newman Sergeant Mark Garner stated that the incident was being taken very seriously due to the potential risks involved. The police obtained CCTV footage and interviewed the plane's owner, who was subsequently charged with an act likely to endanger the life, health, or safety of a person. The man, who does not hold a pilot's license, will face court on November 18th to answer the charges.
