reference: – Buying a plane must be thirsty work: Police in Newman, Western Australia, say a man who had just purchased a two-seater Beechcraft decided to drive it home and stop for a beer at a pub on the main street on the way. A resident says he was astounded to see the plane, which had no wings or steering wheel, parked outside the Purple Pub. Even more special was "seeing one of the local coppers poking his head in the door of the plane looking like he was asking for a license," he tells ABC Radio. "Even though no one was in the plane at the time, I was like, what the hell?" After some initial puzzlement over what, if anything, the driver should be charged with, police settled on a charge of "endangering life, health, or safety," reports WAToday. A police spokesman says the incident is being taken very seriously because there were children walking home from school at the time that the man—who doesn't have a pilot's license—was taxiing through the town, using the plane's foot pedals to steer. He passed a breath test at the scene, but police decided to impound the plane.

final_summary:
Thomas Keith Henderson, a 56-year-old employee of the West Virginia Department of Transportation, has been arrested and charged with driving a state snow plow while intoxicated. On January 29th, around 11:30 pm, Deputy R.L. Jones from the McDowell County Sheriff's Department pulled over the snow plow on Route 6 at the top of Burke Mountain near Keystone after noticing one of its headlights was not functioning properly. Upon approaching the vehicle, Deputy Jones detected the presence of alcohol on Henderson's breath and administered a field sobriety test, which Henderson allegedly failed. 

Henderson was subsequently arrested and taken to the Welch Police Department where he underwent a breathalyzer test, resulting in a blood-alcohol content reading of 0.09. This exceeds the 0.04 legal limit for commercial driver's license holders, which is half the standard limit of 0.08 for non-commercial licenses. Henderson was later arraigned on January 30th and released on a $500 bond.

reference: – DUI charges are bad enough. DUI charges while driving a state snow plow are liable to make a few headlines. That's the predicament for 56-year-old Thomas Henderson of West Virginia, who got pulled over by a deputy atop a mountain late one night this week, reports WOWK-TV. The deputy stopped the plow because one of its headlights was out, but then he smelled alcohol. Henderson failed a field sobriety test, then blew a .09, which is above the state's regular limit of .08, let alone the stricter limit of .04 for commercial drivers, notes AP.

final_summary:
The Los Angeles County Coroner's office has completed the autopsy on actress Brittany Murphy, 32, who died from a sudden cardiac arrest on Sunday. According to sources close to the investigation, the autopsy revealed no signs of foul play or trauma to the body, and it appeared "normal." However, the cause of death remains pending further testing, including toxicology reports and histopathology studies of her organs and tissues, as well as neuropathology studies of her brain.

The Coroner's office has stated that they will not determine the cause of death until these additional reports are received, which could take up to six weeks. One area of interest for the Coroner is the presence of prescription medications in Murphy's system, as she had been taking medication for various medical conditions.

According to law enforcement sources, Murphy suffered internal bleeding prior to her death, although it is unclear whether this was related to an underlying medical condition. The Los Angeles Police Department has concluded its initial investigation into Murphy's death, finding no evidence of a crime having occurred.

Murphy's death has sparked speculation about the potential role of prescription medications in her passing, given that she was taking them for various health issues. The Coroner's office will continue to investigate the circumstances surrounding her death, awaiting the results of the toxicology report and other tests to determine the cause of her sudden cardiac arrest.

final_summary:
Steven Avery, a 43-year-old man from Manitowoc County, Wisconsin, was released from prison in 2003 after serving 18 years for a sexual assault conviction. His release was met with apologies and a welcoming homecoming in his local community, and he became a prominent figure in advocating for wrongful conviction reform. However, just eight months after his release, Avery was arrested and charged with the murder of 25-year-old photographer Teresa Halbach, whose body was found on his family's auto salvage yard property.

The investigation into Halbach's disappearance and murder uncovered evidence linking Avery to the crime scene, including blood from both Avery and Halbach found in her car, a car key belonging to Halbach discovered in Avery's trailer, and Avery's DNA on the key. The case drew widespread attention due to Avery's previous wrongful conviction and his subsequent advocacy work, with some experts suggesting that the new charges could undermine the credibility of the innocence movement.

Avery's lawyers, including Stephen M. Glynn, expressed shock and disappointment at the new allegations, citing the devastating impact on Avery's reputation and the potential consequences for his ongoing civil lawsuit against the former prosecutor and sheriff involved in his initial conviction. The lawsuit, which sought $36 million in damages, now faces uncertainty following Avery's arrest.

Experts from the Innocence Project, a non-profit organization dedicated to exonerating wrongfully convicted individuals, emphasized that Avery's case should not be linked to his earlier wrongful conviction and that the organization remains committed to its mission of finding truth and punishing those responsible for crimes. However, the backlash against the Wisconsin Innocence Project has been significant, with some critics arguing that their efforts may have inadvertently contributed to Avery's alleged involvement in Halbach's murder.

Avery's family members, including his parents and brother, maintain his innocence, claiming that the evidence against him was planted. The family's claims are supported by some inconsistencies in the original investigation, including the use of questionable eyewitness testimony and the handling of DNA evidence. The case has sparked renewed debate about the reliability of eyewitness identification and the importance of proper DNA analysis in forensic investigations.

The arrest has also raised questions about the motivations behind the Manitowoc County authorities' actions, particularly given Avery's high-profile status as a wrongful conviction advocate. Some have suggested that Avery's civil lawsuit may have motivated the authorities to plant evidence against him in the second case. The case has also highlighted concerns about the treatment of Brendan Dassey, Avery's nephew, who was also implicated in the murder and has been criticized for being questioned alone by investigators despite his limited intellectual abilities.

The controversy surrounding Avery's case has also affected the legislative push for criminal justice reforms in Wisconsin, which was previously championed by Avery as a symbol of his fight for justice. The bill, known as the "Avery Bill," has been renamed the "Criminal Justice Reforms" package, and its passage seems uncertain in light of Avery's new charges. The case has left many in the community divided, with some residents expressing skepticism about Avery's guilt, while others believe he was wrongly accused once again.

reference: – Ten years before Making a Murderer fans started debating Steven Avery's case at just about every water cooler in the country, Monica Davey was just another reporter covering Avery's arrest on suspicion of the 2005 murder of Teresa Halbach in Manitowoc, Wis. The news came just as Avery was pursuing a $36 million civil lawsuit against a former prosecutor and sheriff, having recently been acquitted of a sexual assault for which he spent 18 years in prison. "There are 36 million reasons why they should be doing this to him," Davey quoted Avery's brother as saying in a 2005 New York Times article. It caught the eye of two graduate student filmmakers, who would spend the next decade creating Making a Murderer. The series has forced Manitowoc to relive its darkest history and has left the county transformed, Davey writes at the Times following a return visit. While the county tourism office used to field calls about jogging paths and beaches, callers now question why the office would want to draw visitors to such a town. The local police and sheriff's departments, city hall, and even the Manitowoc County Historical Society are forced to listen to "yelling, cussing, and swearing," says an official. As for Manitowoc residents, "the talkative, curious people I had come upon a decade earlier … avoided any talk about Making a Murderer, or simply spotted my notebook and walked away," writes Davey. One, however, said she was fearful of a rally in support of Avery, scheduled for Friday, per WBAY. Even Avery's mother, who ardently defends her son, says the calls for interviews are overwhelming. "I'm too old for this," she says. "It's too much." Read Davey's full piece here.

final_summary:
The search for Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 resumed in earnest on Saturday, with surveillance planes returning to the skies above a newly defined search area in the southern Indian Ocean. The Australian authorities stated that the search area had been shifted nearly 700 miles to the northeast of the previous location, following a new analysis of radar data from March 8, which revealed that the plane was moving faster than initially estimated. This led investigators to believe that the plane would have burned fuel more quickly and possibly fallen into the Indian Ocean farther north than previously thought.

Two aircraft from the Chinese People's Liberation Army Air Force and the Royal Australian Air Force spotted floating debris in the new search area on Saturday, marking the second consecutive day of sightings. However, officials cautioned that these findings were inconclusive on their own due to the presence of ordinary oceanic detritus. 

Five aircraft flying over the zone on Friday also spotted unidentified objects, but none of the items retrieved by crews on two ships were confirmed to be from the missing plane. The Australian Maritime Safety Authority emphasized that the search area, spanning approximately 319,000 square kilometers, presented both opportunities and challenges, with less inclement weather and water conditions compared to the previous area, but also being a smaller region to cover.

The revised search area is roughly the size of New Mexico or Poland, and the Australian and Malaysian authorities acknowledged that finding small pieces of wreckage in such a vast ocean would be a daunting task. Despite the difficulties, the search effort continued, with a flotilla of vessels arriving in the zone to assist in the search and identification of potential debris.

Meanwhile, a Chinese military plane scanning part of the search zone spotted several objects floating in the sea, including two bearing colors similar to those of the missing jet. However, it remains unclear whether these objects are related to the investigation into the disappearance of Malaysia Airlines Flight 370.

In response to the shifting search area, dozens of relatives of passengers on the missing plane were set to fly from Beijing to Kuala Lumpur on Sunday to demand answers from the Malaysian government regarding the fate of their loved ones. The relatives have expressed frustration with the Malaysian authorities, accusing them of withholding information and not doing enough to find the plane.

Malaysia Airlines' commercial director, Hugh Dunleavy, stated that around 40 to 45 Chinese relatives of passengers would travel to Kuala Lumpur to seek more information. The relatives have requested meetings with the prime minister and transportation minister to ask questions about the disappearance of the plane.

If investigators can confirm that the plane went down in the newly targeted search zone, recovering the flight data and cockpit voice recorders – commonly referred to as the black boxes – could be complicated due to the depth of the sea floor, reaching up to 6,000 meters in some areas. The black boxes are designed to preserve critical data for two years or longer, even if submerged in saltwater, and can be cleaned and dried to retrieve the stored information.

The National Transportation Safety Board's Joe Kolly explained that the recorders can withstand saltwater immersion up to 20,000 feet, and the deepest waters of the new search zone for Flight 370 are approximately 13,000 feet. The recovery of the black boxes would be a significant step towards understanding the circumstances surrounding the disappearance of Malaysia Airlines Flight 370.

reference: – The search for Flight 370 in its new search zone continues to produce tantalizing sightings, but no links to the plane so far. The latest comes from a Chinese military plane that spotted three suspicious objects today with colors that were at least a rough match for those of the Malaysian jet, reports AP. Australian and Chinese ships were retrieving those and other pieces of debris as quickly as possible for analysis, but it's no easy feat. "It’s an inaccessible place," Australian Prime Minister Tony Abbott told the media, reports the New York Times. "We are trying to find small bits of wreckage in a vast ocean, and while we are throwing everything we have at it, the task goes on.” NBC News, meanwhile, offers a glimmer of hope in regard to figuring out what happened: The plane's black boxes should be able to survive about two years even if submerged in salt water 20,000 feet deep, it says. The deepest part of the new search area is about 13,000 feet.

final_summary:
Daivon Reeder, a 22-year-old from Detroit, is set to graduate from Eastern Michigan University with a degree in Criminal Justice and a minor in Military Science, becoming the first member of his family to achieve this milestone. Despite facing numerous challenges throughout his academic journey, including losing a scholarship, attending basic training for the Army, and dealing with family problems, Reeder persevered and worked multiple jobs to support his family. He also lacked a father figure growing up, which led him to form an organization with nine other students to mentor young boys and teach them valuable life lessons.

Reeder's determination and resilience have earned him widespread recognition on social media, where his graduation announcement has gone viral. The post, which includes a photo of himself smiling, is a direct response to his stepfather's earlier prediction that he wouldn't graduate from college. In a tweet, Reeder wrote, "My stepdad told me it was pointless to go to orientation I wasn't going to graduate anyway. 4 years later he is in jail and I am well..."

The tweet has been retweeted and shared thousands of times, with many congratulating Reeder on proving his stepfather wrong. When asked about the reaction, Reeder expressed excitement and gratitude, saying, "It's exciting, it really is." However, he acknowledges that his journey was not without its struggles, citing racism as another obstacle he faced as a black male student in a predominantly white university.

Despite these challenges, Reeder remains optimistic and encourages others to follow in his footsteps. He believes that anyone can overcome adversity and make a positive impact, stating, "Just because somebody says you can't do something seems hard or it's not the norm, or it's something your family is not used to doing, you can be the change. You can be the difference."

On Saturday, Reeder will walk with his entire EMU class and officially receive his diploma, marking a significant achievement in his life. His story serves as a testament to the power of perseverance and determination, inspiring others to chase their dreams despite the odds against them.

reference: – In a moment of reflection on the eve of graduating from Eastern Michigan University, student Daivon Reeder struck a chord on social media with a tweet that has since gone viral. Alongside a photo in his cap and gown, Reeder wrote Thursday: "My step dad told me it was pointless to go to orientation, I wasn't going to graduate.....4 years later he in jail & I'm well....," followed by a line of laughing emojis. Hours later, the tweet began trending in Detroit, catching the attention of Twitter as well as the media. Local Detroit station WDIV 4 featured a segment on Reeder, who graduated Saturday with a degree in criminal justice and a minor in military science. He told the station that after losing his academic scholarship, those words pushed him even harder to succeed. "[I have] the average, typical, inner-city Detroit boy story," Reeder, 22, says. "I grew up constantly moving houses." The eldest of four, Reeder said his mother struggled to make ends meet for his family, and his stepfather was not always in the picture. After his stepdad made the comment about graduating, "I kind of heard that and I was like OK, I'll show you," Reeder tells Fox 8. "I'm just a first generation college kid from Detroit trying to beat the odds," he tells BuzzFeed News. "I guess people can relate to a humble beginning." The tweet had more than 600,000 likes and 148,000 retweets as of Monday afternoon. (This tweet made waves for a different reason.)

final_summary:
In a groundbreaking move, tech giant Amazon, conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway, and banking powerhouse JPMorgan Chase announced a historic partnership aimed at revolutionizing the US healthcare system. The joint venture, set to be an independent company, seeks to simplify, improve, and reduce the costs associated with healthcare for the approximately 1.1 million employees of the three corporations. 

According to Warren Buffett, Berkshire Hathaway's CEO, the ballooning costs of healthcare pose a significant threat to the American economy, likening them to a "hungry tapeworm." He emphasized that the trio does not claim to have all the answers but is committed to tackling the issue with a collaborative approach. The new company will operate independently, free from profit-driven motivations, focusing initially on developing technology solutions to streamline healthcare services.

The partnership marks a significant shift in the healthcare landscape, particularly given Amazon's recent forays into the sector. As a company known for disrupting various industries, Amazon's entry into healthcare has been eagerly anticipated by investors and experts alike. The collaboration aims to leverage the combined strengths of the three partners – Amazon's technological prowess, Berkshire Hathaway's financial muscle, and JPMorgan Chase's extensive experience in healthcare administration.

JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon highlighted the importance of providing employees with transparency, knowledge, and control over their healthcare, emphasizing the potential for the initiative to extend beyond the participating companies and benefit the broader American public. The exact scope and goals of the project remain unclear, with the leadership team consisting of Todd Combs from Berkshire Hathaway, Marvelle Sullivan Berchtold from JPMorgan Chase, and Beth Galetti from Amazon.

Industry analysts and experts have welcomed the development, acknowledging the pressing need for innovation in the US healthcare system. According to Adam Fein, president of Pembroke Consulting, it is long overdue for large employers to drive change in the sector. While some experts express skepticism regarding the feasibility of achieving significant cost savings and improved outcomes, others see potential for the partnership to become a game-changer in the industry.

The announcement sent shockwaves through the healthcare sector, with shares of major healthcare companies experiencing significant declines. Despite the uncertainty surrounding the project's specifics, the partnership represents a bold step towards addressing the complexities and inefficiencies plaguing the US healthcare system.

reference: – "The ballooning costs of health care act as a hungry tapeworm on the American economy," says Berkshire Hathaway CEO Warren Buffett, and he has teamed up with some equally huge names in an attempt to go after said tapeworm. Amazon.com, Berkshire Hathaway, and JPMorgan Chase on Tuesday announced that they'd be creating a company tasked with coming up with technological solutions that can cut health-care costs for their hundreds of thousands of US employees. "Our group does not come to this problem with answers," Buffett notes—nor, reports the Wall Street Journal, with details on how much it plans to spend, where the company's HQ will be, or how many of its own employees would be impacted—"but we also do not accept it as inevitable." What the companies' leaders did reveal is that the new company will be "free from profit-making incentives and constraints." "The healthcare system is complex, and we enter into this challenge open-eyed about the degree of difficulty," says Jeff Bezos of Amazon, per CNBC, which notes the three companies employ a total 1.1 million workers, though that figure is not exclusive to the US. The AP reports it's unclear whether the trio would welcome other companies into the effort, but it does flag this line from JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon: "Our goal is to create solutions that benefit our US employees, their families and, potentially, all Americans." But they're not exactly benefiting Wall Street at the moment: Pre-market trading was unkind to health care companies, with the AP noting they represented eight of the 10 down companies on the S&P 500.

final_summary:
In a bizarre lawsuit filed in Miami-Dade County Circuit Court, Dr. Arturo Carvajal, a physician with a family practice in Hollywood, is suing Houston's Restaurant in North Miami Beach for allegedly failing to inform him on how to properly consume an artichoke. According to court documents, Carvajal ordered a grilled-artichoke special at the restaurant in May 2009, despite having never seen or heard of the dish before. His server did not provide guidance on how to eat the artichoke, specifically warning him that the outside portion of the leaf is indigestible.

Carvajal, reportedly unaware of this crucial detail, proceeded to chew on all parts of the artichoke, including the tough, brittle, and sharp-edged outer leaves. This led to severe abdominal pain and discomfort, prompting him to seek medical attention at a local hospital. An exploratory laparotomy revealed that artichoke leaves had become lodged in his small bowel.

The lawsuit alleges that the restaurant and its management were negligent in not training their staff to advise customers on the proper method of consuming an artichoke. Carvajal is seeking damages for bodily injury, pain and suffering, disability, disfigurement, aggravation of pre-existing conditions, and medical expenses. However, representatives from Hillstone Restaurant Group, the parent company of Houston's, have dismissed the lawsuit as a "silly notion," suggesting that it is unreasonable to expect restaurants to warn customers about eating entire artichokes. They compared the situation to eating bones in barbecue ribs, implying that such warnings would be unnecessary. Despite this, the company plans to vigorously defend itself against the lawsuit.

In it, he complains that the server failed "to explain the proper method of consuming an artichoke." His lawyer explains that Carvajal thought "it was like a food he might have eaten in his native Cuba, where you eat everything on the plate." The restaurant's lawyer begs to differ: "What's next?" he asks Business Insurance. "Are we going to have to post warnings on our menu they shouldn't eat the bones in our barbecue ribs?" Click here for more wacky lawsuits.

final_summary:
In a significant development, the Swedish authorities have officially recognized the Church of Kopimism, a group of 3,000 individuals who believe that sharing and data copying is a sacred act, as a legitimate religion. This decision marks a major milestone for the organization, which has been fighting for official recognition since 2010. Founded by philosophy student Isak Gerson, the Church of Kopimism views communication and sharing as fundamental aspects of human existence, deeming it unacceptable to monitor or eavesdrop on others. 

The church's name, "Kopimism," originates from the phrase "Kopimi," meaning "copy me" in Swedish. Despite previous rejections, including one in July 2011, the Church of Kopimism has finally secured official status after submitting a revised application outlining its method of prayer or meditation. According to Gerson, the core ritual of the church involves the act of copying and connecting with others through information sharing.

Although the recognition does not automatically legalize file sharing, the Church of Kopimism hopes that its newfound status will contribute to a shift in societal attitudes towards copyright infringement. Gerson expressed optimism that the official recognition would encourage more people to openly identify themselves as Kopimists, reducing the fear associated with file sharing. The church's membership has grown significantly over the past six months, tripling from 1,000 to 3,000 adherents, and is expected to continue expanding following this development.

Isak Gerson emphasized that the Church of Kopimism welcomes anyone with an internet connection to participate in the movement, urging them to continue sharing information freely. The organization's ultimate goal is to influence future legislation, considering its principles in the creation of copyright laws. As the Church of Kopimism embarks on this new chapter, its message of promoting open sharing and challenging traditional notions of intellectual property continues to resonate with like-minded individuals worldwide.

reference: – Sweden may not condone file-sharing—it's still illegal—but the country is officially OK with belief in the practice. Some 3,000 passionate file-sharers have gotten their beliefs recognized as an official religion. The Missionary Church of Kopimism—as in, "copy-me-ism"—has sought official status in Sweden since 2010, and it's finally won the battle, TorrentFreak reports. The decision follows a pair of rejections; authorities wanted Kopimism to define its prayer practices. "Our main ritual is the act of copying and connecting with each other by sharing information," the church's founder tells Wired. Now, Isak Gerson says, "more people will have the courage to step out as Kopimists. Maybe not in the public, but at least to their close ones." He hopes that the recognition will have an effect on future legislation surrounding file-sharing. Meanwhile, he has some advice for fellow believers: "Keep copying. Maintain hardline Kopimi."

final_summary:
Nechemya Weberman, a 54-year-old respected religious counselor in New York's ultra-orthodox Jewish community, was convicted of 59 counts of sexual abuse against a young girl who had sought his counsel due to questioning her faith and dressing immodestly according to the community's customs. The abuse allegedly occurred behind closed doors in his office from the time the girl was 12 until she was 15, with Weberman using his position as a trusted advisor to intimidate and manipulate her into satisfying his sexual desires.

The victim, now 18 and married, testified for four days, recounting the three-year ordeal, which included forced oral sex, groping, and reenacting scenes from pornography. Despite the lack of physical evidence, prosecutors argued that Weberman exploited his position of authority and trust to commit these heinous acts. Defense attorneys claimed the victim was a "petulant, calculating liar" motivated by anger towards Weberman for betraying her trust and informing her parents about a romantic interest.

The trial shed light on the insular nature of the Satmar Hasidic sect, which discourages questioning of faith or customs and maintains a "modesty squad" to enforce adherence to its rules. The community's strict social norms and fear of repercussions likely contributed to the victim's initial reluctance to report the abuse, as she faced ostracism and intimidation from her peers and community leaders.

District Attorney Charles Hynes expressed hope that the conviction would serve as a catalyst for change within the community, encouraging victims to come forward and urging leaders to reform their policies regarding reporting abuse to external authorities. However, critics have questioned Hynes' handling of similar cases in the past, accusing him of not being aggressive enough in pursuing perpetrators.

Weberman's conviction carries a maximum sentence of 117 years, with the possibility of additional time added at sentencing on January 9. His lawyers plan to appeal, maintaining that the jury received an "unfairly sanitized version of the facts." The victim, who has since moved away from the community, remains haunted by the trauma and continues to face harassment from those who still support Weberman.

reference: – Nechemya Weberman, an unlicensed therapist and powerful member of the Hasidic Jewish community of Brooklyn, will likely spend the rest of his life behind bars after being convicted of 59 counts of sexual abuse yesterday, reports the New York Times. After days of testimony from a victim who says she was repeatedly abused by Weberman starting when she was 12, a judge sentenced him to 103 years in prison, just short of the 117-year maximum. During her testimony, the victim, now 18, told Weberman that he "played around with and destroyed lives as if they were your toys, without the slightest bit of mercy.” Weberman's crimes included various sexual acts with minors, sometimes during therapy sessions under the guise of helping girls become more religious. The case is the first in two decades Brooklyn DA Charles Hynes has brought against a member of the Satmar ultra-Orthodox community. While many criticize Hynes for not being aggressive enough toward Hasidic abusers, he says the self-silencing culture of the Satmar community is to blame. The case has divided the community, even leading to violence against a victim advocate.

final_summary:
In a major crackdown on child exploitation, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) launched Operation Sunflower, a five-week operation spanning 46 states and six countries, resulting in the arrest of 245 suspected child pornographers and the identification of 123 victims of child exploitation. The operation, which concluded in late November and early December, saw law enforcement officials remove 44 children from their alleged abusers, while 79 others were identified as having been exploited by individuals outside their homes or were adults who were victimized as children.

The youngest victims identified in the operation were just 2 years old, with five children under the age of 3 and nine between the ages of 4 and 6. A total of 110 of the victims lived in the United States, with the remaining 13 located in six other countries. The operation also resulted in the arrest of 37 individuals in California, the highest number of any state.

ICE officials worked closely with international law enforcement partners, including Danish authorities, who initially alerted them to a case involving a 16-year-old boy planning to rape an 11-year-old girl. Using advanced forensic analysis technology, ICE agents were able to track down the girl and prevent the crime from occurring. This success story inspired the naming of Operation Sunflower.

One of the suspects arrested during the operation was Michael Lindsay, a 53-year-old man from San Jose, California, who was caught attempting to board a flight to the Philippines, where he had previously engaged in child sex tourism. Evidence suggests that Lindsay had sexual contact with multiple children, and agents are continuing to search for additional victims.

ICE Director John Morton emphasized the importance of the public's role in combating child exploitation, urging citizens to report any suspicious activity to the agency's tip line. Morton also highlighted the use of advanced technology in tracking down perpetrators and identifying victims, stating that "forensic analysis technology has become critical in the fight against child exploitation."

As part of the operation, ICE released images of four suspects, including a woman with distinctive tattoos, believed to be involved in the abuse of a 13-year-old girl whose images were circulated online. The abuse is thought to have occurred in either Los Angeles or the San Fernando Valley area of California, approximately 11 years ago. The agency is seeking the public's assistance in identifying the suspects and preventing further harm to potential victims.

In addition to the Operation Sunflower, ICE's Homeland Security Investigations unit has made significant strides in combating child exploitation, with 292 victims identified or rescued last fiscal year and 1,655 child predators arrested. As the agency continues to work towards protecting vulnerable populations, Morton reiterated his commitment to "defending the defenseless."

reference: – A massive child-porn bust has led to 245 arrests and the identification of 123 exploited children, some as young as two, reports the Contra Costa Times. The crackdown, dubbed Operation Sunflower, began Nov. 1 and ran for five weeks, and extended to 46 states and six countries, officials revealed yesterday. Of the 123 children, 44 were living with their alleged abusers. Five of the children were three or younger, and 41 were between the ages of four and 12. California led the way with 37 arrests, and all but 23 of those arrested were in the United States, reports CNN. "Whenever our investigations reveal the production and distribution of new child pornography online, we will do everything we can to rescue the victim and prosecute the abuser, even if takes us years or (going) around the world to do it," said ICE's director. Investigators say the are continuing to make arrests and attempting to identify further victims. The operation's name is a nod to the impressive 2011 rescue of an 11-year-old in Kansas; click for the fascinating story of how ICE agents tracked her down.

final_summary:
On April 15, 2013, two devastating explosions ripped through the Boston Marathon finish line, claiming the lives of three innocent victims and leaving numerous others severely injured. Among the deceased was eight-year-old Martin Richard, a bright and enthusiastic young boy from Dorchester who had been watching the marathon with his family. Martin's mother, Denise, suffered a severe head injury, while his seven-year-old sister was left with a serious leg wound. Their father, Bill Richard, released a heartfelt statement expressing his gratitude for the outpouring of support and asking for continued prayers for his family.

Krystle Campbell, a 29-year-old restaurant manager from Medford, Massachusetts, was the second victim to be identified. She had traveled to the finish line to photograph a friend completing the run and was described by her father, William Campbell, as a "very caring, very loving person" who was "daddy's little girl." Her mother, Patricia, was overcome with grief as she spoke publicly about her loss, stating, "You couldn't ask for a better daughter... I can't believe this has happened. It doesn't make any sense."

A third victim, a Boston University graduate student from China, was also identified, although their name was withheld pending family approval. The student was watching the marathon with two friends near the finish line when the blasts occurred, and one of their companions was injured and listed in stable condition. The university released a statement expressing its condolences to the family and praising the student's kindness and enthusiasm for learning.

As the city struggled to come to terms with the tragedy, authorities vowed to bring those responsible to justice. FBI officials stated that there were no known additional threats, and Mayor Thomas Menino pledged that the city would not be intimidated by terrorism. The mayor emphasized that Boston was a strong and resilient city that would pull together in the face of adversity.

In the aftermath of the bombings, the city's residents and visitors came together to offer support and comfort to those affected. The Salvation Army deployed mobile kitchens and personnel to provide food, drinks, and emotional and spiritual care, while the First Corps of Cadets Armory served as a staging area for runners seeking their gear bags and making travel arrangements. Despite the trauma and disruption caused by the attacks, there were signs of a return to normalcy, with planes, trains, and automobiles operating at or near regular schedules.

President Barack Obama praised the bravery and compassion of first responders and the American people, stating, "If you want to know who we are, what America is, how we respond to evil, that's it: selflessly, compassionately, unafraid." He planned to visit Boston on Thursday for an interfaith service, while Pope Francis expressed his condolences and invoked God's peace upon the dead and suffering. As the city began the long process of healing and recovery, District Attorney Daniel Conley reflected on the importance of coming together in times of crisis, saying, "Moments like this, and our response to them, define who we are."

reference: – Another fatality in the Boston bombings has been identified: The family of 29-year-old Krystle Campbell says she was killed as she and a friend were cheering on another friend who was racing, reports WCVB. "This is just a waste," says her mother. “Everyone that knew her loved her. She was always smiling. You couldn’t ask for a better daughter. She was the best.” Campbell was a restaurant manager, reports the Boston Herald, which notes an especially wrenching detail: Her father was initially told she was alive, but when he got to the ICU, he realized it was his daughter's friend who had survived. Campbell is the second of the three people killed to be identified. The first was 8-year-old Martin Richard, and his father, Bill, released a statement today thanking supporters and asking for privacy, reports WBZ. "My dear son Martin has died from injuries sustained in the attack on Boston. My wife and daughter are both recovering from serious injuries. We thank our family and friends, those we know and those we have never met, for their thoughts and prayers. I ask that you continue to pray for my family as we remember Martin. We also ask for your patience and for privacy as we work to simultaneously grieve and recover. Thank you." The third victim was a grad student at Boston University, but the person's identity is being withheld until the family says it's OK to release, reports USA Today.

final_summary:
Devon James, a 29-year-old former adult film actress and escort, has made a shocking claim that golf legend Tiger Woods is the father of her secret love child. According to RadarOnline.com, James gave birth to her son in early 2001, prior to Woods' marriage to Elin Nordegren. This revelation comes amidst ongoing speculation surrounding Woods' infidelity, which has led to numerous allegations of extramarital affairs with multiple women. 

James allegedly had a two-and-a-half-year relationship with Woods, during which time they engaged in unprotected sex, resulting in her pregnancy. Although there is no confirmation of whether Woods acknowledged or supported the child, James reportedly informed him of her suspicions in 2006, stating that she believed he was the father. 

RadarOnline.com obtained exclusive photos of the purported love child, who bears a striking resemblance to Woods. Notably, there is no indication that Woods has undergone a paternity test to confirm or deny the claim. Additionally, a source close to the situation revealed that James has insisted on Woods being the father, despite the lack of concrete evidence.

This latest allegation adds to the growing list of women associated with Woods, estimated to be around 15 individuals involved in secret sexual affairs. Another woman, Theresa Rogers, also claimed to have had a child with Woods, although sources close to her dispute this assertion, stating that he is not the father.

A Channel 4 documentary, "Tiger Woods: The Rise and Fall," has further fueled speculation regarding a potential love child, citing a journalist who claims to possess information about a girl born in 2003, along with DNA evidence. The documentary suggests that Woods may eventually acknowledge fathering a child, although neither he nor his representatives have publicly confirmed these allegations.

As if that’s not bad enough, a new documentary airing Thursday in the UK will delve into the mystery of Tiger Woods’ first purported love child, the Sun reports. Tiger Woods: The Rise and Fall includes interviews with journalist Neal Boulton, who says he knows someone who has DNA evidence that backs up the secret-baby claim and adds, “Tiger will eventually admit to fathering a child.” If this all sounds vaguely familiar, it’s because alleged mistress Theresa Rogers—who reportedly gave birth to Tiger’s daughter in 2003 and is the subject of the documentary’s claims—has been crying love child for months.

final_summary:
Airbus has unveiled its latest innovation, the A350 XWB jet, aimed at alleviating some of the most frustrating aspects of air travel. Designed to optimize efficiency, aerodynamics, and comfort, the aircraft boasts several cutting-edge features that set it apart from its competitors. At the heart of the A350 lies its unique composition, with over 50% of its structure made from carbon fiber-reinforced plastic, providing exceptional lightweight strength and durability while minimizing corrosion risks associated with traditional aluminum planes. This reduction in weight enables the plane to require less energy to propel, resulting in improved fuel economy – a staggering 25% better than similar-sized planes from competitors, according to Airbus.

To tackle the issue of jet lag, the A350 incorporates advanced LED lighting systems that adjust their color temperature to mimic the natural sunlight, synchronized with the human body's circadian rhythms. Additionally, the plane's air filtration system refreshes the cabin air every two to three minutes, maintaining a comfortable pressure level equivalent to 6,000 feet above sea level. These features aim to minimize the physical toll and stress caused by crossing multiple time zones.

Passenger comfort has also been prioritized, with the introduction of slightly wider and more spacious economy seats, allowing for easier movement down the aisles. The interior design is modular, enabling airlines to customize the layout and configuration to suit their specific needs, including accommodating varying numbers of business and first-class passengers. This adaptability extends to the integration of computer hardware and software, facilitating seamless updates and upgrades.

With a total investment of $15 billion and 2,600 hours of rigorous testing, the A350 is poised to serve as a flagship for the next 30 years. As one of the first airlines to adopt the A350, Qatar Airways has successfully introduced the plane into its fleet, with the inaugural US landing taking place recently. While the A350 has faced minor setbacks, such as a brief abortive takeoff due to a short runway, the overall performance has been promising. With its innovative design and technological advancements, the A350 XWB is set to revolutionize the aviation industry, offering a more efficient, comfortable, and enjoyable experience for travelers worldwide.

history.draft:
Airbus has unveiled its latest innovation, the A350 XWB jet, aimed at alleviating some of the most frustrating aspects of air travel. Designed to optimize efficiency, aerodynamics, and comfort, the aircraft boasts several cutting-edge features that set it apart from its competitors. At the heart of the A350 lies its unique composition, with over 50% of its structure made from carbon fiber-reinforced plastic, providing exceptional lightweight strength and durability while minimizing corrosion risks associated with traditional aluminum planes. This reduction in weight enables the plane to require less energy to propel, resulting in improved fuel economy – a staggering 25% better than similar-sized planes from competitors, according to Airbus.

To tackle the issue of jet lag, the A350 incorporates advanced LED lighting systems that adjust their color temperature to mimic the natural sunlight, synchronized with the human body's circadian rhythms. Additionally, the plane's air filtration system refreshes the cabin air every two to three minutes, maintaining a comfortable pressure level equivalent to 6,000 feet above sea level. These features aim to minimize the physical toll and stress caused by crossing multiple time zones.

Passenger comfort has also been prioritized, with the introduction of slightly wider and more spacious economy seats, allowing for easier movement down the aisles. The interior design is modular, enabling airlines to customize the layout and configuration to suit their specific needs, including accommodating varying numbers of business and first-class passengers. This adaptability extends to the integration of computer hardware and software, facilitating seamless updates and upgrades.

With a total investment of $15 billion and 2,600 hours of rigorous testing, the A350 is poised to serve as a flagship for the next 30 years. As one of the first airlines to adopt the A350, Qatar Airways has successfully introduced the plane into its fleet, with the inaugural US landing taking place recently. While the A350 has faced minor setbacks, such as a brief abortive takeoff due to a short runway, the overall performance has been promising. With its innovative design and technological advancements, the A350 XWB is set to revolutionize the aviation industry, offering a more efficient, comfortable, and enjoyable experience for travelers worldwide.

reference: – If it works, it could be an air-traveling perk to trump all others: no more jet lag. Airbus is making the bold claim that it can at least reduce the malady for those aboard its new A350 XWB line of jets. The main tool is a set of LED lights within the cabin that mimics natural shifts in sunlight and theoretically keeps people's natural circadian rhythms in sync, reports Fast Company. Another factor: Because of the planes' relatively light frames, they can be pressurized to 6,000 feet, which the airline says allows for more humidity and makes the air pressure inside the aircraft closer to the pressure on the ground than is the case with most jets, per Quartz. A third factor is the jet's filtration system, which replaces the air in the cabin in its entirety every two to three minutes. The idea there is that, if nothing else, the improved air quality should help people sleep better on long flights. And the big question: Has it worked? It's a little too early to tell. The first such jet landed in the US only earlier this month via Qatar Airways, though other airlines plan to add the jet to their fleets, notes a post at Science Alert. (This protein might provide another way to fight jet lag.)

final_summary:
According to recent research conducted by Stéphanie Mazza and her team at the University of Lyon, published in the journal Psychological Science, sleep plays a crucial role in the learning process, extending far beyond mere memory recall. The study revealed that sleep not only enhances relearning, but also enables individuals to relearn information twice as quickly and up to three times more effectively compared to staying awake. 

The research involved 40 French-speaking adults who were tasked with learning a list of 16 Swahili-French word pairs. Participants were divided into two groups - one that learned in the morning and relearned in the evening of the same day, and another that learned in the evening, slept overnight, and then relearned in the morning. The results showed that the group that slept between learning sessions outperformed the group that stayed awake, recalling an average of 10 word pairs on their first attempt versus 7, respectively. Furthermore, the sleep group required significantly fewer cycles to relearn the material, with even the lowest-performing members still outpacing the highest-performing members of the wake group.

Moreover, the study demonstrated that the benefits of sleep extend beyond short-term improvements, with the sleep group retaining more information over the long term. After one week, the sleep group recalled an average of 15 word pairs, compared to 11 for the wake group, and after six months, their recall performance was three times better, averaging 9 versus 3 for the wake group. A follow-up experiment confirmed that the combination of sleeping after initial learning and engaging in relearning was essential for achieving long-term memory benefits.

Mazza and her colleagues propose that sleep "transforms" memories, making the effects of initial learning stronger and facilitating subsequent relearning. They suggest that sleep not only prevents memories from being forgotten but also makes it easier to restore them during relearning. The study's findings support this theory, showing a positive correlation between total sleep time and recall and relearning performance.

However, the researchers acknowledge that their study had limitations, including varying the sleep and wake groups' training times, which may have influenced the results. Future studies are needed to determine whether the benefits of sleep apply to other types of learning, such as non-verbal materials, and to explore the specific mechanisms underlying sleep-dependent memory consolidation.

In practical terms, the study's findings suggest that incorporating sleep into learning strategies can be a powerful tool for improving retention and relearning. By scheduling study sessions to occur shortly before bedtime and allowing for adequate rest, individuals may be able to reduce the amount of practice needed and enhance their long-term memory. As the study's lead author notes, sleeping between study sessions may be a particularly effective way to optimize learning, especially when combined with repeated practice and relearning.

reference: – It's well established through previous research that sleep after learning is best for many memory-related tasks, including word lists, mazes, auditory tones, and so on. Sleep seems so vital to recall that some speculate it is directly responsible for, not just supportive of, learning, reports Scientific American. So researchers out of the University of Lyon chose to investigate another aspect of learning—not recall but relearning, where something previously learned has been forgotten and must be re-acquired. Reporting in the journal Psychological Science in August, they wroite that, coupled with practice, "Sleep makes perfect," and that "sleeping after learning is definitely a good strategy, but sleeping between two learning sessions is a better strategy." The team tested 40 French-speaking adults tasked with learning 16 Swahili words. Some learned them at night, slept, and relearned them in the morning, while others learned the words in the morning, didn't sleep, and relearned them at night. The group that slept between sessions performed so much better that even those who forgot the most words relearned them faster during their morning session than the least forgetful members of the group relearning the words at night. The results don't clarify which is at play—sleep boosting learning, which helps improve later relearning, or sleep simply allowing for uninterrupted learning processes to speed up relearning—but either way, sleeping between studying seems worth a shot. (This woman only sleeps three hours a night.)

final_summary:
Since 1996, Alexa Internet has been providing its daily crawl data to the Internet Archive, which adds it to the Wayback Machine after a certain embargo period. Meanwhile, an analysis of IRS data by Forbes reveals that affluent individuals tend to prefer warmer climates and low-tax environments when deciding where to relocate within the United States.

According to the study, the top destination for wealthy movers in 2008 was Collier County, Florida, specifically the city of Naples, with 15,150 tax returns showing individuals moving to the area from other parts of the country. The average reported income of these newcomers was $76,161 per person, equivalent to $304,644 for a family of four. Notably, although more residents departed Collier County than arrived, the average income of those leaving was significantly lower at $26,128 per person.

The majority of households moving to Collier County originated from other parts of Florida, including Lee, Miami-Dade, Broward, Palm Beach, and Orange counties. Additionally, significant numbers of migrants came from major northern cities such as Cook County, Illinois (Chicago), Oakland County, Michigan (near Detroit), and Suffolk County, New York (Long Island).

Greene County, Georgia, ranked second on the list, with 788 people moving to the area in 2008, approximately 75 miles east of Atlanta. Other top destinations included Nassau County, Florida, near Jacksonville; Llano County, Texas, 70 miles northwest of Austin; and Walton County, Florida, 80 miles east of Pensacola.

Experts attribute the prevalence of Florida and Texas on the list to the absence of state income taxes in both states. According to Robert Shrum, manager of state affairs at the Tax Foundation in Washington, D.C., "If you're a high-income earner, then that, from a tax perspective, is going to be a driving decider if you're going to move to one of those two states." When considering property taxes, Texas ranks fourth-lowest in the country, and Florida ranks eighth-lowest.

Eight states with targeted high-income tax brackets – California, New Jersey, New York, Maryland, Hawaii, Oregon, Connecticut, and Wisconsin – saw six of the top 10 counties with the most affluent departures. One notable exception is Pitkin County, Colorado, home to the expensive Aspen ski community, where 962 tax filers and dependents left in 2008 due to economic contraction, with an average income of $71,473 per capita. Many of these individuals relocated to neighboring Garfield County, where housing prices were significantly lower.

Forbes employed a specific methodology to identify areas attracting the wealthy, focusing on counties where incoming households earned higher incomes than those that did not move, and outgoing households earned lower incomes. Conversely, the study found that counties with departing households earning higher incomes and incoming households earning lower incomes were likely experiencing an exodus of the affluent. By applying these criteria and excluding counties with fewer than 500 people and average incomes below $35,000, the researchers identified emerging hotspots for wealthy migration. However, the IRS cautions that these estimates may be approximate due to limitations in the data, particularly regarding non-filers and late submissions.

reference: – Wealthy Americans are on the move, largely to the low-income-tax havens of the Sunbelt. A new Forbes analysis of IRS data from 2008 shows Collier County, Fla., at the head of the pack, picking up 15,150 new residents with an average income of $76,161—compared to $26,128 for the people moving away. A nifty feature on the Forbes website illustrates the migration and breaks it down by average income, county-to-county. For instance, click on New Orleans (Orleans Parish, La.) and you see graphics showing not just migration in and out but the number of people moving and their average income: to Seattle (King County, Wash.), 56 people, $36,200; from Seattle, 41 people, $25,000. To get the details on migration in and out of the county of your choice—fair warning: it's addictive—click here. To see a slideshow breaking down the most popular destinations, click here.

final_summary:
Historian Dr. Paul Booth of Keele University has made a groundbreaking discovery in the field of etymology, uncovering the earliest known reference to the word "fuck" in a court case dating back to 1310. While examining the Chester county court plea rolls, Dr. Booth stumbled upon the name "Roger Fuckebythenavele," which appears three times between December 8, 1310, and September 28, 1311. The name is believed to be a nickname, with Dr. Booth suggesting two possible interpretations: it may refer to an incident where Roger attempted unsatisfactory sex, possibly due to inexperience, or it could be a derogatory term implying Roger was considered unintelligent or foolish.

Prior to this discovery, the earliest recorded use of the word "fuck" was in the 1475 poem "Flen flyys," which contains a line referencing "fvccant vvivys of heli," a Latin-English mix translating to "...they fuck the wives of Ely." However, historians have questioned whether this usage was indeed a reference to the modern-day definition of the word. Another early appearance of the name "John le Fucker" in 1278 has been disputed, with some believing it to be a variant of the word "fulcher," meaning soldier.

Dr. Booth's finding pushes back the origins of the word "fuck" significantly, challenging previous assumptions about its etymology. According to linguist Jesse Sheidlower, author of "The F Word," the term is of Germanic origin and shares similarities with words in other Germanic languages, including Dutch, German, and Swedish, which convey both sexual and non-sexual meanings.

As the discoverer of this significant piece of linguistic history, Dr. Booth is set to publish an article about his findings in the journal "Notes and Queries." His research sheds new light on the evolution of language and highlights the complexities of medieval terminology. When asked about the possibility of the name being a joke inserted by the court clerk, Dr. Booth suggests that the repeated appearances of the name make it unlikely to be a prank, and even if it were, the significance of the word's usage remains unchanged.

While the true nature of Roger Fuckebythenavele's identity remains unclear, Dr. Booth speculates that the name could be a reflection of his reputation or a commentary on his perceived intelligence. Other examples of unusual surnames from the same period, such as "Hunfridus de aureo testiculo" and "Alwin Clawecunt," further illustrate the creative and often humorous ways in which medieval individuals were identified.

reference: – What's in a name? Just a crass term that's endured for centuries, if this holds any water. An English history researcher says he's spotted the earliest-known "f-word" on record in court documents from 1310, the Telegraph reports. Paul Booth came upon a reference to "Roger F***ebythenavele"—likely the guy's nickname, Booth says—while analyzing court records during the stormy rule of Edward II (1307-27). The name is "written clearly, and three times, and I think that shows it's not a joke," says Booth. Looks like F***ebythenavele was summoned twice to court in Cheshire, in December of 1310 and May of 1311, and finally outlawed on September 28, 1311. A court clerk might have altered the guy's name for fun, Booth admits, but short of that he sees two explanations. "First, that it applies to an actual event—a clumsy attempt at sexual intercourse by an 'Inexperienced Copulator' (my name for Roger), revealed to the world by a revengeful former girlfriend," he tells Vice.com. "Fourteenth-century revenge porn perhaps?" Or it might be an "elaborate way of describing someone regarded as a 'halfwit'—ie, that is the way that he would think of performing the sexual act." Before this, the earliest f-word on record was the phrase (translated from a Latin/English mix) "…they f**k the wives of Ely" from the poem "Flen flyys," circa 1475, Medievalists.net reports. The word has Germanic roots and is related to words that have "sexual meanings as well as meaning[s] such as 'to strike' or 'to move back and forth,'" writes Jesse Sheidlower in his book The F Word.

final_summary:
On Tuesday, a federal appellate court decision raised concerns that Netflix and other bandwidth-intensive websites may have to pay tolls to broadband providers to ensure quality service, potentially disrupting their business models and increasing costs for consumers. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia struck down the Federal Communications Commission's (FCC) "open Internet" rules, which mandated equal treatment of Internet traffic and prohibited broadband providers from engaging in discriminatory practices.

The ruling effectively ended the principle of net neutrality, allowing broadband providers to discriminate against content they dislike, favoring their business partners while delaying or blocking websites they deem unfavorable. This could result in slower loading speeds for websites that cannot afford to pay for faster content delivery, disproportionately affecting small businesses and independent websites.

With the demise of net neutrality, broadband providers can now charge content providers for faster delivery, further exacerbating the issue of unequal access to the Internet. This could lead to a situation where large corporations, such as Comcast, favor their own content over competitors', hindering the success of smaller, independent websites and startups.

The ruling also opened the door for broadband providers to block or throttle specific types of online traffic, including peer-to-peer file sharing and online video streaming, which could impact services like Netflix and BitTorrent. Furthermore, the decision allows broadband providers to increase revenue without investing in improving their infrastructure, potentially worsening the existing issues with America's slow broadband speeds.

The FCC's failure to classify broadband providers as common carriers, a move that would have granted them regulatory authority to enforce net neutrality, contributed to the court's decision. The agency's reluctance to reclassify broadband providers as common carriers, despite consumer groups urging them to do so, has left the door open for the current scenario.

In the wake of the ruling, experts predict that broadband providers will explore alternative revenue streams, including sponsored data plans, where content providers pay for their users' data consumption. This could lead to a situation where internet access becomes a subsidiary of internet services, with larger companies subsidizing users' data plans and gaining unprecedented control over the internet and its users.

As a result, the internet's ecosystem may undergo significant changes, with smaller companies struggling to compete with larger corporations that can afford to pay for preferential treatment. The loss of net neutrality has far-reaching implications for the future of the internet, potentially leading to a more fragmented and controlled online environment.

reference: – Yesterday was the day net neutrality died, with a court ruling that Internet service providers aren't bound by FCC non-discrimination rules—so they can prioritize some traffic over others. The decision may mean major changes for the everyday user, and today, media sites are offering the details. Among their warnings: The Wall Street Journal reports that the decision could have a huge impact on sites like Netflix and YouTube, whose video services hog a lot of bandwidth (they account for 32% and 19% of peak web traffic, respectively). If ISPs start making Netflix, for instance, pay a usage fee to maintain speedy content delivery, that cost could make a serious dent in its profits—or trickle down to consumers. ISPs could charge sites big fees in exchange for faster content delivery, potentially giving giants who can pay, like Amazon, a big leg up over independent retailers—not to mention other small businesses, the Huffington Post notes. Providers might even go so far as to prioritize traffic to partner sites—Time Warner might push CNN, for example. Another anti-competitive fear, via BuzzFeed: Big companies might start paying your data fees for the use of their sites. That might sound good—free data?—but it would mean that companies with the ability to pay would have a major advantage over start-ups in winning users. As BuzzFeed points out, "If, in this future, you're choosing between two streaming music services, and one of them pays for your data, there's a very good chance you’re going to pick that one." Beyond concerns over fees, there are worries that providers could block certain websites completely: Comcast users could theoretically end up with no access to, say, Netflix or Vonage, writes Troy Wolverton in the San Jose Mercury News. But net neutrality, in some form, may live to see another day, the Journal notes, reporting that the ruling "left open the door for the FCC to craft rules in a different form that might accomplish its earlier intentions."

final_summary:
On November 9, 1861, during the early stages of the American Civil War, the sidewheel steamer Keystone State met its demise while traveling from Detroit to Milwaukee, Wisconsin. The ship's disappearance went unnoticed for over a week, with wreckage only surfacing on November 16, 1861, near Port Austin, and the wheelhouse washing ashore. The tragic event resulted in the loss of 33 crew members and fueled speculation surrounding the ship's alleged clandestine cargo of gold and war materials.

For over 150 years, the fate of the Keystone State remained a mystery, with various theories emerging about the true nature of its cargo. However, thanks to the efforts of shipwreck hunter David Trotter and his team from Undersea Research Associates, a significant portion of the mystery has been unraveled. In July 2013, Trotter's team successfully located the wreck of the Keystone State approximately 50 miles north of Michigan's "thumb" in Lake Huron, at a depth of around 200 feet.

Trotter, a seasoned shipwreck enthusiast with over 100 discoveries under his belt, employed side-scan sonar technology to pinpoint the location of the wreck. Following its identification, the team conducted multiple dives to document the site and investigate the circumstances surrounding the ship's sinking. One of the primary objectives was to determine whether the ship's cargo, listed as "iron implements" or farm machinery on the manifest, was indeed legitimate or if it held a more sinister purpose.

The Keystone State, measuring 288 feet in length, was a luxurious vessel for its time, ranking as the second-largest ship on the Great Lakes upon its launch in 1849. Its intended route typically spanned between Detroit and Buffalo, New York, but on this fateful voyage, it was headed towards Milwaukee. The unusual cargo and the ship's hasty departure from Detroit without lifeboats raised suspicions among historians and enthusiasts alike.

David Trotter believes that the crew might have jettisoned the cargo in an attempt to lighten the load and prevent the ship from capsizing in the rough waters. He also speculates that Captain Wilkes Travers may have hesitated to steer the ship towards land, fearing it could capsize in a sea trough due to the ship's design. The team's findings suggest that the ship did indeed struggle significantly, with evidence pointing to extensive damage caused by the storm, as well as potential damage sustained upon impact with the lakebed.

Despite initial rumors of a hidden treasure, no gold has been discovered thus far. However, the surrounding area contains a substantial debris field, which remains unexplored. Due to the extreme water pressure at such great depths, divers can only spend around 15-20 minutes exploring the wreck before needing to decompress for over an hour on their ascent.

With over 35 years of experience in shipwreck hunting, David Trotter continues to contribute significantly to our understanding of maritime history. His notable discoveries include the 238-foot steamer New York, found in 2012, and a 90-foot double-masted schooner discovered in 2011. For Trotter, each new discovery represents an opportunity to connect with historical events and uncover secrets that have lain undisturbed for centuries.

He also speculates that Captain Wilkes Travers may have hesitated to steer the ship towards land, fearing it could capsize in a sea trough due to the ship's design. The team's findings suggest that the ship did indeed struggle significantly, with evidence pointing to extensive damage caused by the storm, as well as potential damage sustained upon impact with the lakebed.

Despite initial rumors of a hidden treasure, no gold has been discovered thus far. However, the surrounding area contains a substantial debris field, which remains unexplored. Due to the extreme water pressure at such great depths, divers can only spend around 15-20 minutes exploring the wreck before needing to decompress for over an hour on their ascent.

With over 35 years of experience in shipwreck hunting, David Trotter continues to contribute significantly to our understanding of maritime history. His notable discoveries include the 238-foot steamer New York, found in 2012, and a 90-foot double-masted schooner discovered in 2011. For Trotter, each new discovery represents an opportunity to connect with historical events and uncover secrets that have lain undisturbed for centuries.

reference: – The Keystone State, which set sail in 1849 as one of the largest and most luxurious steamer ships of its time, sank during a November storm in 1861 with 33 people aboard. No one knew its fate for more than a week, until debris was spotted, and it's been lost since then—until now. A shipwreck hunter found it at the bottom of Lake Huron, 40 to 50 miles from where it was last seen and far from where David Trotter expected to find it. Some historians say the ship may have been secretly hauling Civil War supplies when it sank, the Detroit Free Press reports—and there are even rumors it may have been carrying gold, according to Michigan Live. By 1857, after an economic downturn and the rise of the railroad, many ships like the Keystone State were considered too pricey to operate. By 1861, however, thanks to the Civil War, the Keystone State had been taken out of storage and refurbished. It picked up its cargo in Detroit—officially, farm implements, hardware, and grain, but "it was an emergency shipment," a historian explains; he adds that typically a ship wouldn't have made a trip like that so late in the year. Also suspect: The ship wasn't carrying any lifeboats (everyone aboard died in the wreck), which could indicate it left in a hurry. Trotter's team found her using side-scan sonar in July, and has since made 30 dives at the site—which turned up no gold or cargo. Some speculate that the crew dumped the cargo in a bid to save the ship. (Another ship was found this year in Lake Superior.)

final_summary:
On January 29, 2011, a devastating attack shook the southern province of Kandahar, Afghanistan, where a suicide bomber riding a motorcycle packed with explosives rammed into a car carrying Deputy Governor Abdul Latif Ashna, killing him instantly and injuring three of his bodyguards. The Taliban claimed responsibility for the attack, stating that the bomber also killed three bodyguards and the driver. This high-profile assassination marked a resurgence of targeted killings in Kandahar, a tactic employed by the Taliban in the past to disrupt governance and undermine the authority of the government.

The attack occurred as Ashna was being driven to work in the provincial capital, highlighting the vulnerability of public officials in the region. The Taliban's actions are seen as a deliberate attempt to destabilize the area, which is considered a crucial battleground in the ongoing conflict between the government and the insurgency. The killing of Ashna comes amidst a broader trend of violence in Kandahar, where a series of bombings and shootings have taken place in recent months.

In a separate incident, a Taliban suicide bomber detonated explosives at a Kabul supermarket on January 28, killing six members of a prominent Afghan family, including a young doctor, his wife, and their four children. The attack was claimed by the Taliban, who stated that their target was an official with the US-based security contractor Xe, formerly known as Blackwater. However, a representative for USTC Holdings, which acquired Xe, denied any involvement, and a senior international intelligence official suggested that the Haqqani network, linked to al-Qaeda, may have carried out the attack.

The incidents highlight the ongoing struggle for control in Afghanistan, particularly in the southern provinces where the Taliban maintains a strong presence. The US-led coalition, led by General David Petraeus, continues to grapple with the insurgency, which has shown resilience despite targeted strikes and military operations. As the conflict enters its 10th year, the stakes are high, with the outcome hanging in the balance. The Taliban's ability to carry out high-profile attacks underscores the challenges facing the government and the international community in stabilizing the country and building trust with the local population.

Meanwhile, protests erupted in Kabul and other cities, condemning the execution of Afghans in Iran and calling for the release of Afghan political prisoners. Demonstrators carried signs denouncing Iranian President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad and demanding justice for those killed in the cross-border executions. The Afghan government has confirmed the execution of six Afghans in Iran but disputes claims of higher numbers.

As the situation in Afghanistan remains volatile, the transfer of security responsibilities from international forces to Afghan authorities is underway. Major General John W. Nicholson, the deputy chief of staff for operations of the US-led coalition, was promoted to the rank of major general and tasked with overseeing the transition process. The ceremony, attended by General Petraeus and Afghan officials, marked a significant step towards handing over security duties to the Afghan military by the end of 2014. Despite these efforts, the Taliban's continued ability to launch high-profile attacks raises concerns about the prospects for stability in the region.

The province is seen as vital in the Afghanistan war. "The enemies of Afghanistan cannot stop the Afghan people from development and progress by killing such personalities," said President Hamid Karzai in a statement condemning the attack. "There are thousands of other brave Afghans who will stand against the enemy and serve the people."

final_summary:
Sean "Diddy" Combs publicly addressed the death of his ex-partner, Kim Porter, in a heartfelt social media post three days after she passed away at the age of 46. According to law enforcement insiders, Porter was found unresponsive at her Los Angeles home, with a 911 call reporting a person in cardiac arrest; however, no official cause of death has been disclosed. Porter had reportedly been experiencing flu-like symptoms for several weeks prior to her passing. 

Combs and Porter shared three children together - 20-year-old son Christian, and 11-year-old twin daughters Jessie James and D'Lila - while also adopting Porter's 12-year-old son Quincy from her previous relationship with music producer Al B. Sure!. The couple had a long-term relationship spanning over a decade before parting ways in 2007, yet remained close until her untimely death. In his emotional tribute, Combs described their bond as "more than best friends" and "soulmates," stating that he struggled to come to terms with her loss and expressed deep sadness at being without her.
