reference: – Sean "Diddy" Combs took to Instagram on Sunday to comment publicly for the first time on the death of ex Kim Porter, the mother of three of his children. Alongside of video of the two of them that's been viewed nearly 6 million times, the 49-year-old wrote: "For the last three days I've been trying to wake up out of this nightmare. But I haven't. I don't know what I'm going to do without you baby. I miss you so much. Today I'm going to pay tribute to you, I'm going to try and find the words to explain our unexplainable relationship. We were more than best friends, we were more than soulmates. WE WERE SOME OTHER S---!! And I miss you so much. Super Black Love." CNN reports the video features footage from a 2006 Essence shoot that shows a pregnant Porter dancing with Diddy as SWV's "When U Cry" plays. "We don't get a chance to see much" black love, Porter says, in extolling themselves as an example of it. She and Diddy split in 2007; their children are son Christian, 20, and 11-year-old twin daughters, Jessie James and D'Lila, per the New York Post. Porter, 46, was found dead Thursday in her Toluca Lake, Calif., home. No cause of death has been released.

final_summary:
Eman Ahmed Abd El Aty, a 36-year-old Egyptian woman weighing approximately 1,100 pounds, is set to undergo potentially lifesaving surgery in Mumbai, India, after being confined to her home for over two decades. According to her family, Abd El Aty has not left her home since she was 11 years old, tipping the scales at around 500 kilograms. Her weight makes her the heaviest woman alive, surpassing the current Guinness World Record holder, Pauline Potter of the United States, who weighed 292 kilograms in 2010.

Abd El Aty's health issues stem from a combination of factors, including a parasitic infection causing elephantiasis, a condition characterized by swollen limbs, and obesity-related lymphoedema, leading to massive swelling of her legs. Her family sought help from various doctors in Egypt, but their efforts were unsuccessful until they reached out to Dr. Muffazal Lakdawala, a renowned bariatric surgeon at the Center for Obesity and Digestive Surgery in Mumbai.

Dr. Lakdawala, who has performed the most single-incision laparoscopic surgeries worldwide and has operated on high-ranking Indian politicians, including two ministers in Prime Minister Narendra Modi's cabinet, agreed to take on Abd El Aty's case. However, obtaining a medical visa for her proved challenging, as the Indian Embassy in Cairo initially refused to grant one due to her inability to travel in person. Dr. Lakdawala turned to Twitter, reaching out to Sushma Swaraj, India's Minister of External Affairs, who was undergoing treatment for kidney failure in a hospital. Swaraj promptly responded, ensuring Abd El Aty received her medical visa within 24 hours.

With the visa secured, Dr. Lakdawala is arranging for a chartered flight to transport Abd El Aty to Mumbai, where she will undergo surgery. The operation will be performed pro bono, and Dr. Lakdawala estimates that Abd El Aty will need to stay in Mumbai for two to three months for recovery and subsequent treatment. He hopes to help her lose at least 200-300 pounds during the surgery, although it may take two to three years for her to reach a healthy weight of 100 kilograms.

The surgery, a last resort for individuals struggling with severe obesity, involves reducing the size of the stomach or rerouting the digestive system to minimize food intake. Dr. Lakdawala has successfully performed such procedures on numerous patients, including Indian ministers Nitin Gadkari and Venkaiah Naidu. With Abd El Aty's case, he faces a significant challenge, acknowledging that it will be a test of his skills and those of his team.

reference: – Eman Ahmed Abd El Aty's family says the 36-year-old Egyptian woman hasn't left her home in 25 years, the BBC reports. Her extreme weight forced her to crawl instead of walk by age 11, and an ensuing stroke left her bedridden. Her family says she weighs more than 1,100 pounds, which would make her the heaviest woman alive—the current Guinness record-holder is only 643 pounds—and close to the heaviest woman ever, according to the Washington Post. While Abd El Aty's weight hasn't been confirmed, Dr. Muffazal Lakdawala in India estimates she is at least 990 pounds based on photos of her. "My initial reaction was '‘How is she even alive?'" he says. His next reaction was to try to save her. Abd El Aty is expected to arrive in Mumbai for surgery in the next 10 days, but it won't be an easy journey. "In every sense, she's a challenge," Lakdawala says. The bariatric surgeon has been raising money to get her to Mumbai and will be operating pro bono, the Times of India reports. But it's not just money that's an issue. Because she can't leave her room, Abd El Aty was unable to make it to the Indian embassy to be fingerprinted for a visa; only a personal plea from Lakdawala secured her an exemption. And while transporting Abd El Aty will be challenging in its own right, Lakdawala says the surgery itself is "very high risk." He says he's "hopeful" but not "confident" he can help Abd El Aty lose up to 300 pounds. (For the obese, objects are closer than they appear.)

final_summary:
On October 9, 2014, a time capsule buried within the lion statue atop the Old State House in Boston, Massachusetts, was finally unearthed after over a century. The Bostonian Society, responsible for maintaining the historic landmark, had long suspected the presence of the capsule, thanks to a 1901 article in The Boston Globe hinting at its existence. However, it wasn't until the lion statue was removed for refurbishment that the capsule was confirmed to be inside. 

The delicate process of retrieving the capsule was carried out by Robert Shure, owner of Skylight Studio, who carefully removed the crown from the lion's head and cut the copper bands securing the box in place. The box itself measured approximately 8-by-12 inches in length and 6 inches deep, weighing between five and ten pounds. 

Upon opening the box, the society's archivist, Elizabeth Roscio, was thrilled to find that the contents remained remarkably well-preserved, with no signs of mold or water damage. However, one unexpected item caught everyone off guard – a mysterious red book, which was not mentioned in the 1901 article detailing the capsule's contents. The book's purpose and origin remain unknown, leaving room for speculation that it might be a family history, possibly belonging to Samuel Rogers, the craftsman who placed the capsule in the lion's head.

According to the 1901 article, the original contents of the capsule included photographs of prominent politicians, letters, newspapers, and campaign buttons from the 1900 presidential election. Despite these expectations, the society chose not to disturb the contents further due to their fragile condition, opting instead to transport the box to a controlled environment for sorting and study. 

As part of the preservation efforts, the Bostonian Society is seeking public input on what to include in a new time capsule to be placed in the lion's head. Suggestions range from modern-day items such as iPhones and cannolis to historical artifacts like a Boston Marathon medal. The society aims to create a more accessible time capsule, incorporating elements from the past and present, including photographs and documents from the current era.

reference: – New York City opened an old time capsule today that turned out to be ... kind of boring. Boston, on the other hand, has itself a mysterious red book, reports WBUR. Historians today removed a copper box that was placed inside the lion atop the Old State House back in 1901 and were surprised to find the hard-cover book on top, apparently in great condition. The curious will have to wait a little longer, however, because officials didn't open the book or anything beneath it inside the box—they'll do that later in a controlled environment to make sure nothing gets ruined. Expect old photos, letters, newspapers, and similar memorabilia. But as for the book, no mention of it was made in a newspaper article of the time about the time capsule. An official with the Bostonian Society guesses that it might be a family history of a carpenter named Samuel Rogers, reports the Boston Globe. Rogers worked on the building and helped prepare the time capsule, notes a separate Globe story. And while he did reportedly pop a family history inside, there's no account of it being in a red book. Eventually, all the items will go on public display, and a new time capsule will go back inside the lion. One thing for sure that will be included: a medal from the 2013 Boston Marathon, the one hit by bombs, reports AP. (Click for more on a time capsule that was opened 40 years late.)

final_summary:
At the 67th Primetime Emmy Awards, held on September 20, 2015, the organizers created a montage of spoilers for several popular television shows, leaving thousands of viewers annoyed and disappointed. The montage included revealing clips from shows such as "Nurse Jackie", "Boardwalk Empire", "Sons Of Anarchy", and "Mad Men". This move was unexpected, especially since these shows had concluded earlier in the year and their endings had already been discussed extensively online.

Host Andy Samberg acknowledged the potential backlash during the ceremony, jokingly referring to the event as the "Spoiler Awards" and stating, "I guess everyone on every show died." His comment seemed to add insult to injury for the frustrated audience members. Many viewers took to Twitter to express their discontent, with users like Dalton Ross tweeting, "The #Emmys just set the land-speed record for most spoilers per minute."

Some individuals defended the decision to include the spoilers, pointing out that the shows had ended months prior and were no longer under embargo. Kelly Sharon tweeted, "To everyone complaining about spoilers: most of those shows ended in the spring. If you haven't watched you're not going to." However, this argument failed to console those who felt betrayed by the premature revelation of the plot twists.

Jon Hamm won the award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama Series for his portrayal of Don Draper in "Mad Men", which was one of the shows featured in the spoiler-filled montage. Despite the controversy surrounding the spoilers, the ceremony continued with other notable winners, including "Game of Thrones" taking home the award for Outstanding Drama Series.

reference: – Whoops: Viewers watching the Emmys last night were dismayed when a montage celebrating shows that came to a close this year gave away the endings of many of those shows, Sky News reports. Among the series spoiled: Mad Men, Sons of Anarchy, Nurse Jackie, Parenthood, and Boardwalk Empire. "Welcome back to the spoiler awards," host Andy Samberg said when he came back onstage. "I guess everyone on every show died." There was outrage on Twitter, the New York Daily News reports, but not everyone was sympathetic to the complainers. For example: "To everyone complaining about spoilers: most of those shows ended in the spring. If you haven't watched you're not going to," tweeted one person. But, as another pointed out, "Hey #Emmys, we aren’t all @AndySamberg! Not everyone can lock themselves in a bunker and watch ALL the shows."

final_summary:
On December 22nd, 2012, Multiexport Foods, Inc. initiated a voluntary recall of its cold-smoked salmon products due to possible contamination with Listeria monocytogenes, a bacterium that can cause serious and potentially life-threatening infections in vulnerable populations such as young children, the elderly, and those with weakened immune systems. The initial recall targeted the "Paramount Reserve" brand's 12 oz Twin Pack and 1.25 lb Pack sizes.

The company subsequently expanded the recall on December 26th, 2012, to include additional lot-sublot-batch codes, citing the potential risk of contamination. The affected products were distributed to various retail outlets across 42 states, including California and Puerto Rico, through Wal-Mart's Sam's Club stores. 

The recalled items consisted of cold-smoked salmon packaged in 12 oz Twin Packs (vacuum-sealed with a cardboard sleeve) and 1.25 lb Bundles under the "Paramount Reserve" brand. The UPC codes for these products are 6 88264 86705 0 for the Twin Pack and 6 88264 86664 0 for the 1.25 lb Pack. Specific lot-sublot-batch codes, printed on stickers attached to the back of the packaging, also identified the affected products.

According to Wal-Mart, the discovery of Listeria monocytogenes occurred during a routine laboratory test on a shipment of salmon that had not yet been distributed to stores. Although no illnesses have been reported, the company took the precautionary measure of recalling the products to ensure consumer safety. Customers who purchased the affected items can return them to the store for a full refund.

Tampa Bay Fisheries, Inc., in collaboration with Multiexport Foods, cooperated with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration throughout the recall process. The affected areas where the recalled products were distributed include Alabama, Alaska, Arizona, Arkansas, California, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Florida, Georgia, Hawaii, Idaho, Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, Michigan, Minnesota, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, Nevada, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, Ohio, Oklahoma, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Puerto Rico, Rhode Island, South Carolina, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, Vermont, Virginia, Washington, West Virginia, Wisconsin, and Wyoming.

reference: – If they're serving smoked salmon at the New Year's Eve party, it might pay to find out where it's from. Info on UPC codes and affected states is here. No illnesses have been linked to the fish, but a routine lab test turned up traces of the potentially deadly bacteria.

final_summary:
A growing body of research suggests that social media, particularly Facebook, plays a significant role in hindering emotional recovery and personal growth after a breakup. According to a study published in the journal Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking, individuals who frequently monitor their ex-partner's Facebook activity exhibit greater current distress, negative feelings, and longing for the ex-partner, as well as lower personal growth. The study, conducted by Tara C. Marshall, analyzed data from 464 participants who had experienced a breakup and found that Facebook surveillance was associated with these negative outcomes, even after accounting for offline contact and established personality and relational predictors.

The researchers identified three distinct categories of Facebook harassment: Covert Provocation, Public Harassment, and Venting. Those who engaged in these behaviors were more likely to also perpetrate cyberobsessional pursuit (COP) and obsessive relational pursuit (ORI), which are forms of cyberstalking and stalking. Moreover, participants who admitted to engaging in stalking behaviors tended to do so online, offline, and on Facebook.

Another study by Marshall found that remaining Facebook friends with an ex-partner was associated with lower levels of negative feelings, sexual desire, and longing for the ex-partner compared to those who had defriended their ex. However, this group also reported lower personal growth, suggesting that even weak-tie contact with an ex-partner through remaining Facebook friends may hinder the process of moving on.

The studies highlight the challenges of navigating social media after a breakup, particularly when it comes to managing exposure to an ex-partner's online presence. While conventional wisdom suggests that cutting off contact with an ex makes for a smoother recovery, the ease of access to social media platforms like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram can make it difficult to achieve distance. In fact, a recent study found that nearly half of people have made contact with an ex-partner through Facebook, and over half have looked through their ex's photos to find pictures of them with a new romantic partner.

Marshall's research suggests that Facebook surveillance may obstruct the process of healing and moving on from a past relationship, and that avoiding exposure to an ex-partner, both offline and online, may be the best remedy for healing a broken heart. However, the study also notes that the motives behind remaining Facebook friends with an ex-partner and engaging in Facebook surveillance are complex and multifaceted, and warrant further exploration in future research.

reference: – What with Facebook, Flickr, and other fine venues for stalking "exes," breakups aren't nearly as final as they once were. But for your own good, please stop following them around cyberspace. "Conventional wisdom, and even science, has it that cutting off contact with an ex makes for a smoother recovery," writes Tracy Clark-Flory at Salon. And she knows the struggle all too well, having tracked an ex from Flickr to Twitter until she realized that the ring on his finger wasn't "merely an engagement ring." Not only was he already married, but Clark-Flory had to see a live-tweeted photo of him standing in the aisle. "It’s one thing to realize that the man you once wanted to marry" has moved on, she writes, "and another to be a virtual witness to it." Clark-Flory digs up studies to make her case, like one that finds "Facebook stalking ... may obstruct the process of healing" and another in which 30% of college students admit to posting status updates "to taunt or hurt" an ex. The only problem: "It’s never been easier to secretly keep tabs on exes," writes Clark-Flory, "and it’s never been harder not to."

final_summary:
In the aftermath of the 2012 Benghazi attack, which resulted in the deaths of four American personnel, the Obama administration faced intense scrutiny over its handling of the crisis. According to a newly released House Benghazi report, State Department officials held an emergency conference call on the night of the attack, where they discussed the possibility of deploying Marines to the scene. However, concerns were raised about the potential impact of the Marines' uniforms on regional diplomacy, with Under Secretary of State Patrick Kennedy expressing worries that the uniforms could exacerbate tensions.

Despite these concerns, the military ultimately decided not to deploy Marines to Benghazi. Instead, the focus shifted to evacuating American personnel from the area. However, the process was marred by logistical challenges, with soldiers reportedly having to change in and out of their uniforms multiple times before being redeployed.

As the situation unfolded, the Obama administration struggled to provide a coherent explanation for the attack. Initially, Secretary of State Hillary Clinton pointed to an anti-Islamic video as the catalyst for the violence, despite internal communications suggesting that the attack was actually a premeditated terrorist strike. This narrative was further complicated by conflicting statements from various administration officials, with some acknowledging that the attack was likely orchestrated by extremist groups.

Internal emails and memos reveal that senior officials, including Clinton and White House advisor Ben Rhodes, were aware of the discrepancy between their public and private assessments of the attack. Despite this, they continued to push the narrative that the video was responsible for the violence, even as eyewitness accounts and intelligence reports contradicted this claim.

The administration's efforts to pin the blame on the video were met with skepticism by some within the State Department, with one diplomat cautioning against drawing attention to the film in local messaging. Meanwhile, the CIA's director, Michael Morell, acknowledged in a written statement that the agency had initially assessed the attack as a terrorist operation.

As the days passed, the administration's narrative continued to evolve, with officials eventually acknowledging that the attack was indeed a terrorist strike. However, the damage had already been done, with the public left with a distorted understanding of the events surrounding the Benghazi attack. The controversy surrounding the incident has continued to simmer, with Democrats on the House Benghazi committee accusing Republicans of attempting to politicize the investigation and withholding key information from the public.

reference: – The Republican-led House Benghazi committee released its final report Tuesday after two years of investigations. The 800-page report lays out the committee's findings on the 2012 attacks in Libya that left four Americans dead. Here's what you need to know: Benghazi is "unlikely to be a potent tool for weakening Clinton" in the general election, as the committee and report have little credibility and aren't seen as serious by many, the Los Angeles Times reports. Easily distilling the massive report, the Washington Post lays out its five "most serious accusations," including that the CIA "missed warning signs" and "misread how dangerous Libya ... was at the time." New evidence of “culpability or wrongdoing” on Clinton's part is lacking in the report, despite it being “one of the longest, costliest, and most bitterly partisan congressional investigations in history," according to the New York Times. Given a lack of new evidence against Clinton, the Weekly Standard uses the report as a tool for blaming Obama. “The Obama administration knowingly provided the American people a false story about the Benghazi attack, its causes, and its consequences," the Standard states. How various government agencies responded to the Benghazi crisis is one of the more interesting aspects of the report. According to Politico, a conference call in the midst of the attack included a conversation about whether Marines should wear their uniforms if deployed. Marines changed in and out of their uniforms multiple times while waiting for an answer and were never deployed. According to the Hill, a Democratic lawmaker on the House Benghazi committee calls it "one of the saddest exercises I've ever engaged in" during 20 years in Congress. He accuses Republicans of hiding the final report from Democrats to avoid having it fact-checked. Zeroing in on what it calls the "most revealing paragraph" in the report, Vox concludes there's a pattern of spinning "non-damning facts as damning" for the Obama administration. The paragraph in question admits there were no US forces close enough to prevent the attack but insists that, in itself, was a failure on the part of the White House. "I think it's time to move on," the AP quotes Clinton as saying after the release of the report, which she says includes nothing not previously discovered by an accountability board.

final_summary:
Eli Rosenbaum, a dedicated Nazi hunter and director of the Justice Department's Office of Human Rights and Special Prosecutions, has spent his career tracking down and pursuing justice for former Nazis who fled to the US after World War II. Born into a Jewish family, Rosenbaum's interest in Nazi hunting began when he was a child, watching a TV dramatization of the Auschwitz trial, which left a lasting impression on him. This sparked a lifelong commitment to uncovering the truth about Nazi atrocities and bringing perpetrators to justice.

Rosenbaum's work involves investigating and prosecuting individuals who served as Nazi guards, often using scarce documentation and relying on witnesses who are increasingly difficult to find due to the passage of time. The cases are considered "cold" due to the decades-long delay between the crimes and the pursuit of justice. As Rosenbaum notes, "Our World War II cases are the ultimate cold cases... It's very much a search for the proverbial needle in a haystack."

One such case involves Jakiw Palij, a 92-year-old man living in Queens, New York, who was a guard at the Trawniki concentration camp in Poland. Palij claims he was coerced into serving the Nazis, but Rosenbaum and the Justice Department argue that he directly contributed to the slaughter of thousands of people by forcing prisoners to work or preventing them from escaping. In 2004, a federal judge ordered Palij's deportation, but none of the three European countries to which he could be sent have agreed to accept him.

Palij's case highlights the challenges faced by Nazi hunters like Rosenbaum, including the difficulty of gathering evidence and the reluctance of countries to accept deportees. Despite these obstacles, Rosenbaum remains committed to pursuing justice for the victims of the Holocaust, saying, "What Mr. Palij did prevented other people from reaching old age. He served at the Trawniki SS training and base camp – really a school for mass murder – and he trained on live Jews at the adjacent Trawniki Jewish Labor Camp. And, in the end, everyone who was held there was massacred."

Since 1979, the Justice Department's human rights unit has successfully stripped 73 former Nazis of their US citizenship and deported 59 of them. Rosenbaum, who has led 137 cases, including the conviction of John Demjanjuk, an accessory to over 28,000 murders at the Sobibor death camp, continues to work tirelessly to bring closure to the families of victims and to hold accountable those responsible for the atrocities of the Holocaust.

The US Holocaust Memorial Museum has supported Rosenbaum's work by providing access to documents collected on war crimes, and experts like Peter Black, a senior historian at the museum, praise Rosenbaum's dedication to achieving justice for the victims. As Black notes, "You can't just decide that because someone gets away with [atrocities] for 40 years, that they are innocent. You have to do what you can to achieve justice for the victims, as a statement about our response to these crimes."

reference: – During some three decades with Justice Department, Eli Rosenbaum has earned a well-deserved reputation as a Nazi hunter. He has worked on 137 cases involving suspected Nazis, and in all but 30 of them, the accused lost citizenship or was deported. Now, CNN reports, just a single active case remains. But when it comes to Jakiw Palij, who lives in Queens, NY, deportation isn’t likely. In fact, a federal judge ordered the 92-year-old deported in 2004, but the European countries he could be sent to won't take him. Palij, CNN notes, will likely die here. "What Mr. Palij did prevented other people from reaching old age," says Rosenbaum, who now also oversees more recent war-related crimes as the DOJ's director of Human Rights Enforcement Strategy and Policy. Palij is accused of being a guard at the Trawniki death camp in German-occupied Poland—"in the end," Rosenbaum says, "everyone who was held there was massacred." Palij, who, per the New York Times, came to the US in 1949 as a refugee using falsified immigration papers, has denied any wrongdoing. In 2003, he told the Times that the Nazis coerced him into service patrolling bridges and roads at night. "We knew they would kill me and my family if I refused," he says. "I did it to save their lives, and I never even wore a Nazi uniform." The founder of the Simon Wiesenthal Center downplayed that line of argument: "To work at those camps, you had to be eager to be there. They only took people they knew were loyal and brutal and not sympathetic to pleas of the inmates." In a 2013 interview with NPR, Rosenbaum described questioning the suspects about their alleged Nazi pasts as "surreal ... these people look close to harmless." (Joseph Goebbels' "love nest" is proving to be a tough sell for Berlin.)

final_summary:
Steve Wynn's plans to sell a pair of valuable artworks at a Christie's auction were recently derailed due to unforeseen circumstances. One of the pieces, Pablo Picasso's 1943 self-portrait "Le Marin," suffered damage during the final stages of preparation for the sale, prompting its removal from the auction block. This development comes nearly a decade after Wynn himself caused significant damage to another Picasso artwork, "Le Reve," when he accidentally struck it with his elbow in 2006. The incident left a hole the size of a silver dollar in the painting, which was valued at $139 million prior to the accident but ultimately sold for $155 million to hedge fund manager Steve Cohen in 2013.

The damaged "Le Marin" was one of three artworks consigned to Christie's by Wynn, including a 1964 portrait of a woman with a cat, "Femme au chat assise dans un fauteuil," and Andy Warhol's 1963 "Double Elvis [Ferus Type]." While the latter remains scheduled for sale on May 17, the former was also removed from the auction due to a mutual agreement between the seller and Christie's. Both "Le Marin" and "Femme au chat assise dans un fauteuil" carried guarantees from Christie's, which typically covers damages and other contingencies under their consignment contracts.

Wynn's decision to withdraw these artworks from the auction may be seen as a strategic move given his recent resignation as CEO of Wynn Resorts Ltd. amidst allegations of sexual misconduct, which he has denied. Despite this setback, the remaining artwork, "Double Elvis [Ferus Type]," is set to proceed with the sale, estimated to fetch over $30 million. This piece previously sold at Sotheby's in 2012 for $37 million to the Mugrabi family, owners of one of the world's largest private Warhol collections.

reference: – Pricey mistake: A Picasso worth an estimated $70 million had to be yanked from an auction at Christie's this week because it sustained damage as workers were preparing it. It's not clear how bad the damage is to Le Marin, or The Sailor, or even how the damage occurred, but the auction house said in a statement that two outside conservators "have made recommendations for the successful restoration of the painting," reports ABC News. One weird twist: The painting belongs to former casino mogul Steve Wynn, who previously had bad luck with a different Picasso painting. Back in 2006, Wynn stuck his elbow through Picasso's Le Reve while showing it to friends in his Las Vegas office, reports Bloomberg, which adds that Wynn has a disease that messes up his peripheral vision. That restoration had a happy ending: The painting had been valued at $139 million prior to the damage, and Wynn eventually sold it for $155 million. This hasn't been a stellar year for Wynn: He stepped down as CEO of Wynn Resorts amid a slew of sexual harassment allegations.

final_summary:
U.S. Senator Mark Udall, a long-time advocate for government transparency, has urged President Barack Obama to fully declassify the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence's extensive study on the CIA's detention and interrogation program. The report, which took four years to complete and cost $40 million, details the CIA's use of waterboarding and other interrogation techniques considered torture by many experts. According to committee members, the report reveals that the CIA misled the Bush administration and Congress about the effectiveness of these methods.

The CIA has been resistant to releasing the report, citing concerns over national security. However, Senator Udall believes that the full declassification of the report is crucial in setting the record straight on this "dark chapter" in the CIA's history. He has threatened to block the confirmation of Caroline Krass as CIA general counsel unless there is significant progress on his request.

A separate controversy surrounding the report centers on allegations that the CIA monitored computers used by Senate aides to prepare the study, potentially violating an agreement between the committee and the agency. This incident has sparked a heated debate between the CIA and the Senate Intelligence Committee, with the two sides engaging in a closed-door battle over the report's contents.

The CIA's actions have been described as an "unprecedented breakdown" in relations between the agency and its congressional overseers. The dispute has also drawn attention from the White House, with officials closely tracking the situation but refraining from direct intervention due to their own disagreements with the committee over the release of sensitive documents.

The report's fate hangs in the balance, with some questioning whether any part of it will ever be made public. The document details how the CIA's interrogation techniques did not yield the intelligence that led to the killing of Osama bin Laden in 2011. Senator Ron Wyden, a member of the committee, has expressed concern over the CIA's potential violation of the Federal Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, which prohibits unauthorized access to computers.

In a letter to President Obama, Senator Udall called for the White House to take control of the declassification process, stripping the CIA of its authority to decide what parts of the report can be released. He also referenced an internal CIA review that confirms some of the committee's findings, acknowledging significant mistakes and errors made during the CIA's detention and interrogation program. The CIA has downplayed the significance of this document, describing it as a collection of summaries rather than an analytical report.

reference: – The CIA's Inspector General is calling for the Justice Department to look into allegations that the agency illegally spied on aides to the Senate Intelligence Committee as they gathered information for a potentially contentious report on the CIA's torture program, McClatchy reports. The CIA had insisted that the Intelligence Committee staffers use computers at Agency headquarters to review millions of documents related to its interrogation program. The committee believes the CIA monitored those computers, despite a specific agreement not to, according to McClatchy sources. The 6,000-page report remains classified, but the New York Times notes that it is a "withering indictment" of CIA tactics and results. Mark Udall yesterday sent President Obama a letter saying that, "As you are aware, the CIA has recently taken unprecedented action against the committee," without specifying what that action was. Committee chair Dianne Feinstein would confirm only that "there is an IG investigation." The committee has been battling to release its unflattering report, which the agency wants to keep classified. Udall accused the agency in December of withholding an internal review from the committee—and that apparently led the CIA to accuse the committee of gaining unauthorized access to agency databases, leading to this current dispute.

final_summary:
A 20-year-old woman called 911 on Tuesday morning, reporting that she was being held against her will in a multi-million dollar mansion in a gated suburban neighborhood near Atlanta. The woman, who had been living in the house for approximately a month, described her captor, identified as 33-year-old Kenndric Roberts, as a man who carried a gun and had ordered her to undergo plastic surgery. According to the 911 recording, Roberts had initially promised the woman a modeling career and financial assistance, but the situation quickly turned violent when she attempted to leave.

The woman told the dispatcher that she was one of eight women, aged 19 to 22, being held at the mansion, which was rented by Roberts. The women were reportedly forced to work at local strip clubs, with their IDs and phones confiscated by Roberts, who threatened to harm them or their families if they tried to escape. The woman claimed that Roberts had even threatened to pay someone to surgically remove her breast implants and mutilate her body if she didn't comply with his demands.

Police responded to the 911 call and rescued the eight women, who were taken to safety or reunited with their families. Roberts was arrested and charged with multiple counts of false imprisonment, human trafficking, and possession of a firearm in the commission of a felony. An AK-47 and a Glock.45-caliber handgun were seized from the premises.

According to investigators, Roberts used social media platforms, including SeekingArrangement.com, to lure the women into his trap, promising them modeling opportunities and financial assistance. However, once they arrived at the mansion, they were subjected to physical and emotional abuse, with some being forced to undergo unnecessary medical procedures.

The incident highlights the growing concern over human trafficking in Atlanta, which is considered one of the largest hubs for sex exploitation in the United States. Local authorities and anti-trafficking organizations, such as 4Sarah Inc., are working together to raise awareness about the issue and provide support to victims. The case serves as a reminder that human trafficking can occur anywhere, even in affluent neighborhoods, and that victims often feel trapped due to desperation and lack of resources.

reference: – A man has been charged with holding eight women against their will inside a million-dollar home in the suburbs of Atlanta, NBC News reports. Authorities say 33-year-old Kenndric Roberts met the women online and offered them modeling jobs. But once they moved into the 6,800-square-foot, five-bedroom house he was renting, things changed, according to the Atlanta Journal-Constitution. The women—who range in age from 19 to 22—tell police Roberts threatened them if they tried to leave, forced them to work at strip clubs, threatened their families, and made them get plastic surgery. One of the women referred to Roberts as "our boss," the AP reports. It's unclear how long the women had been in the home. What authorities now believe was a human trafficking operation was discovered Tuesday when one of the women called 911 and asked police to help her escape a "very bad situation" in a "very nice house." She said a man who had a "house full of girls" was threatening to kill her if she left. Police responded and found the eight women. Roberts was arrested on Wednesday. He's been charged with multiple counts of false imprisonment, trafficking persons for labor, and more. Federal charges are also possible. The women have been returned to their families or placed in safe houses.

final_summary:
On February 21, 2013, a deadly shooting erupted on the Las Vegas Strip, resulting in three fatalities and at least six injuries. The incident began with a dispute at a nearby hotel, which escalated into a violent confrontation involving two vehicles - a silver-gray Maserati and a black Range Rover SUV. The Maserati sped off at a high rate of speed, with the SUV following closely behind. At approximately 4:30 am, shots were fired from the SUV, striking the Maserati, which then crashed into a taxi cab that exploded in a fireball upon impact.

The taxi driver and passenger were killed instantly, while the Maserati driver was pronounced dead at a hospital shortly thereafter. A passenger in the Maserati was among the injured. The SUV, which had tinted windows, black custom rims, and paper dealer plates from out-of-state, fled the scene and was being sought by authorities. Officials contacted police departments in three neighboring states to be on the lookout for the vehicle, assuming that multiple individuals inside were armed and dangerous.

The incident marked the latest in a series of violent episodes on the Las Vegas Strip, which has raised concerns about safety for tourists and locals alike. Despite assurances from law enforcement that the area remains one of the safest in the world, the shooting has left many shaken. The investigation is ongoing, with police reviewing footage from traffic cameras and interviewing witnesses to determine the motive behind the altercation.

As the search for the SUV continues, the community is grappling with the aftermath of the tragedy. The Professional Drivers Association, a taxi driver organization, announced plans to donate $5,000 to the family of the slain taxi driver, Michael Boldon. Boldon, a 63-year-old man who had recently moved to Las Vegas to care for his mother, leaves behind a grieving family, including his brother, Tehran, who expressed a desire to see those responsible brought to justice.

The identity of the Maserati driver, Kenneth Wayne Cherry Jr., also emerged, revealing a young man with a passion for cars and music. Cherry, who went by the stage name "Kenny Clutch," was remembered by his attorney as a loyal and dependable individual who would do anything for his friends.

As the investigation unfolds, authorities are working to piece together the events leading up to the shooting, including possible connections to a nightclub altercation. Meanwhile, the Las Vegas Strip remains closed, with tourists and locals navigating the challenges of the shutdown, including delayed commutes and lost revenue for businesses. The incident serves as a stark reminder of the complexities and dangers that exist beneath the surface of the city's glittering facade.

reference: – Police in three states are still hunting for a Range Rover whose occupants opened fire on another car on the Vegas Strip this morning and set off a deadly chain reaction. Cops don't know most of the details, but they say it started with a dispute of some kind in one of the nearby hotels, then spilled into the street, reports the Las Vegas Sun. When the Range Rover caught up to a Maserati at a stop light, shots were fired from the SUV that sent the Maserati crashing into a taxi, which exploded upon impact. Three people—the Maserati driver, the taxi driver, and a taxi passenger—were killed, and three others were injured, reports the LA Times. Police are hoping to piece together more details by interviewing a passenger in the Maserati, who was injured in the melee. Earlier reports that gunfire came from both vehicles as part of a moving shootout were incorrect. The AP collects this quote from a tourist visiting from Manchester, England: "This doesn't happen where we come from, not on this scale. We get stabbings, and gang violence, but this is like something out of a movie. Like Die Hard or something."

final_summary:
On Sunday, David Crosby, the renowned singer-songwriter of Crosby, Stills & Nash, was involved in a car accident in the Santa Ynez Valley area of California. According to reports, Crosby was driving his 2015 Tesla at a speed of 55 miles per hour, which is the posted speed limit, when he struck a 46-year-old jogger named Jose Jimenez. The incident occurred near Crosby's residence, and emergency responders airlifted Jimenez to a nearby hospital where he was treated for multiple fractures, abrasions, and lacerations. Fortunately, Jimenez's injuries are reported to be non-life-threatening.

In a statement to E! News, a representative for Crosby expressed his concern and relief over the incident, stating that Crosby is "obviously very upset that he accidentally hit anyone" and wishes Jimenez a speedy recovery. The California Highway Patrol investigation revealed that Crosby failed to see Jimenez due to driving into the sun, which obscured his vision.

This incident comes amidst a tumultuous period in Crosby's personal life. In recent months, Crosby has maintained a relatively low public profile, although he did resurface last year to address a long-standing feud with fellow band member Neil Young. The dispute reportedly stemmed from Crosby's comments about Young's girlfriend, actress Daryl Hannah, whom he described as a "poisonous predator" in an interview with The Idaho Statesman. Young responded by expressing anger towards Crosby, prompting Graham Nash to intervene and downplay the situation, describing Young as merely "upset."

The incident also follows the release of Crosby's solo album, "Croz," which debuted in January. In an interview with Rolling Stone, Crosby discussed his decision to pursue solo projects outside of Crosby, Stills & Nash, citing a desire to challenge himself creatively and explore different musical styles. Despite the ongoing tensions within the band, Crosby, Stills & Nash have continued to perform together, including a recent appearance on The Tonight Show with Jimmy Fallon, where they paid tribute to Iggy Azalea's song "Fancy" with a humorous twist, replacing the original lyrics with references to their own initials, C-S-N-Y.

As news of the accident spread, fans and colleagues alike took to social media to express their well-wishes for Jimenez's recovery. Meanwhile, Crosby remains under investigation by local authorities, who will continue to probe the circumstances surrounding the incident.

reference: – A jogger in California is lucky to be alive after an encounter with folk-rock legend David Crosby. Jose Jimenez, 46, was on a road near Crosby's Santa Ynez when the singer hit him in his 2015 Tesla, which was traveling at or near the 55mph speed limit, CNN reports. Jimenez was airlifted to the hospital with fractures and other non-life-threatening injuries. Crosby stopped after the collision, and the California Highway Patrol says he's unlikely to face charges since the jogger was supposed to be on the other side of the rural road, running against traffic, E! Online reports. A CHP spokesman says Crosby "was not impaired or intoxicated in any way," but he didn't see the jogger because of the sun. "David Crosby is obviously very upset that he accidentally hit anyone," a rep tells E! Online. "And, based off of initial reports, he is relieved that the injuries to the gentleman were not life-threatening. He wishes the jogger a very speedy recovery." Apart from the accident, the 73-year-old singer-songwriter hasn't been in the news much lately, Rolling Stone notes, although he put out new solo album Croz early last year, and last fall confirmed that Crosby, Stills, and Nash would never tour with Neil Young again—but said he knows "at least 20 better guitar players than Neil."

final_summary:
Saddam Hussain, a 25-year-old marine engineer from Jamshedpur in Jharkhand, is struggling to find employment due to his namesake, the infamous former Iraqi dictator Saddam Hussein. Despite graduating second in his batch of 2014 from Noorul Islam University in Tamil Nadu, Saddam has been rejected 40 times in job interviews with multinational shipping companies. Initially, he couldn't understand why he was being rejected, but after inquiring with HR departments, he discovered that his name was the primary reason.

Employers fear that having a crew member with a name that evokes suspicion could create operational difficulties when crossing international borders. A top executive from TeamLease Services, a leading recruitment consultant, acknowledged that individuals with such names may face issues with border patrol and airport authorities, potentially causing problems for both the individual and the employer.

Saddam attempted to rectify the situation by changing his name to Sajid through legal means, obtaining a new passport, driver's license, and other necessary documents. However, he soon realized that his university would not change his degree certificate without altering his Class 10 and 12 exam certificates first. He approached the Central Board of Secondary Education (CBSE) for a name change but has yet to receive a response.

Driven to desperation, Saddam filed a petition in the Jharkhand High Court to compel the CBSE to change his name. Although the court has scheduled a hearing for May 5, the process is slow-moving. Meanwhile, Saddam continues to struggle, spending sleepless nights as his batchmates secure employment.

Saddam's story is not unique; others share similar experiences. In Iraq, several individuals bear the same name, facing various forms of persecution and harassment. One journalist, also named Saddam, recounted how his father lost his government job due to his association with the Ba'ath party, while another individual shared a harrowing experience of being captured by a Shia militia. Even in India, Saddam's name has caused problems, with some people mistaking him for the notorious dictator.

reference: – Saddam Hussain's name is making it difficult for him to get a job. "People are scared to hire me," the 25-year-old Indian marine engineer tells the Hindustan Times. Hussain was, yes, named for that Saddam Hussein; the name was given to him by his grandfather. It turned out to be a poor choice, as the young man has been unable to find a job since graduating university two years ago, despite having done very well in school. And he's applied for dozens of positions. He says he's spoken with some of the HR departments of companies he's applied with, and they told him his name was the problem, because it could cause "suspicion." One recruitment expert points out that this would be a problem especially if a job requires travel abroad, during which he might get stopped by airport or border authorities: "He might just keep getting stuck or the company has to pull him out of the sticky situation, making the hire cumbersome," the expert says. Hussain got his first name legally changed to Sajid, but though he got all his official documents changed to reflect his new name, his original name still poses a problem because it's listed on educational certificates, which prospective employers want to see. He's working on getting those certificates changed as well, but the process is moving slowly, the BBC reports. (Meanwhile, in America, a guy is trying to change his name to Hitler.)

final_summary:
On September 8, 2015, a disturbing incident occurred at the Rőszke border hotspot in Hungary, where a camerawoman employed by the Hungarian nationalist television channel N1TV, identified as Petra László, was caught on camera tripping a man carrying a child in his arms while attempting to flee from police officers. The incident was captured on a 20-second video shared by Stephan Richter, a reporter for the German television channel RTL, which quickly went viral on social media platforms.

As the man fell, László continued filming, and the child was left injured. The camerawoman then kicked two other refugee children, a young girl and boy, as they attempted to flee the scene. This shocking display of aggression sparked widespread outrage and condemnation across the globe, with many calling for László to be held accountable for her actions.

N1TV's editor-in-chief, Szabolcs Kisberk, released a statement announcing that László's employment had been terminated with immediate effect, citing her "unacceptable behavior." However, opposition parties in Hungary, including Együtt-PM and the Democratic Coalition, announced plans to initiate charges of violence against a member of the community against László, a crime punishable by up to five years in prison.

The incident highlighted the tense atmosphere at the Rőszke border hotspot, where hundreds of refugees had gathered in an attempt to escape the police and continue their journey to Germany and Austria. The situation had escalated over several days, with many refugees attempting to breach the border despite the presence of law enforcement.

Social media users expressed their disgust and anger towards László's actions, with some accusing the N1TV channel of promoting a biased and xenophobic agenda, echoing the views of Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán, who has taken a hardline stance on immigration. The incident led to the creation of a Facebook group, "The Petra László Wall of Shame," where hundreds of people shared their outrage and disappointment over the camerawoman's behavior.

Photographers from various news organizations, including Reuters, captured the incident, providing further evidence of László's actions. The images showed the man carrying the child falling to the ground after being tripped by the camerawoman, leaving the child vulnerable and exposed. The incident serves as a stark reminder of the human cost of the ongoing migrant crisis in Europe and the need for compassion and understanding in the face of adversity.

reference: – A journalist working for a far-right TV station is out of a job after she was unable to resist her urge to become part of the story she was covering. The Hungarian camerawoman was filmed kicking and tripping refugees as they tried to get past police at an overcrowded camp in southern Hungary near the border with Serbia yesterday, the New York Times reports. A German journalist filmed Petra Laszlo of N1TV as she tripped a man carrying a young child, causing him to fall on the boy, the Guardian reports. Laszlo was also filmed kicking two children as they made their way past the police line. N1TV, which has close links to the far-right Jobbik party, says she has been let go for "unacceptable behavior." The Washington Post reports that many commentators describe her shocking behavior as more evidence that the Hungarian media is strongly biased in favor of Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who has stepped up the construction of a border fence and says the influx of people needs to be blocked to "keep Europe Christian."

final_summary:
Edward Snowden, the former US intelligence analyst turned whistleblower, has been on the run since revealing classified information about the National Security Agency's (NSA) global surveillance program. Snowden, who claims to be a "stateless person" after having his passport revoked by the US government, has applied for asylum in numerous countries, including India, China, France, Ireland, and Venezuela, among others. According to WikiLeaks, Snowden has submitted asylum requests to a total of 21 countries, although eight European nations have stated that such applications cannot be made from abroad.

Snowden's asylum bids have faced significant obstacles, with India rejecting his request outright, citing a "careful and restrictive policy on asylums." External Affairs Minister Salman Khurshid explained that India has not ratified the 1951 Refugee Convention and lacks a formal procedure for granting asylum. Similarly, several European countries, including Norway, Poland, Germany, Austria, Finland, Ireland, Spain, and Switzerland, have stated that asylum requests can only be made on their soil.

Russia, however, has taken a more complex stance on Snowden's asylum bid. Initially, the Kremlin indicated that it would consider Snowden's request, but with certain conditions, including ceasing his "anti-American activities." However, Snowden reportedly withdrew his application after learning of these conditions. Russian President Vladimir Putin emphasized that Russia would not hand over Snowden to the US, but rather allow him to stay in the country if he agreed to stop harming American interests.

Meanwhile, Ecuadorian President Rafael Correa has distanced his government from Snowden, stating that Quito respects the right to asylum but will only consider a request if Snowden reaches Ecuadorean territory. Correa claimed that the Ecuadorean consul in London, Fidel Narvaez, had issued a temporary travel document to Snowden without authorization, which allowed him to travel from Hong Kong to Moscow. Correa acknowledged that Narvaez had acted out of desperation to protect Snowden, but maintained that the document was invalid and would not be recognized.

In a letter to Correa, Snowden expressed his gratitude for Ecuador's consideration of his asylum request and praised the country's commitment to human rights. However, Correa's statements contradict Snowden's earlier assertions that Ecuador had facilitated his flight from Hong Kong. The Ecuadorean president emphasized that his government would not intervene in Snowden's situation, which is now largely in the hands of the Russian authorities.

As Snowden remains stranded in Moscow's Sheremetyevo airport, WikiLeaks has accused the US government of exerting pressure on countries to reject his asylum requests. Snowden himself has criticized President Barack Obama for using citizenship as a tool to intimidate individuals seeking asylum. The controversy surrounding Snowden's asylum bids highlights the complexities of international diplomacy and the challenges faced by whistleblowers seeking refuge from persecution.

reference: – Can Edward Snowden afford to be choosy? Authorities in Russia say the NSA leaker has reportedly canceled a request for asylum after balking at the conditions imposed by Vladimir Putin, including no longer leaking information damaging to the US, the AP reports. Snowden has applied for asylum in no fewer than 21 countries, according to WikiLeaks, but the rejections are starting to pile up: Germany, Norway, Austria, Poland, Finland, and Switzerland all say he needs to be on their soil to request asylum. And India this morning "concluded that we see no reason to accede to that request." Norway has confirmed that its Moscow embassy has received a fax that is "probably from him," while Poland's foreign minister says he has received an asylum request from Snowden that he will not recommend granting, the BBC reports. Requests have also been made to Bolivia, Brazil, China, Cuba, Ecuador, France, Iceland, Italy, Ireland, Netherlands, Nicaragua, Spain, and Venezuela, WikiLeaks says. Ecuador's president now says helping Snowden leave Hong Kong was "a mistake," the Guardian reports, but Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro—who is currently visiting Moscow—says his country has yet to receive an asylum request from Snowden, but the leaker has "done something very important for humanity" and "deserved the world's protection."

final_summary:
In a late-night session, the US Senate passed a sweeping tax overhaul bill, marking a significant milestone in the Republican Party's efforts to revamp the US tax system for the first time in over three decades. The bill, which includes approximately $1.4 trillion in tax cuts, aims to lower the corporate tax rate to 20% from 35%, reshape international business tax rules, and temporarily reduce individual taxes. The legislation also addresses various Republican objectives, including opening the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil drilling and repealing certain provisions.

Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell played a pivotal role in navigating the bill through the Senate, where Republicans controlled a slim majority of 52-48 seats. Despite facing opposition from within their own ranks, McConnell managed to secure the necessary votes by offering concessions to several key senators, including Susan Collins of Maine, Ron Johnson of Wisconsin, and Steve Daines of Montana. These concessions included expanding tax breaks for small businesses, homeowners, and others, which ultimately swayed the votes of the aforementioned senators.

The bill's passage was met with fierce criticism from Democrats, who argued that it disproportionately benefits corporations and the wealthy at the expense of lower- and middle-class Americans. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer described the bill as "removed from the reality of what the American people need," citing concerns that the legislation would exacerbate budget deficits and widen the wealth gap.

One of the key sticking points in the negotiations was the inclusion of a provision that would have exempted Hillsdale College, a conservative institution with ties to Education Secretary Betsy DeVos, from a new tax on university endowments. This provision was ultimately stricken from the bill after a contentious debate.

The bill's passage sets the stage for a final compromise between the Senate and House versions of the tax bill, which must be reconciled before being sent to President Donald Trump for signature. While the bill has been met with skepticism from some Republicans, including Senator Bob Corker, who opposed the measure due to concerns over its impact on the national debt, others, such as Senator Jeff Flake, have expressed support despite reservations.

The bill's provisions include a range of changes to the tax code, including the abolition of the Alternative Minimum Tax, the expansion of the standard deduction, and the elimination of certain itemized deductions. Additionally, the bill would allow drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge and repeal the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate, which requires most people to purchase health insurance or face a penalty.

With the Senate's passage of the tax bill, Republicans are poised to achieve a major legislative victory, which they hope will boost their chances in the 2018 midterm elections. However, the bill's popularity remains uncertain, with many Americans expressing skepticism about its potential impact on the economy and the distribution of tax burdens.

reference: – It passed in the wee hours, and by a wee margin: The Senate passed the biggest tax overhaul in more than 30 years Saturday morning 51-49. Tennessee's Sen. Bob Corker was the only Republican to side against the bill. With the House having passed its bill in November, Congress is just a few steps away from getting something on President Trump's desk. He tweeted in part, "Look forward to signing a final bill before Christmas!" Politico reports the final hours before the vote were filled with "horse trading" and some ridicule from Democrats: Minority Whip Dick Durbin tweeted a photo showing a page of the bill with semi-legible handwriting scrawled in the margin, saying, "Trying to review the #GOPTaxScam but they are making hand-written changes to brand new text as we speak – can anyone else read this?" Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer swung at Republicans for releasing a revamped 479-page bill that senators didn't have time to review. GOP Sen. Ron Johnson's take, per the AP: "You really don't read this kind of legislation." Among the numerous last-minute amendments that were debated: A controversial provision by GOP Sen. Pat Toomey that would have given Michigan's conservative Hillsdale College a pass on a new tax on university endowments was axed. Politico reports the House will on Monday formally assemble the conference committee that will work to get the House and Senate measures to jibe; the Senate hasn't commented on a formal conference yet. The Wall Street Journal offers a chart that illustrates key differences between the two bills.

final_summary:
Celia Barquín Arozamena, a 22-year-old Spanish golfer and Iowa State University student, was found murdered on a golf course in Ames, Iowa, on September 17, 2018. The victim, who had recently won the European Ladies' Amateur Championship, was discovered with multiple stab wounds to her head, neck, and upper body. 

Collin Daniel Richards, a 22-year-old homeless man, was subsequently charged with first-degree murder. Authorities alleged that Richards had committed a random act of violence against Barquín Arozamena, who was described as an "outstanding team player" by the Royal Spanish Golf Federation. 

Richards had a history of run-ins with law enforcement, having previously been charged with domestic abuse, trespassing, public intoxication, and intimidation with a dangerous weapon. He had also served a seven-month prison sentence in June 2018. 

According to the police complaint, Richards had expressed an "urge to rape and kill a woman" to an acquaintance in recent days. A police dog tracked Barquín Arozamena's scent to a homeless encampment near the golf course, where Richards was found with scratches on his face and a laceration on his left hand. 

Two pairs of shorts with bloodstains and a knife were recovered from Richards, which he allegedly gave to others after the attack. Richards was arrested after asking two acquaintances to drop him off near the encampment to retrieve his tent. 

Barquín Arozamena was a rising star in the golfing world, having won numerous awards and accolades throughout her career. She was a three-time all-conference selection, earned first-team All-Big 12 academic honors three times, and qualified for NCAA regionals in all four years she competed. 

In addition to her golf achievements, Barquín Arozamena was a civil engineering major at Iowa State University and had been named the school's female athlete of the year in 2018. She was set to be honored at a football game on Saturday, but instead, the team wore helmet decals with her initials to pay tribute to her memory. 

The university and the golfing community were shocked by her sudden and tragic death, with many expressing their condolences to her family and friends. The case remains under investigation, with authorities considering it a random act of violence.

reference: – A 22-year-old golfer headed for the height of her sport was instead murdered on the course. Wrapping up a civil engineering degree at Iowa State University, where she'd been named female athlete of the year, Spain's Celia Barquín Arozamena was found dead a short distance from her unattended golf bag on the Coldwater Golf Links in Ames around 10:30am Monday, reports the BBC. After determining Barquín was assaulted, police by evening had charged 22-year-old Collin Daniel Richards of no known address with first-degree murder, per a release. Details weren't provided, though WOI-TV reports Richards has a history of arrests for intimidation with a dangerous weapon, trespassing, and public intoxication dating back to 2015. "This is a tragic and senseless loss of a talented young woman and an acclaimed student athlete," Iowa State president Wendy Wintersteen says, per ESPN. "She was a player who was heading for the very top, without a doubt," adds the technical director of the Spanish Golf Federation, per the BBC. Ranked 69th in the country by Golfweek, Barquín had secured an invite to next year's Women's British Open with a victory at the European Ladies' Amateur championship in July. Considered one of the most accomplished players in Iowa State golf history, the Puente San Miguel native also became the Big 12 women's golf champion and only the third Iowa State women's golfer to compete in the US Women's Open earlier this year, per the Independent.

final_summary:
Transport for London (TfL), the city's transport authority, has revoked Uber's operating license in a major blow to the ride-hailing giant. The decision, announced on Friday, states that Uber is not a "fit and proper" private car hire operator due to its lack of corporate responsibility regarding public safety and security. Specifically, TfL cited Uber's approach to handling serious criminal offenses and its use of software, known as Greyball, to block regulatory bodies from accessing its app and conducting regulatory or law enforcement duties.

Uber's current license expires on September 30th, allowing the company time to appeal the decision in court. The firm's CEO, Dara Khosrowshahi, confirmed that Uber will challenge the ruling, acknowledging that the company's reputation has taken a hit. In a letter to staff, Khosrowshi stated that a bad reputation comes with significant costs, emphasizing the importance of acting with integrity and learning from past mistakes.

London Mayor Sadiq Khan expressed support for TfL's decision, stating that companies operating in the city must adhere to high standards, particularly concerning customer safety and security. Khan emphasized that innovation and new technologies should not come at the expense of these fundamental requirements.

The decision has sparked a mixed reaction from various stakeholders. Many Uber users expressed opposition to the move, citing the convenience and affordability offered by the service. Some users, including those with disabilities, highlighted the importance of Uber in providing accessible transportation options. On the other hand, critics of Uber, including traditional black-cab drivers and employment rights campaigners, welcomed the decision, arguing that the company has consistently disregarded regulations and mistreated its drivers.

The revocation of Uber's license is seen as a significant setback for the company, which has faced numerous challenges globally due to concerns over safety and competition from established taxi industries. As Uber prepares to appeal the decision, the company faces pressure to reform its business model and address criticisms surrounding its treatment of drivers and commitment to public safety.

reference: – London's transport authority says it won't renew Uber's license to operate in the British capital, arguing that it demonstrates a lack of corporate responsibility with implications in public safety and security. Transport for London says the car-hailing app was not "fit and proper to hold a private hire operator license." It cited Uber's approach to reporting serious criminal offenses and its "approach to explaining the use of Greyball in London"; that's the software Uber reportedly used to block regulatory bodies from gaining full access to the app, preventing "officials from undertaking regulator or law enforcement duties." The AP reports London Mayor Sadiq Khan said he supported the decision, saying any operator of taxi services in the city "needs to play by the rules." He says that "providing an innovative service must not be at the expense of customer safety and security." Uber's current license is up on Sept. 30, but the Guardian reports it can appeal the decision over the next 21 days. It can keep operating "until any appeal processes have been exhausted," per Transport for London.

final_summary:
In the days leading up to Black Friday, a group of determined shoppers has already begun camping out in front of Best Buy in Beaumont, California. Among them are Vicky Torres and Juanita Salas, who arrived at the store on the previous Wednesday, securing their spots in anticipation of the holiday shopping event. They plan to wait through the night, taking turns sleeping and keeping each other company, until the store opens its doors on Black Friday. Their goal is to snag a 50-inch high-definition television for a significantly reduced price of $199.

According to store manager Eric Tschudy, the number of "campers" has increased each year, and the store appreciates the dedication shown by these early birds. For Torres and Salas, camping out is a strategic move, as they are on a fixed income and cannot afford to splurge on expensive gifts during the regular season. By camping out, they aim to secure the best deals available on the items they want.

While some passersby may view their actions as extreme or foolish, others have expressed admiration for their dedication. However, Torres and Salas emphasize that they are not there to help others or save spots for those who did not arrive early enough; their sole focus is on getting the items they need at discounted prices. In fact, Torres noted that some people have even asked them to save spots in line for themselves, highlighting the growing trend of Black Friday camping.

Supporting his wife's efforts is Jacob Alva, who has taken on the role of providing essential services such as food, phone charging, and bathroom breaks. He is confident in the safety measures put in place by the police and security personnel, allowing him to feel comfortable leaving his wife's spot unattended. As the big day approaches, Torres and Salas remain committed to their mission, fueled by hot coffee, snacks, and determination.

reference: – Some stores are trying to resist Black Friday madness by refusing to open on Thanksgiving, but for die-hard shoppers, it's never too early to get ready for the mega-sales. Take Vicky Torres and Juanita Salas, who were already bundled up and camped out in front of the Beaumont, Calif., Best Buy weeks before everyone starts defrosting their turkeys. They've been there since last Wednesday, ABC 7 reports, and they're "good to go" for the duration, Torres says, with warm clothing, hot coffee, and food. "The point is to get the sales, because everybody is on a fixed income and we don't have that kind of money to splurge," says Salas' husband, Jacob Alva. The two women alternate who stays overnight to save their places in line, though there's no indication from reports that anyone else is actually in said line yet. If both women have to leave for a bathroom break or for any other reason, Alva takes over, he tells the San Bernardino County Sun; he also charges their phones and brings them sustenance. He's not particularly worried about his wife's safety, claiming "the cops and security out here are really good." Torres and Salas did the same "fun" thing last year—Best Buy's Beaumont manager tells the Sun "we love Vicky here." So what's their holiday must-have this time around? A 50-inch high-def TV for $199, they tell ABC. (Cyber Monday might be even bigger than Black Friday.)

final_summary:
During Paris Fashion Week, Karl Lagerfeld, the renowned creative director of French fashion house Chanel, unveiled his latest fall-winter 2014-2015 collection in an unconventional setting – a mock Chanel supermarket. The Grand Palais, typically a venue for glamorous fashion shows, was transformed into a sprawling supermarket, complete with rows of Chanel-branded products, including pasta, cheese, sauce, detergent, and other everyday essentials.

Lagerfeld's vision was to blur the lines between high-end fashion and everyday life, drawing inspiration from the idea that luxury should be accessible and relatable. He stated, "For me, the supermarket is the pop art of today." The designer's team created over 500 unique labels and displayed more than 100,000 items, some of which will be donated to charity.

Models walked down the catwalk, pushing shopping carts and carrying metallic baskets adorned with Chanel's iconic logo, as they pretended to shop for groceries. The collection itself featured oversized tweed jackets paired with skinny pants and sneakers, as well as comfortable-looking jogging suits and bright pink shredded leggings. The soundtrack included loud pop music and public announcements, adding to the immersive experience.

The show was attended by A-list celebrities, including singer Rihanna and actress Keira Knightley, who sat in the front row alongside her husband, Klaxons singer James Righton. After the show, the audience was invited to take home some of the Chanel products, leading to a chaotic scene as attendees rushed to grab items from the shelves. Security guards had to intervene to prevent further chaos.

In contrast to Lagerfeld's supermarket-themed show, other designers showcased their collections in more traditional settings. Iris van Herpen's show featured a dramatic and unsettling theme, with models appearing to suffocate in plastic bags. Meanwhile, Alexander McQueen's Sarah Burton presented a cryptic collection with an 18th-century vibe, featuring A-line skirts and lace-up boots. Valentino's Maria Grazia Chiuri and Pierpaolo Piccioli drew inspiration from the swinging sixties, incorporating diaphanous fabrics and pop art elements into their designs.

Overall, Paris Fashion Week 2014 was marked by innovative and thought-provoking presentations from various designers, each pushing the boundaries of fashion and creativity.

reference: – Chanel's show at Paris Fashion Week yesterday had a somewhat pedestrian theme: The Grand Palais, where the show was held, was turned into a Chanel supermarket. There were rows of food and household items, all with Chanel-branded names like Coco beer and Tweed cola, and models pushed shopping carts or carried baskets through the aisles while music and fake announcements played ("the young Marine is waiting for her parents at the cashiers," for example); signs advertised "sales"—like "1 for the price of 2" and "50% markup." Altogether, more than 100,000 items were displayed with more than 500 different labels; some will be donated to charity, Reuters reports, but others were grabbed by audience members after the show ... and things got so crazy that security guards had to intervene, with the AP noting fashionistas' bags were actually searched upon exit, with stolen goods removed, though one apparently made off with a Chanel doormat. "For me the supermarket is the pop art of today," said designer Karl Lagerfeld ... though of course he also said he himself rarely actually visits supermarkets. Chanel last made headlines for featuring models in sneakers.

final_summary:
Harper Lee, the renowned author of "To Kill a Mockingbird," has filed a trademark infringement lawsuit against the Monroe County Heritage Museum in Mobile, Alabama, alleging that the museum has exploited her fame and the prestige of her Pulitzer-winning book without offering compensation. The lawsuit, filed in federal court, seeks to prohibit the museum from using the name of Lee's book to promote its activities or sell merchandise.

According to the complaint, the museum has become increasingly brazen in its trademark violations, particularly since Lee suffered a stroke and is now in poor health. The museum's attorneys deny the allegations, claiming that they have been honoring Lee's legacy for over two decades and that every statement in the complaint is false or meritless.

The museum, which attracts 30,000 visitors annually, has been selling merchandise featuring the book's title and characters, including t-shirts, coffee mugs, and other items. The lawsuit alleges that the museum's primary mission is to profit from Lee's work rather than preserve the town's history. However, the museum disputes this claim, stating that it has been hosting productions of the book for 25 years and selling related merchandise for about 20 years.

Lee's novel, which has sold over 30 million copies worldwide, is set in the fictional town of Maycomb, modeled after her hometown of Monroeville. While Lee has acknowledged that the book was inspired by her upbringing in the town, she maintains that the setting, plot, and characters are products of her imagination.

The lawsuit has sparked controversy in Monroeville, with some residents supporting the museum and others backing Lee. The museum's executive director, Stephanie Rogers, claims that most people in the town are behind the museum, but dissenting voices can be heard, with some residents expressing concern that the lawsuit is motivated by greed.

The dispute has also raised questions about Lee's mental capacity, with some speculating that her sister, Alice, and her lawyer, Tonya Carter, may be influencing her decisions due to her poor health. However, there is no concrete evidence to support these claims, and Carter has not commented on the matter.

The lawsuit is the latest development in a series of conflicts involving Lee, including a previous lawsuit against her literary agent, which was settled in September. The outcome of the current lawsuit remains uncertain, but it has already caused tension in the small town of Monroeville, where Lee's legacy is deeply revered.
