reference: – A 2-year-old girl was recently given a 50/50 chance of surviving stage 4 neuroblastoma cancer, and dad Adam Koessler wants to do everything in his power to boost those chances. The Australian man started putting cannabis oil in Rumer's food and noticed "extremely beneficial" and even "miraculous" results, he says—but on Jan. 2, he was arrested and charged with supplying dangerous drugs to a person under 16. While on the cannabis oil (which does not contain the compound that gets marijuana users high), Rumer was in less pain, had more energy, and was able to eat, gain weight, and even go outside. "Her skin color came back, her eyes were sparkling again," her father says. "Her cancer-ridden little body was alive again." Without the cannabis oil, her condition has declined and she's in intensive care on morphine, the Newcastle Herald reports. Medical marijuana advocates are standing behind Koessler, who—as part of his bail conditions—is not allowed any contact with Rumer. The NSW Hemp Party spokesman points out the fact that Rumer was put on opiate painkillers after being forced off cannabis oil and says, "The current legal position regarding the enforced medical treatment of an innocent child is unconscionable." The Cairns community is also rallying behind Rumer and her family, donating money toward Rumer's treatment, the Cairns Post reported last month, shortly after Rumer was diagnosed on Christmas Day. Two crowdfunding campaigns have raised more than $30,000, and a Change.org petition for Koessler to see his parental rights reinstated and medicinal cannabis oil to be decriminalized has more than 157,000 supporters. A Facebook page apparently run by Rumer's mother (her parents are not together, according to her dad's GoFundMe page) says her cancer was recently downgraded to stage 3. (This dad credits his son's recovery from cancer to cannabis oil.)

final_summary: A user of the platform had attempted to save their email notifications for content posted by Michele Munz, but encountered an issue during the process. The intended outcome would be receiving a daily email with a link to new content from Michele Munz whenever she posts something new. However, this feature requires specific conditions to be met: it sends emails only once per day and only when there are actual new matching items available.

reference: – A giant bell on a billboard in St. Louis will ring every time a child completes chemotherapy at a local hospital, per the AP. The 12-foot-tall metal bell went up Wednesday on a billboard over Interstate 44 that reads "Childhood wins another round against cancer." Hospital staff will use a mobile app to trigger the bell every time a child completes cancer treatment at St. Louis Children's Hospital, explains the St. Louis Post-Dispatch. The bell's speakers enable people passing by to hear it ring. The hospital has its own bell that children with cancer can ring to mark their last day of chemotherapy, and five to 10 kids a month do so.

final_summary:
In October, Senator Al Franken introduced an amendment to the FY 2010 Defense appropriations bill that aimed to prohibit the US government from contracting with companies that restrict their employees from taking workplace sexual assault cases to court. The amendment, which passed with a significant majority of 68-30 votes, included provisions that barred companies from preventing employees from suing over claims of assault, false imprisonment, intentional infliction of emotional distress, or negligent hiring practices. The amendment applied to contracts worth $1 million or more and allowed employees to sue both the alleged perpetrator and their employer under Title VII.

The amendment's language remained intact throughout the reconciliation process between the Senate and House versions of the bill, despite initial concerns that it might be stripped. A spokesperson for Senator Franken confirmed that the final version of the amendment was "remarkably strong" and maintained its original intent.

However, the amendment does contain a national security waiver clause, which permits the Department of Defense to exempt certain cases from going to court if they pose a threat to national security. However, for this exemption to be granted, the Secretary of Defense must personally justify the decision to Congress and make it publicly available.

This amendment has significant implications for companies like Halliburton, which has faced numerous allegations of mistreating female employees. One such case involves Lynda Darden, a former administrative associate at Halliburton's Alvarado Camp in Texas, who was allegedly fired in June 2009 after informing her supervisors of her pregnancy. Darden's lawyer, Todd Kelly, claims that she was the only employee laid off at the time, despite having more experience than others in the same position. Kelly has represented several women in cases against Halliburton and KBR, including Jamie Leigh Jones, who was gang-raped by coworkers in 2005 and subsequently locked in a shipping container.

Kelly describes Halliburton as a challenging environment for women, stating that the company fails to treat its female employees fairly. Darden is seeking damages for back pay and front pay in lieu of reinstatement, citing that her firing was a pretext for discriminatory purposes.

reference: – Does everybody's favorite energy contractor, Halliburton, hate procreation? A former Halliburton employee is suing the company for wrongful termination and sex discrimination because, in the words of her lawyer, "she apparently violated the company's policy against procreation." Halliburton said it fired the pregnant administrative associate as part of general lay-offs, but the canned worker points out no one else in her department was fired, including people with less experience. Additionally, soon after the firing Halliburton briefly posted a job listing online for her former position, reports the Dallas Observer. The woman's lawyer, Todd Kelly, has sued Halliburton several times in recent years, including over the gang-rape of a woman at former Halliburton subsidiary KBR, a defense contractor. "I don't believe that Halliburton treats its women as they should," Kelly said. The rape case led Minnesota Sen. Al Franken to sponsor a defense bill amendment stipulating that defense contractors can't bar lawsuits from employees for sex assault.

final_summary:
Adam Peterson, the 29-year-old son of Milwaukee County's Chief Medical Examiner, Dr. Brian Peterson, has become the latest victim of a devastating trend of narcotics-related deaths in the city. According to reports, Adam was found unresponsive at a friend's apartment on the 2700 block of West Clybourn Street, where first responders administered Narcan but were unable to revive him. He was pronounced dead at approximately 7:30 pm.

This tragic event marks the 141st suspected narcotics-related death in Milwaukee County, with over 70 such fatalities occurring within the past seven weeks alone. The county's medical examiner's office reported that this year is on pace to surpass the previous five years in terms of heroin-related deaths, exceeding the number of homicides recorded between 2011 and 2014.

Dr. Brian Peterson, who has headed the medical examiner's office since 2008, attempted to convey the gravity of the situation during a recent opioid roundtable discussion, stating that the number of overdose deaths far exceeds the combined totals of homicides, motor vehicle accidents, and infant sleep-related deaths. The office has since revised their projection for total drug-related deaths in 2016, citing an unprecedented 168 confirmed fatalities as of July 27, surpassing the 2012 total and nearing the record 254 deaths recorded in 2015.

Adam Peterson's death highlights the widespread nature of the crisis, with victims ranging in age from 1 to 63 years old. His autopsy revealed a history of substance abuse and the presence of medications such as topiramate and citalopram in his belongings. The investigation into his death continues, but it serves as a poignant reminder of the devastating impact of addiction on families and communities.

U.S. Senator Ron Johnson expressed his condolences to the Peterson family, emphasizing that no family is immune to the effects of addiction. The Milwaukee community has come together to address the issue, partnering with organizations like IMPACT to provide support and resources for those struggling with addiction. Those seeking assistance can reach out to IMPACT at 866-211-3380.

reference: – There have been more than 160 drug-related deaths in Milwaukee County, Wis., this year, 70 of them in the last seven weeks, and that is more than just a grim statistic for Chief Medical Examiner Brian Peterson. His own son, Adam Peterson, a 29-year-old with a history of drug use, was found on the floor of a friend's apartment Monday and was pronounced dead after failing to respond to anti-overdose drug Narcan, WISN reports. Brian Peterson, who has asked for privacy, told a conference earlier this month that his office was dealing with more overdose deaths than murders, car accident deaths, and sleep-related infant deaths combined, the Journal Sentinel reports. "We had a 1-year-old found dead on a mattress of methadone poisoning," Peterson told the round table. "Unfortunately, that's not uncommon either." He described medical examiners as "kind of the last responders," whose job it is "to help with clean data to help everybody understand." "I was sitting at the same table as Brian two weeks ago in Milwaukee discussing the trail of tragedies and heartbreak that addiction leaves in its wake," Sen. Ron Johnson tells the Journal Sentinel. "No family is immune. My prayers go out to Brian and his family at this time. I will continue to fight this fight for all those who have lost loved ones." (This small city in West Virginia had to deal with 27 overdoses in less than five hours.)

final_summary:
In a shocking discovery, a 36-year-old woman with special needs was found chained to a bed in the basement of a house on the 200 block of Linton Street in Philadelphia's Olney section. According to authorities, the woman was discovered by police during a response to a reported neighborhood dispute between the homeowner, Regina Bennett, 46, and a neighbor. Bennett was initially arrested for threats and assault against the neighbor, but further investigation led to the discovery of the woman in the basement.

The woman, who was extremely thin and had sores on her body, was found bound to a bed in "unsuitable conditions." Medics were called to the scene, and she was transported to Albert Einstein Medical Center for evaluation. Bennett, who is the woman's cousin and caretaker, according to some reports, faces multiple charges, including terroristic threats, simple assault, public drunkenness, aggravated assault, unlawful restraint, false imprisonment, and related offenses.

This incident bears striking similarities to another case in Southwest Philadelphia, where a 63-year-old deaf and mute man was held captive for months by an acquaintance, Dwayne Young, who allegedly profited from the victim's social security checks. In that case, the victim was found locked in a basement with no food, forced to urinate in a bucket, and subjected to physical abuse. Young has been charged with kidnapping, aggravated assault, forgery, receiving stolen property, and related offenses.

The case also echoes the infamous Tacony dungeon case, in which Linda Ann Weston and others were accused of imprisoning four people in a boiler room for their social security benefits. The motives behind these cases appear to be financial gain, with perpetrators targeting vulnerable individuals with disabilities to exploit their social security benefits.

Neighbors describe Bennett as having a dual personality, with one resident calling her "Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde." Bennett had reportedly expressed prejudice against non-African Americans and had a history of altercations with neighbors, including cursing out a neighbor who greeted her. Despite these red flags, Bennett had been caring for the woman with special needs, according to some reports, although investigators have yet to confirm their exact relationship.

Bennett remains in custody, facing serious charges, while the woman with special needs receives medical attention and support. This disturbing case highlights the need for greater awareness and protection of vulnerable individuals with disabilities, particularly those who rely on caregivers for their well-being.

reference: – It's the kind of story where you don't want the word "again" to come into play. But Philadelphia is once again in the news with the revelation that a woman with special needs was being held captive in a basement there. The Philadelphia Daily News reports that the discovery came in a roundabout way: Regina Bennett, 46, was arrested in the wee hours of Saturday morning after getting into an altercation with her neighbor; police were told there was a young child in her home, but when they searched it, they instead found a 36-year-old woman chained and zip-tied to a bed in "unsuitable conditions," reports MyFoxPhilly. Bennett's relationship to the victim remains murky: While some outlets describe the women as cousins, others say Bennett was the woman's caretaker. WPVI reports that it spoke with the victim's sister, Alissia Robinson, who puts her mental ability at that of a 10-year-old and says police told her the 36-year-old was found naked. Robinson says her sister began living with Bennett in 2007, and that she hadn't seen her for a year following a fight with Bennett. Bennett is staring down a dozen charges, including aggravated assault, kidnapping for ransom, and neglect of care.

final_summary:
American Outdoor Brands Corp., the parent company of Smith & Wesson, has reported a significant decline in profits and sales, citing "challenging market conditions" as the primary reason. The company's share price plummeted following the announcement, with a $4.7 million profit reported for the quarter, a stark contrast to the $37.6 million earned during the same period the previous year. Sales revenue dropped by nearly a third, from $233.5 million to $157.4 million.

CEO James Debney, a British national and a strong supporter of the National Rifle Association (NRA), acknowledged the devastating school shooting in Parkland, Florida, where a Smith & Wesson M&P 15.223 semi-automatic rifle was used to kill 17 students and teachers. However, he emphasized the company's commitment to supporting the right of law-abiding citizens to own firearms while continuing to develop solutions to enhance community safety.

Debney attributed the decline in sales to a shift in consumer behavior, stating that the fear-driven buying spree witnessed during the Obama administration has dissipated under President Donald Trump. This phenomenon, dubbed the "Trump slump," has affected multiple gun manufacturers, with Remington filing for bankruptcy and others experiencing significant losses. The slump is partly due to decreased demand, driven by factors such as fewer hunters and a reduced sense of urgency around personal protection in an era of declining crime rates.

Major retailers like Dick's Sporting Goods and Walmart have responded to the changing landscape by altering their gun sales policies, including discontinuing the sale of assault-style rifles and raising the minimum age for purchasing firearms to 21. The NRA has faced backlash, with several companies severing ties with the organization, and President Trump's stance on gun control remains uncertain, having expressed support for stricter regulations in recent days.

As a result, American Outdoor Brands has taken steps to mitigate the financial impact, including repaying $75 million in debt and extending the due date on another $75 million of notes to August 2020. The company has also revised its profit forecast downward, projecting earnings of 31-33 cents per share for the current fiscal year, significantly below analyst expectations. With the gun industry facing unprecedented challenges, American Outdoor Brands and its peers must adapt to the evolving market demands and regulatory environment.

history.draft:
American Outdoor Brands Corp., the parent company of Smith & Wesson, has reported a significant decline in profits and sales, citing "challenging market conditions" as the primary reason. The company's share price plummeted following the announcement, with a $4.7 million profit reported for the quarter, a stark contrast to the $37.6 million earned during the same period the previous year. Sales revenue dropped by nearly a third, from $233.5 million to $157.4 million.

CEO James Debney, a British national and a strong supporter of the National Rifle Association (NRA), acknowledged the devastating school shooting in Parkland, Florida, where a Smith & Wesson M&P 15.223 semi-automatic rifle was used to kill 17 students and teachers. However, he emphasized the company's commitment to supporting the right of law-abiding citizens to own firearms while continuing to develop solutions to enhance community safety.

Debney attributed the decline in sales to a shift in consumer behavior, stating that the fear-driven buying spree witnessed during the Obama administration has dissipated under President Donald Trump. As a result, American Outdoor Brands has taken steps to mitigate the financial impact, including repaying $75 million in debt and extending the due date on another $75 million of notes to August 2020. reference: – The CEO of the company that made the rifle used in the Parkland mass shooting says the firm "shares the nation's grief"—but it hasn't changed its mind about gun laws. "We share the nation’s grief over this incomprehensible and senseless loss of life, and we share the desire to make our community safer,” James Debney, CEO of American Outdoor Brands, said when announcing the firm's latest results Thursday, per the Guardian. He said the company, formerly known as Smith & Wesson, would seek "effective solutions" to safety issues, while still "protecting the rights of the law-abiding firearm owner." Debney is a strong supporter of the NRA, though he has said he never fired a gun before becoming CEO of a gun-maker. Debney blamed "challenging market conditions" for falling sales and a collapse in profits, Bloomberg reports. He added, however, that after the Parkland shooting, there had been "increased foot traffic" to gun stores, leading to "some increased sales," Reuters reports. "We will operate our business under the assumption that the next 12 to 18 months could deliver flattish revenues," said Debney, announcing that the company had cut its profits forecast and sales targets. In after-hours trading, the company's stock, already down more than 50% over the last 12 months, fell 12%. (The GOP is "flummoxed" by President Trump's push for gun control.)

final_summary:
Philippine authorities have expressed outrage over the portrayal of Manila in Dan Brown's latest novel, "Inferno", describing the city as the "gates of hell". The book, released earlier this year, features a character who recounts experiencing six-hour traffic jams, suffocating pollution, massive poverty, and a thriving child sex trade during a visit to the city. 

Francis Tolentino, chairman of the Metropolitan Manila Development Authority (MMDA), responded to the depiction by sending a letter to Brown and his publishers criticizing the "inaccurate portrayal of our beloved metropolis". Tolentino argued that while Manila does face significant challenges such as poverty and traffic congestion, the city's strong Catholic faith and compassionate residents are being misrepresented. He claimed that Manila is actually the "doorway to heaven" due to its spiritual significance and the presence of the "cradle of Catholicism".

However, Tolentino's assertion that Manila is not plagued by severe traffic congestion appears to contradict official data, as the city's notorious traffic jams often last for several hours. Despite this, Tolentino insisted that the traffic situation is manageable, stating that it typically lasts no longer than two hours.

Brown's previous works have faced criticism in the predominantly Catholic Philippines, with bishops labeling them "sacrilegious" for allegedly undermining church teachings. The Manila city council had also banned screenings of the film adaptation of "The Da Vinci Code" in 2006, citing offense to the Catholic Church.

Not everyone in the Philippines agrees with Tolentino's stance, with some Filipinos taking to social media to express support for Brown's portrayal of the city. One Twitter user quipped that Tolentino might not have visited Manila himself, given his seemingly idealized view of the city.

Senator-elect JV Ejercito echoed a different sentiment, suggesting that Manila requires a "massive rehabilitation" to address its numerous problems, including corruption and bureaucratic inefficiencies. Ejercito's father, former President Joseph Estrada, has recently been elected as the mayor of Manila, promising to restore the city to its former glory.

Tolentino has written to Brown, urging him to correct his portrayal of Manila and inviting him to visit the city to experience its true nature firsthand. He believes that a personal visit would allow Brown to see the city's hospitality and the kindness of its residents, which he claims are being misrepresented in the novel.

reference: – Manila officials were probably excited to learn that one of the characters in Dan Brown's latest sure-to-be blockbuster visits the city. That is, until they read the descriptions of the child-sex trade, pollution, and poverty, all of which prompted the character in Inferno to declare, "I've run through the gates of hell." Well. Manila official Francis Tolentino has written a strongly worded letter to Brown and his publisher complaining of the "inaccurate portrayal of our beloved metropolis" and demanding a correction of some kind, reports AFP. Tolentino even pored over Philippines travel records and declared that Brown has never visited the country. If he had, says Tolentino, Brown would know that instead of being the "gates of hell," Manila is actually ... wait for it ... the "doorway to heaven," reports ABS-CBN. (And people call Brown a hack.) No word yet in response from the Da Vinci Code author.

final_summary:
Following a highly critical U.S. Department of Justice report into the fatal police shooting of Michael Brown, the Missouri Supreme Court has taken extraordinary measures to reform Ferguson's municipal court system. On March 16, state appeals Judge Roy L. Richter was appointed to oversee the court, tasked with implementing necessary reforms to respect defendants' rights and restore the integrity of the system. This decision came after the Justice Department report highlighted racial profiling and bigotry by police, as well as a profit-driven municipal court system in the predominantly black St. Louis suburb.

The report found that Ferguson's police and court systems operated as a money-making enterprise, generating nearly one-quarter of the city's annual budget through fines and fees. The court often imposed monetary penalties and issued arrest warrants when individuals failed to pay on time or missed court dates, resulting in relatively minor infractions leading to arrests and jail time. Furthermore, the report revealed instances of Ferguson officials receiving "fixed" tickets for colleagues and friends, citing a double standard based on racial stereotyping.

Ferguson Municipal Judge Ronald J. Brockmeyer resigned amidst the controversy, stating that he wanted to promote public confidence in the court and facilitate the city's healing process. His departure followed the firing of court clerk Mary Ann Twitty last week. Missouri Governor Jay Nixon praised the Missouri Supreme Court's actions, calling them "strong and appropriate."

In response to the changes, Judge Richter took charge of the court and began making reforms. During his first session, he provided a detailed overview of the state's court system and announced plans to lower some of the fines. While some community leaders welcomed the move as a positive step towards reconstruction, others remained skeptical, arguing that more drastic measures were needed to address systemic issues within the police force and judicial system.

reference: – The Missouri Supreme Court is helping with the housecleaning in Ferguson: The court says that to "restore public trust and confidence" in the municipal court division, it's taking the "extraordinary action" of reassigning all municipal court cases to a state appeals court judge, reports the New York Times. Ronald Brockmeyer, the Ferguson judge who doubles up as a prosecutor, resigned from both roles yesterday. The 70-year-old tells the St. Louis Post-Dispatch that he doesn't agree with a Justice Dept. report on the biased and "abusive" practices he allegedly played a key role in, including the "creative" use of fines and fees to raise money for the city, "but it's not worth fighting." The top Missouri court says it's assigning Judge Roy L. Richter to hear pending and future cases in Ferguson, and the appeals court judge will also have the power to "restore the integrity of the system" with a revamp of municipal policies, reports the AP. The court says it is also looking at statewide reforms. St. Louis University law professor Brendan Roediger tells the Times that this is the first case he knows of where a state court has taken over an entire municipal docket. "It's a very big deal because it actually is the solution," he says. "It puts the cases in front of full-time professional courts with no conflicts of interest."

final_summary:
Habeas corpus, a cornerstone of Western law dating back to the Magna Carta in 1215, ensures that individuals cannot be detained indefinitely without cause. The Founding Fathers enshrined this principle in the US Constitution, considering it essential to preserving liberty, justice, and democracy. However, in 2006, the Military Commissions Act (MCA) revoked the right to habeas corpus for those detained at Guantanamo Bay and foreigners labeled "enemy combatants," extending this provision to US citizens living abroad.

The majority of Guantanamo detainees were not captured by US forces but rather sold to the US by Afghan warlords for substantial bounties. Only ten of the nearly 800 detainees have been charged with crimes. Despite numerous court victories affirming the detainees' right to challenge their detention, the Bush administration has hindered their access to a fair trial. The Supreme Court has agreed to review the cases for the third time, aiming to uphold habeas corpus and hold the administration accountable.

The MCA's erosion of habeas corpus compromises the foundational principles of the US legal and constitutional framework, threatening the rights of all citizens. This development has significant implications, as it creates a second-class system of justice for non-citizens, including lawful permanent residents, who can be declared "unlawful enemy combatants" and denied due process.

In a separate development, a New York judge has granted a writ of habeas corpus to two research chimpanzees, Hercules and Leo, held at Stony Brook University. This historic ruling recognizes chimpanzees as potential legal persons, marking the first time an animal has been afforded such status under US law. The Nonhuman Rights Project, which filed the lawsuit, aims to secure the release of the chimpanzees and establish a precedent for the rights of other cognitively complex animals, including elephants, great apes, and whales and dolphins. While some experts caution against overinterpreting the ruling, the Nonhuman Rights Project sees it as a crucial step towards achieving the goal of bodily liberty for these animals.

While some experts caution against overinterpreting the ruling, the Nonhuman Rights Project sees it as a crucial step towards achieving the goal of bodily liberty for these animals.

reference: – The writ of habeas corpus lets prisoners appear in court to make their captors justify why they're being held. Until now in the US, those who've used this legal tactic have been human. But a New York judge yesterday ruled that the writ may be used by two lab chimpanzees at Stony Brook University, Science magazine reports. NY Supreme Court Justice Barbara Jaffe ordered a university rep to address a suit filed by the Nonhuman Rights Project by appearing in court May 6 to argue why the college is holding Hercules and Leo. The group, which says the animals are being detained for biomedical experiments, says its ultimate goal is to have the animals released to the Save the Chimps sanctuary in Fort Pierce, Fla., per the NhRP's website. "This is a big step forward to getting what we are ultimately seeking: the right to bodily liberty for chimpanzees and other cognitively complex animals," Natalie Prosin, the project's executive director, tells Science. The case kicked off in December 2013 with lawsuits that also included two chimps held on private property; each case was struck down, sending the NhRP down the appeals path. Not everyone's convinced the upcoming hearing will mean liberation for the chimps. A Pepperdine University law professor tells Science that the judge likely just wants to hear both sides of the story: "It would be quite surprising if the judge intended to make a momentous substantive finding that chimpanzees are legal persons" without doing so, he notes. Prosin, meanwhile, says her group isn't stopping with the chimps and will also target the release of other animals. (Click to read about what chimps talk to each other about.)

final_summary:
Johnny Bobbitt Jr., a 35-year-old homeless Marine Corps veteran, gained international recognition after using his last $20 to purchase gasoline for a stranded motorist, Kate McClure, on an Interstate 95 exit ramp in Philadelphia. McClure, 28, and her boyfriend, Mark D'Amico, 39, launched a GoFundMe campaign to raise money for Bobbitt, which ultimately collected over $402,000 from more than 14,000 donors. Initially, the couple provided Bobbitt with some of the funds, but he claims they have withheld the majority of the money, allegedly spending it on personal expenses and luxury items, including a camper, which they purchased for him but later forced him to vacate due to his homelessness.

Bobbitt's attorneys argue that McClure and D'Amico have committed fraud by mismanaging the funds and using them for their own benefit, rather than following through on their promise to establish trusts for Bobbitt's financial security. According to the lawsuit, the couple has refused to provide Bobbitt with access to the remaining funds, citing concerns about his alleged substance abuse issues. However, D'Amico has acknowledged that there is still over $150,000 left from the donations.

Bobbitt's situation has taken a turn for the worse, as he has relapsed into homelessness and is currently panhandling on the streets of Philadelphia. His attorneys have filed a lawsuit in the Superior Court of New Jersey in Burlington County, seeking compensatory damages, interest, and an accounting of the funds raised on GoFundMe. The lawsuit also requests the appointment of a receiver to oversee the account and ensure that the funds are used for Bobbitt's benefit. A hearing on the matter is scheduled for Thursday.

reference: – The Philadelphia couple who raised more than $400,000 for a homeless veteran are using the fund as their "personal piggy bank," lawyers for Johnny Bobbitt Jr. write in a lawsuit filed Tuesday. Though lawyers say Bobbitt is staying at a hotel during a third stint in rehab, the 35-year-old Marine Corps veteran had been back to panhandling and battling a drug addiction on Philadelphia's streets despite a generous GoFundMe campaign organized by Kate McClure, 28, and Mark D'Amico, 39. They started the campaign after Bobbitt used his last $20 to buy gas for a stranded McClure and later bought a camper in which Bobbitt resided on property owned by McClure's family. Asked to leave in June because of drug use, per the Philadelphia Inquirer, Bobbitt alleges he's received just $75,000 of what he's due. McClure and D'Amico have interfered, spending more than half of the $402,000 raised "to fund a lifestyle that they could not otherwise afford," the lawsuit claims, per ABC News. Bobbitt previously wondered at how McClure, a receptionist, could afford a new BMW. But the couple have said only $500 of the fund was improperly spent on gambling and repaid. Noting well over $150,000 remains but is being withheld from Bobbitt for fear he will spend it on drugs, the couple say they'll open the fund to review by an accountant, per the Inquirer. Too late, according to Bobbitt's lawyers, who say the lawsuit seeking a court-appointed overseer of the account came after D'Amico ignored repeated requests for a full accounting. "He's really left us with no choice but to go forward," one tells the AP. A Thursday hearing is set.

final_summary: Tom Hanks served as a juror in a domestic assault case in Los Angeles, where a defendant faced up to a year in jail for allegedly striking his girlfriend. Defense attorney Andrew Flier initially expressed concern that Hanks' celebrity status could sway the jury's decision, but ultimately chose him for the final spot on the panel after finding him to be a fair and impartial juror. During the trial, Hanks took extensive notes, interacted with fellow jurors, and even laughed at a lawyer's joke. However, prosecutors revealed that another lawyer from the Los Angeles City Attorney's Office had interacted with Hanks outside the courtroom, thanking him for his service, prompting Flier to request a mistrial due to potential prosecutorial misconduct. The court instead agreed to reduce the charges against the defendant to disturbing the peace and imposed a $150 fine. City Attorney's Office spokesperson Frank Mateljan confirmed the interaction between the prosecutor and Hanks, stating that the office would review the matter. Representatives for Hanks did not respond to requests for comment, while the alleged victim's reaction to the resolution remained unknown due to confidentiality protocols surrounding domestic violence cases. Despite speculation about Hanks' potential influence on the verdict, Flier stated that Hanks appeared committed to delivering a just outcome, saying he had indicated he would "vote the way of justice" without revealing his intended decision.

reference: – Tom Hanks was just trying to do his civic duty, and he ended up inadvertently messing up a trial. Hanks was serving on a jury in LA this week, but when a lawyer in the City Attorney's Office made the mistake of talking to the actor, the case had to be settled with a plea deal. The prosecuting attorney revealed the interaction Tuesday and asked for a mistrial based on prosecutorial misconduct, CNN reports. Instead, yesterday, both sides agreed to a reduced charge. The defendant initially faced up to a year in jail on domestic assault charges; the reduced disturbing the peace charge carries with it just a $150 fine. So what did the lawyer approach Hanks about? He reportedly thanked the actor for his service. TMZ earlier reported that Hanks was either an awesome juror or just acting like one: taking lots of notes, listening closely, talking to the other jurors during the breaks, and laughing at one of the lawyer's jokes. The defense attorney confirms to CNN that, though he was initially skeptical of putting Hanks on the jury, "He never looked or made any statements like he wanted to get off jury duty. So based on everything, he seemed like a very fair juror." How would he have voted? The defense attorney says Hanks simply "looked at me like he always had, smiled and said, 'I was going to vote the way of justice.'"

final_summary:
Scientists have pinpointed the origin of the AIDS pandemic to the 1920s in Kinshasa, the capital of what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo. According to a recent study published in the journal Science, a "perfect storm" of factors, including rapid population growth, a thriving sex trade, and the presence of unsterilized needles in health clinics, created an environment conducive to the spread of the virus. 

Researchers from the University of Oxford and the University of Leuven in Belgium used advanced genetic sequencing techniques to analyze hundreds of HIV samples from different time periods and locations, allowing them to reconstruct the virus's family tree and identify its ancestral roots. The study found that HIV emerged in Kinshasa in the 1920s, where it spread rapidly due to the city's extensive transportation network, including railways and rivers.

By the 1940s, over a million people were passing through Kinshasa on the railways each year, facilitating the virus's spread to neighboring regions. The researchers believe that the virus was also transmitted through the use of unsterilized needles in health clinics and the changing behavior of sex workers, who catered to a predominantly male clientele.

As the virus spread, it formed secondary reservoirs in cities such as Mbuji-Mayi and Lubumbashi in the south and Kisangani in the north, from which it eventually spread to countries in southern and eastern Africa. The pandemic gained momentum after the Republic of the Congo achieved independence in 1960, allowing the virus to break out of isolated groups and spread globally.

While HIV has likely jumped from apes to humans numerous times throughout history, only one strain, known as HIV-1 group M, has gone on to cause a global pandemic. Researchers believe that the unique combination of factors in Kinshasa in the early 20th century created a "perfect storm" that allowed this strain to spread uncontrollably, resulting in the devastating impact of the AIDS pandemic, which has infected nearly 75 million people worldwide.

reference: – More than half a century before AIDS even had a name, the HIV virus was already spreading across Africa via the city now known as Kinshasa, researchers say. In the 1920s, the city was Leopoldville, capital of the Belgian Congo, and scientists say a "perfect storm" of conditions caused the pandemic to emerge, the BBC reports. The fast-growing city was full of transient male workers who visited prostitutes, researchers say. Sexually transmitted diseases were rife, and unsterilized needles at health clinics also helped spread the disease, according to a study published in the journal Science. Researchers studied HIV's genetic code as well as historical documents. The researchers note that strains of HIV have been transmitted from primates to humans at least 13 times, but only one has caused a global epidemic. They believe a key factor in the 1920s spread was the Congo's booming transport network early last century, which saw a million people travel through Kinshasa every year on the railways alone, reports Reuters. Researchers say the disease was at first confined to specific groups of people, but it started to spread into the general population and around the world after 1960, the Guardian reports. It took genetic research to track the pandemic back to its source because the disease hits the immune system, meaning victims died from any of a wide variety of infections, making the spread of HIV before it was identified hard to track through medical records, says the lead researcher, an evolutionary biologist. (HIV has infected 75 million people, but UN officials say real progress is being made and the epidemic could be over by 2030.)

final_summary:
On May 24, 2016, 15-year-old Aubrey Carroll went missing after being dropped off at the bus stop by his father, Michael Carroll, in Spalding County, Georgia. An extensive search operation ensued, involving the Spalding County Sheriff's Office, the FBI, and other local authorities. Initially, it was believed that Aubrey might have run away, but as time passed, concerns grew that he may have fallen victim to foul play or exploitation. 

The case remained unsolved for nearly two years, with Aubrey's family and investigators re-interviewing witnesses and re-examining evidence. In June 2017, the Spalding County Sheriff's Office reopened the case, assigning a new investigator, Todd Hendrix, to work on the case. Despite offering a $5,000 reward for information leading to Aubrey's whereabouts, no concrete leads emerged.

However, in April 2018, investigators made a breakthrough when they discovered that Aubrey had created a Facebook page under an assumed identity. Through this account, they tracked his movements across multiple states, including Alabama, Arizona, and others on the West Coast and Midwest. Authorities revealed that Aubrey had become part of a group living off the grid, using cash-only transactions and traveling between states.

Sheriff Darrell Dix led the investigation, which involved collaboration with the FBI and Texas Rangers. In a meeting with Aubrey's parents, officials presented them with evidence of their son's activities, including his interactions with law enforcement agencies. They chose not to contact Aubrey directly, fearing he might disappear again, but instead waited for him to initiate contact.

That opportunity arose when a family member reached out to Aubrey, convincing him to speak with his mother. Aubrey eventually agreed to return home, with specific conditions regarding who he would interact with and where he would go upon his return. On the evening of April 16, 2018, Aubrey's mother informed authorities that her son was safe and with her. The next day, Sheriff Dix and Lt. Mike Morris met with Aubrey to discuss his disappearance.

Aubrey revealed that he had left voluntarily and had not been harmed or exploited in any way. His story, according to Sheriff Dix, was "absolutely amazing," detailing experiences that left investigators stunned. Aubrey expressed gratitude to those who had prayed and searched for him, urging others with missing loved ones to maintain hope and perseverance. With his safe return, the case was officially closed, and authorities acknowledged the crucial role played by Aubrey's family and the investigators in bringing him home.

reference: – A Georgia teen who failed to return home from school nearly two years ago has resurfaced in the state with a new look. Aubrey Jayce Carroll, who turned 17 in January, appeared alongside Spalding Sheriff Darrell Dix in a video posted to Facebook Tuesday, sporting long hair and a beard, reports the Macon Telegraph. Though the formerly well-groomed teen didn't detail where he's been since leaving his Griffin home in May 2016, Dix says Aubrey "traveled extensively on the West Coast and Midwest" after joining a group of people who barter and use only cash. "They basically looked like a group of people from the Woodstock era in their clothing and lifestyle," Dix notes in a press release, per 11Alive, adding Aubrey encountered police, including in Alabama and Alaska, at least five times during his travels. Just last year, Dix said Aubrey's family was "tortured by the lack of information" about the missing teen. That changed last Tuesday, when authorities met with his parents and showed them a Facebook page Aubrey was using under a different name, reports NBC News. The group decided not to contact the teen for fear he'd go dark again (due to his age, he couldn't be forced to return home), opting to wait until he had another encounter with police. But one relative "went out on a limb" and messaged him anyway and apparently convinced Aubrey to contact his mother. He told her he was ready to come home so long as certain conditions were met regarding who he saw and where he went. Though much of his time away remains a mystery, Dix says Aubrey's "tale was absolutely amazing. He has seen and done things that make your jaw drop." (Another missing Georgia teen was found using Snapchat.)

final_summary:
In the Italian city of L'Aquila, a high-profile trial has begun against six scientists and a former government official, accused of manslaughter over their handling of a devastating earthquake that struck the city in 2009. The 6.3 magnitude quake resulted in the deaths of 309 people and left widespread destruction in its wake. 

Prosecutors claim that the defendants, all members of the Serious Risks Commission, failed to adequately warn residents of the impending disaster despite being aware of hundreds of low-level tremors that had shaken the city in the preceding months. A week prior to the quake, the commission issued a statement assuring the public that there was no reason to believe a major earthquake was imminent, although they also recommended stricter enforcement of anti-seismic measures, particularly in building construction.

The defense team argues that predicting major earthquakes is inherently unpredictable, even in areas known to be seismically active. They point out that the commission had clearly stated that it was impossible to forecast whether a stronger quake would occur, yet still managed to provide a warning about potential risks. However, critics argue that the commission's message may have been too vague and failed to convey the gravity of the situation.

The trial has sparked intense debate within the scientific community, with over 5,000 scientists signing an open letter in support of the defendants, arguing that the case amounts to putting science on trial. They contend that it is impossible to accurately predict earthquakes and that the defendants were doing their best with the available data. 

On the other hand, some survivors of the disaster, including Dr. Vincenzo Vittorini, whose wife and daughter perished in the quake, believe that the trial can serve as a catalyst for improved communication on seismic risks. He emphasized that residents were not expecting a precise prediction of the quake's timing but rather a warning that they were living in a high-risk area.

The trial has significant implications for Italy's approach to earthquake preparedness and risk assessment. The Italian National Institute of Geophysics and Volcanology (INGV) has long maintained a map of seismic risks across the country, with L'Aquila classified as a high-risk zone since 2006. The case highlights the challenges of balancing the need for accurate risk assessment with the limitations of current scientific knowledge.

As the trial continues, the defendants face up to 15 years in prison, while lawyers representing civil plaintiffs seek damages of €50 million. The outcome of the case will likely have far-reaching consequences for the scientific community, policymakers, and the general public, shedding light on the complexities of earthquake forecasting and the importance of effective risk communication.

reference: – Predicting earthquakes is shaky business at best, but that detail isn't stopping Italian officials from prosecuting a group of prominent scientists over their failure to do exactly that. Six scientists and one government official face charges of manslaughter for allegedly failing to provide adequate warning of imminent disaster to locals after the region was struck by more than 400 low-magnitude tremors in the months preceding a major quake. The group of experts met in 2009 to analyze the risk posed by the seismic activity, but didn't find cause to raise alarm. Six days later, a major quake devastated the central Italian city of L'Aquila and killed 309 people, reports the AFP. Among the defendants are some of Italy's top scientists in the field. "You cannot put science on trial," says a defense lawyer, citing his client's statement during the expert panel meeting that "one cannot rule out a major quake." The group has gained the wide support of the scientific community. But "no one expected to be told the exact time of the quake," says a doctor who lost his wife and daughter in the quake. "We just wanted to be warned that we were sitting on a bomb." The trial opened today, and the BBC notes that seven defendants face up to 15 years in jail.
